## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]*
**Define Pulse Width Modulation (PWM). With a neat labelled diagram, define the terms *duty cycle* and *period* of a PWM waveform.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** *Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) is a modulation technique in which the width of the rectangular pulses is varied in proportion to a modulating signal, while the period (frequency) of the pulses is kept constant.*

**Diagram (1 mark):** A neat rectangular waveform is drawn showing one complete cycle with:
- $T$ marked as the period (one full cycle).
- $T_{ON}$ marked as the high-time (pulse width).
- $T_{OFF}$ marked as the low-time.

**Formulas (1 mark):**

$$
D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T} \times 100\% \quad ; \quad f = \frac{1}{T} \text{ Hz}
$$

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]*
**List any four registers of the LPC2148 PWM module and state the function of each in one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

| # | Register | Function (1 line) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `PWMMR0` | Defines the PWM period (frequency) by determining when the Timer Counter is reset. |
| 2 | `PWMMR1` … `PWMMR6` | Define the duty cycle of PWM channels 1 through 6 respectively. |
| 3 | `PWMTCR` | Timer Control Register — used to **enable** the counter and **reset** the counter. |
| 4 | `PWMMCR` | Match Control Register — selects actions (interrupt, reset, stop) on MR-matches. |
| 5 | `PWMPCR` | PWM Control Register — enables individual PWM outputs (PWMENA1…PWMENA6) and selects single/double edge mode. |
| 6 | `PWMLER` | Latch Enable Register — when a bit is set, the corresponding `PWMMRx` update takes effect at the next MR0 reset. |
| 7 | `PWMPR` | Prescale Register — divides the PCLK input frequency to the Timer Counter. |

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Internal Choice A]**

> **Q.A(a)** Explain the internal PWM module of ARM LPC2148 with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of PWMMR0, PWMMR1, PWMMCR, PWMPCR, and PWMLER registers. **(7 marks)**

> **Q.A(b)** In LPC2148, the PCLK = 15 MHz and the Prescale Register (PR) is set to 0. Design a PWM waveform on channel PWM1 with a frequency of 1 kHz and a duty cycle of 40 %. Calculate PWMMR0 and PWMMR1. Also find the average voltage seen by a 3.3 V load. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

*(1 mark for introduction + 2 marks for block diagram description + 3 marks for register roles + 1 mark for conclusion)*

The LPC2148 PWM module is a 32-bit timer-based hardware block that generates up to **6 PWM outputs** (single-edge or double-edge) without continuous CPU intervention. It contains:

- A 32-bit **Timer Counter (PWMTC)** that increments on every PCLK (after prescaling).
- A **Prescale Counter (PWMPC)** and **Prescale Register (PWMPR)** that divide the incoming clock.
- Seven **Match Registers (PWMMR0 … PWMMR6)** — MR0 sets the period, MR1–MR6 set the duty cycle of each channel.
- A **Match Control Register (PWMMCR)** that decides what happens on a match (interrupt, reset TC, stop TC).
- A **PWM Control Register (PWMPCR)** that enables each PWM output pin and selects single/double-edge mode.
- A **Latch Enable Register (PWMLER)** that synchronises all new MR values to the next MR0 reset.
- A **Timer Control Register (PWMTCR)** that contains Counter Enable and Counter Reset bits.

**Register roles (3 marks):**

1. **PWMMR0** = Period register. When TC matches MR0, the counter resets to 0, beginning a new period. Value of MR0 sets the PWM frequency.
2. **PWMMR1** = Duty-cycle register for channel 1. When TC matches MR1, the PWM1 output pin goes LOW, ending the ON-time.
3. **PWMMCR** = Configures the match behaviour. Bit-1 (`PWMMCR[1]`) is set to **1** so that TC is **reset on MR0 match** — without this, no periodic waveform is possible.
4. **PWMPCR** = Output control register. Bit-9 (`PWMENA1`) is set to **1** to enable PWM1 output on pin P0.0. Bits 1-6 enable channels 1-6.
5. **PWMLER** = Latch Enable. After writing to MR0 and MR1, writing `0x03` to PWMLER latches both values so that the changes take effect on the next MR0 reset boundary.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Thus the LPC2148 PWM is a fully hardware-driven peripheral: once initialised, it produces the waveform on its output pin automatically while the CPU is free to do other tasks.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

Given: $f_{clk} = 15$ MHz $= 15 \times 10^{6}$ Hz, $PR = 0$, $f_{PWM} = 1$ kHz $= 1 \times 10^{3}$ Hz, $D = 40$ %, $V_{CC} = 3.3$ V.

**Step 1 — PWMMR0 calculation (3 marks):**

$$
PWMMR0 = \frac{f_{clk}}{f_{PWM} \times (PR+1)} - 1
$$

$$
PWMMR0 = \frac{15 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 10^{3} \times 1} - 1 = 15000 - 1 = 14999
$$

**Step 2 — PWMMR1 calculation (2 marks):**

$$
PWMMR1 = \frac{D}{100} \times (PWMMR0 + 1) = 0.40 \times (14999 + 1) = 0.40 \times 15000 = 6000
$$

**Step 3 — Average voltage (2 marks):**

$$
V_{avg} = D \times V_{CC} = 0.40 \times 3.3 = 1.32 \text{ V}
$$

**Final Answers:** $PWMMR0 = 14999$, $PWMMR1 = 6000$, $V_{avg} = 1.32$ V.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Internal Choice B]**

> **Q.B(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **single-edge** and **double-edge** PWM modes of the LPC2148. Which mode is preferred for motor control and why? **(7 marks)**

> **Q.B(b)** An LPC2148 has a PCLK of 30 MHz. The PWMMR0 is loaded with 29999 and PR = 0. The PWMMR3 register is loaded with 12000. Compute (i) the PWM output frequency, (ii) the duty cycle on channel 3, (iii) the ON-time in microseconds, and (iv) the average voltage for a 3.3 V output. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

*(2 marks for diagrams + 2 marks for explanation of single-edge + 2 marks for explanation of double-edge + 1 mark for application choice)*

**Single-Edge PWM Mode:** The PWM output is **set HIGH** at the beginning of the period (TC = 0) and **set LOW** at the TC = MRx match. The rising edge is aligned (single edge) across all channels. This is the *default* mode and is the easiest to configure. Diagram: a rectangular pulse with rising edge at $t = 0$ and falling edge at $t = T_{ON}$.

**Double-Edge PWM Mode:** Both the rising and falling edges of the pulse are controlled by MRx values. The TC is reset on **two matches**: one sets the rising edge, the other sets the falling edge. This allows more flexible waveform generation including centre-aligned PWM. Diagram: the pulse is positioned *within* the period and can be shifted left or right.

**Application choice (1 mark):** For DC motor control and most power electronics, **single-edge PWM** is preferred because (i) it is simpler to program, (ii) it has lower switching losses in some topologies, and (iii) it matches the standard H-bridge driver IC inputs (L293D, BTS7960). Double-edge mode is preferred for **3-phase AC motor drives (SPWM)** and **digital power factor correction** where centre-aligned switching reduces harmonic distortion.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

Given: $f_{clk} = 30$ MHz $= 30 \times 10^{6}$ Hz, $PWMMR0 = 29999$, $PWMMR3 = 12000$, $PR = 0$, $V_{CC} = 3.3$ V.

**(i) PWM output frequency (2 marks):**

$$
f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{clk}}{(PWMMR0 + 1) \times (PR+1)} = \frac{30 \times 10^{6}}{30000 \times 1} = 1000 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(ii) Duty cycle on channel 3 (2 marks):**

$$
D_3 = \frac{PWMMR3}{PWMMR0 + 1} \times 100 = \frac{12000}{30000} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

**(iii) ON-time in microseconds (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_{ON} = \frac{PWMMR3}{f_{clk}/(PR+1)} = \frac{12000}{30 \times 10^{6}} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 400 \text{ }\mu s
$$

**(iv) Average voltage (1.5 marks):**

$$
V_{avg} = D \times V_{CC} = 0.40 \times 3.3 = 1.32 \text{ V}
$$

**Final Answers:** $f_{PWM} = 1$ kHz, $D_3 = 40\%$, $T_{ON} = 400\,\mu s$, $V_{avg} = 1.32$ V.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in PWM questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "- 1" in PWMMR0 formula.** The TC counts values from 0 *up to and including* PWMMR0, so the total count is `PWMMR0 + 1`, not `PWMMR0`. Writing PWMMR0 = 15000 instead of 14999 for a 1 kHz / 15 MHz case is a **1-mark deduction** in most valuations.
> 2. **Not setting PWMLER after writing to PWMMRx.** In code-based questions, examiners specifically look for the line `PWMLER = 0x02;` (or equivalent) after `PWMMR1 = …;`. Missing this line → **1 to 2 marks lost** on the programming sub-part.
> 3. **Forgetting to enable the PWM output pin** via PINSEL. PWM1 appears on P0.0 only if `PINSEL0 = 0x02` (function 01 on bits 0-1 of PINSEL0). Without this, the pin stays as a normal GPIO and the CRO will show nothing.
> 4. **Confusing PWMTCR values.** Writing `PWMTCR = 0x01` (reset only) and never writing `0x09` (reset + enable) means the counter is *never started*. A classic error.
> 5. **Mixing up MR0 and MR1 roles.** PWMMR0 = period, PWMMR1 = duty cycle. Swapping them produces a very high frequency (in the MHz range) which appears as a flat DC line on a 20 MHz CRO. Examiners will mark this as a *conceptual error*.
> 6. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** Always write `Hz`, `seconds`, `Volts`, `%` explicitly. A bare number "15000" without context loses half a mark in strict valuation.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — PWM on LPC2148**

- **PWM = Pulse Width Modulation.** Frequency is constant, *width* of the ON-pulse changes.
- **Duty cycle $D = T_{ON} / T \times 100\%$**, bounded between **0 %** and **100 %**.
- **LPC2148 PWM block** has **7 match registers**: MR0 for period, MR1–MR6 for six channels.
- **PWMMR0 formula:** $PWMMR0 = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{f_{PWM} \times (PR+1)} - 1$ — remember the **-1** term.
- **PWMMRx formula (single-edge):** $PWMMRx = \dfrac{D}{100} \times (PWMMR0 + 1)$.
- **PWM frequency formula:** $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{(PWMMR0+1)(PR+1)}$.
- **Average voltage:** $V_{avg} = D \times V_{CC}$.
- **Five mandatory registers** in any PWM code: `PWMPR`, `PWMMR0`, `PWMMRx`, `PWMMCR`, `PWMPCR`, `PWMLER`, `PWMTCR`.
- **PWMMCR** must have bit-1 = 1 to *reset TC on MR0 match*. Without this, the waveform is **not periodic**.
- **PWMLER** must be written after every change to MR registers; bits 0–6 correspond to MR0–MR6.
- **PWMPCR** — set `PWMENA1` to `PWMENA6` (bits 1–6) to enable each channel; set `PWMSELn` (bits 9–14) to 0 for **single-edge** mode, 1 for **double-edge** mode.
- **PWMTCR** — write `0x09` = (bit-0 = 1 *Counter Enable* + bit-3 = 1 *Counter Reset*). Writing `0x01` alone resets but does not start; writing `0x09` resets *and* starts the counter.
- **Pin selection:** PWM1 → P0.0, PWM2 → P0.7, PWM3 → P0.1, PWM4 → P0.8, PWM5 → P0.21, PWM6 → P0.9. Configure `PINSEL0` and `PINSEL1` accordingly.
- **PCLK** for KTU lab is typically **15 MHz, 30 MHz, or 60 MHz** — read the `VPBDIV` register to confirm before calculation.
- **Standard lab values:** $PWMMR0 = 14999$ (for 1 kHz at 15 MHz PCLK, PR=0); duty cycles observed at 20 %, 40 %, 50 %, 60 %, 80 %.
- **Lab observation check:** Decrease PWMMR1 → LED dims, motor slows. Increase PWMMR1 → LED brightens, motor speeds up. This is the *visual proof* of duty cycle control.
- **Applications to remember:** DC motor speed control, LED dimming, servo motor positioning, SMPS, audio tone generation, heater control.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Writing to PWMMRx but forgetting to set the corresponding bit in PWMLER — the value will NOT appear on the pin until PWMLER is updated.
- **Examiner's keyword triggers** (use these in answers for full marks): "Match-and-toggle principle", "hardware-driven peripheral", "single-edge vs double-edge", "latch enable register", "prescale register", "MR0 reset mechanism".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demonstrate the use of an external interrupt to toggle an LED On/Off
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Demonstrate the use of an external interrupt to toggle an LED On/Off

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **External Interrupt (Definition as per KTU 2024 PCCPL408 Syllabus):**
> An *external interrupt* is a hardware-triggered asynchronous signal generated by an external peripheral or physical event (e.g., a push-button press, sensor pulse, or logic-level transition) that causes the microcontroller to suspend its current execution flow, save its context, and jump to a predefined **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. For the ATmega328P (Arduino Uno), the relevant external interrupt pins are **Digital Pin 2 (INT0)** and **Digital Pin 3 (INT1)**.

In the AVR architecture, external interrupts are managed by the **External Interrupt Control Register (EICRA)** for trigger selection and the **External Interrupt Mask Register (EIMSK)** for enabling/disabling, along with the **External Interrupt Flag Register (EIFR)** which holds the interrupt request flags.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doorbell vs. Polling"

Imagine you are deeply focused on reading a textbook (the main `loop()` function running):

- **Polling Method (Bad Analogy):** Every 5 seconds you stop reading, get up, walk to the door, and check if anyone is outside. Most of the time no one is there — you wasted time and energy.
- **Interrupt Method (Smart Analogy):** You install a doorbell. You keep reading happily. The moment a visitor presses the doorbell (external event), the microcontroller automatically pauses your reading, handles the visitor (executes the ISR), and then resumes reading exactly where it left off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Interrupts allow **event-driven programming**, which is more power-efficient and responsive than continuous `digitalRead()` polling. This is a fundamental concept tested heavily in KTU lab viva-voce examinations.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Debounce Time (Tₐ):** Typically **5 ms – 50 ms** for mechanical push buttons.
- **Trigger Modes:** `LOW`, `CHANGE`, `RISING`, `FALLING` (Arduino framework abstraction of AVR EICRA bits).
- **ISR Overhead:** AVR context save/restore takes approximately **8–12 clock cycles**.
- **Standard LED Current Limiting Resistor:** **R = (V_supply − V_f) / I_LED**; for a typical red LED (V_f ≈ 2V, I = 15 mA) on a 5V Arduino, **R ≈ 220 Ω**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How an External Interrupt Works

The interrupt mechanism follows a strict hardware-defined sequence:

- **Step 1 — Edge/Level Detection:** The I/O pin (D2 or D3 on Uno) continuously monitors the electrical signal. The trigger condition is configured via `EICRA` bits `ISC01`, `ISC00` (for INT0) and `ISC11`, `ISC10` (for INT1).
- **Step 2 — Flag Set in EIFR:** When the trigger condition is met, the corresponding bit (`INTF0` or `INTF1`) in the **External Interrupt Flag Register (EIFR)** is set to logic **1** by hardware.
- **Step 3 — Mask Check in EIMSK:** The **External Interrupt Mask Register (EIMSK)** must have the corresponding bit (`INT0` or `INT1`) set to **1**, otherwise the flag is ignored. This allows software to disable interrupts dynamically.
- **Step 4 — Global Interrupt Enable (SREG I-bit):** The **I-bit in the Status Register (SREG)** must be set using the `sei()` instruction (or `interrupts()` in Arduino). Without this master switch, no interrupt (masked or not) is serviced.
- **Step 5 — Vector Jump:** The CPU completes the current instruction, pushes the **Program Counter (PC)** onto the stack, and jumps to the interrupt vector address stored in the **Interrupt Vector Table** (e.g., `0x0002` for INT0 on ATmega328P).
- **Step 6 — ISR Execution:** The **Interrupt Service Routine** executes. It is declared with the `ISR()` macro in AVR-GCC or attached via `attachInterrupt()` in Arduino.
- **Step 7 — Return via `RETI`:** The `RETI` instruction restores the PC from the stack, automatically re-enables global interrupts (sets I-bit), and resumes the main program exactly at the instruction that was interrupted.

### 2.2 The `Why` Behind Each Step

- **Why masking?** To allow critical sections of code to run uninterrupted (atomic operations).
- **Why save PC?** To resume the main program seamlessly — the CPU has no idea it was "interrupted."
- **Why a special `RETI` instead of `RET`?** Because `RETI` atomically re-enables interrupts; using `RET` would leave the system in a state where new interrupts are ignored.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Register | Formula / Value | Function / Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| LED Current (I_LED) | $\displaystyle I_{LED} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{R}$ | Calculate series resistor value | Amperes (A) |
| Series Resistor (R) | $\displaystyle R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_{LED}}$ | Protect LED from overcurrent | Ohms (Ω) |
| Debounce Delay (T_d) | $\displaystyle T_d \approx 5 \text{ to } 50$ | Mechanical button bounce suppression | Milliseconds (ms) |
| INT0 Vector Address | $0x0002$ | ATmega328P vector for External Interrupt 0 | Hex address |
| INT1 Vector Address | $0x0004$ | ATmega328P vector for External Interrupt 1 | Hex address |
| EICRA Trigger Bits | `ISC01:ISC00` | `00`=LOW, `01`=CHANGE, `10`=FALLING, `11`=RISING | Bit pattern |
| EIMSK Enable | `INT1` / `INT0` | Bit set to 1 to enable respective interrupt | Bit |
| EIFR Flag | `INTF1` / `INTF0` | Auto-cleared when ISR is entered (or write 1 to clear) | Bit |
| SREG I-bit | Bit 7 of SREG | Global interrupt enable master switch | Bit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** A common viva question is *"What happens if the I-bit in SREG is cleared?"* — The answer is that **NO interrupt (including external, timer, or UART) will be serviced**, even if its individual mask bit is set. This is called the "Global Interrupt Mask."

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

External interrupts are the backbone of **Real-Time Embedded Systems**: airbag deployment sensors in automobiles, IoT motion detectors, industrial emergency-stop buttons, and battery-powered wake-up circuits where the MCU must "sleep" (using `LowPower.sleep()`) and only wake on an external event — saving enormous amounts of power.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Series Resistor Value for the LED

This is the only analytical derivation for this lab experiment, and it is critical for the **circuit design part of the KTU viva**.

**Given:**
- Supply voltage $V_{CC} = 5.0\ \text{V}$ (Arduino Uno logic level)
- Red LED forward voltage drop $V_f = 2.0\ \text{V}$ (typical)
- Desired safe LED current $I_{LED} = 15\ \text{mA} = 0.015\ \text{A}$

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CC} &= V_R + V_f \\
V_R &= V_{CC} - V_f \\
V_R &= 5.0\ \text{V} - 2.0\ \text{V} \\
V_R &= 3.0\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law to compute the series resistance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{V_R}{I_{LED}} \\
R &= \frac{3.0\ \text{V}}{0.015\ \text{A}} \\
R &= 200\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Choose the nearest standard E12 resistor value:**

The closest standard resistor from the **E12 series** is **220 Ω** (the next available value above 200 Ω to keep current slightly lower and safe).

**Step 4 — Verify the actual LED current with R = 220 Ω:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{actual} &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{R} \\
I_{actual} &= \frac{3.0\ \text{V}}{220\ \Omega} \\
I_{actual} &\approx 13.6\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is well within the safe operating range of a standard 5 mm red LED (max ~20 mA).

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A **220 Ω resistor** is the universally accepted standard value for indicator LEDs on a 5V Arduino. Use **330 Ω** for a slightly dimmer, longer-life LED.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Trigger Modes vs. Use Cases

| Trigger Mode | `EICRA` Bits (ISCx1:ISCx0) | Behavior | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| `LOW` | `00` | Interrupts continuously while pin is LOW | Emergency stop, fault lines |
| `CHANGE` | `01` | Interrupts on any edge (RISING + FALLING) | Toggle switches, quadrature encoders |
| `FALLING` | `10` | Interrupts on HIGH → LOW transition | Button press (active-low wiring) |
| `RISING` | `11` | Interrupts on LOW → HIGH transition | Hall-effect sensors, PIR motion sensors |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Algorithm: Arduino Implementation

The complete logical flow for the program:

- **Step 1:** Initialize Serial communication at 9600 baud (optional, for debugging).
- **Step 2:** Configure LED pin (e.g., Pin 13) as `OUTPUT`.
- **Step 3:** Configure interrupt pin (e.g., Pin 2) as `INPUT_PULLUP` to avoid floating pin.
- **Step 4:** Call `attachInterrupt(digitalPinToInterrupt(2), toggleLED, FALLING)` to bind ISR.
- **Step 5:** Inside the ISR, invert the global LED state variable and write to the pin.
- **Step 6:** The empty `loop()` function can do anything else (or nothing) — the interrupt handles the toggling asynchronously.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hardware Connection Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Arduino Uno ATmega328P] --> B[Digital Pin 2 INT0]
    A --> C[Digital Pin 13 LED_BUILTIN]
    A --> D[5V Supply Rail]
    A --> E[GND Common Ground]
    F[Push Button Switch] --> B
    F --> E
    G[External 10k Pull-down Resistor] --> B
    G --> E
    H[LED Component] --> C
    I[Current Limiting Resistor 220 ohm] --> H
    I --> C
    J[LED Cathode to GND] --> E
    D --> F
    style A fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#99ccff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#ff9966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The push button is wired between **5V** and **Pin 2**. A **10 kΩ pull-down resistor** keeps the pin at a clean `LOW` (0V) when the button is open. The LED is driven from **Pin 13** through a **220 Ω resistor** to **GND**.

### 4.2 Software Execution Flow (Mermaid Sequence Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Power On Reset] --> P1[setup runs: pinMode, attachInterrupt]
    P1 --> P2[Global interrupt flag sei enabled]
    P2 --> P3[loop function starts executing]
    P3 --> P4{Button Press Detected on Pin 2?}
    P4 -->|No| P3
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Hardware sets INTF0 flag in EIFR]
    P5 --> P6[CPU finishes current instruction]
    P6 --> P7[Push PC onto Stack]
    P7 --> P8[Jump to INT0 Vector 0x0002]
    P8 --> P9[Execute ISR toggleLED]
    P9 --> P10[Read current LED state]
    P10 --> P11{Invert state}
    P11 -->|LOW| P12[Set Pin 13 HIGH LED ON]
    P11 -->|HIGH| P13[Set Pin 13 LOW LED OFF]
    P12 --> P14[RETI instruction]
    P13 --> P14
    P14 --> P15[Pop PC from Stack]
    P15 --> P16[Resume loop exactly where it stopped]
    P16 --> P3
    style P4 fill:#ffff99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P11 fill:#ff9966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P14 fill:#99ff99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Reference Arduino Code (Lab Implementation)

```cpp
/*
 * Experiment 2: External Interrupt to Toggle LED
 * Platform: Arduino Uno (ATmega328P)
 * Course: PCCPL408 - KTU 2024 Scheme
 */

const int LED_PIN    = 13;     // On-board LED
const int INT_PIN    = 2;      // INT0 hardware pin
volatile bool ledState = LOW;  // 'volatile' because modified in ISR

void setup() {
    pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(INT_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP);   // Internal pull-up; press to GND
    attachInterrupt(digitalPinToInterrupt(INT_PIN), toggleLED, FALLING);
    Serial.begin(9600);
    Serial.println("System Ready — Press button to toggle LED");
}

void loop() {
    // Main program can do other work or simply idle
    // LED toggling happens entirely inside the ISR
}

void toggleLED() {
    ledState = !ledState;              // Invert the current state
    digitalWrite(LED_PIN, ledState);   // Apply new state to pin
    Serial.print("LED is now: ");
    Serial.println(ledState ? "ON" : "OFF");
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `volatile` keyword** is **mandatory** in KTU lab exams. It tells the compiler that the variable can change at any time "outside the normal flow" (i.e., inside the ISR), preventing the compiler from optimizing it away or caching it in a CPU register.

### 4.4 Pin Function Mapping Table

| Arduino Pin | ATmega328P Name | Special Function | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Digital 2 | PD2 (Port D, bit 2) | **INT0** | External Interrupt 0 input |
| Digital 3 | PD3 (Port D, bit 3) | **INT1** | External Interrupt 1 input |
| Digital 13 | PB5 (Port B, bit 5) | SCK (SPI) | On-board LED output |
| 5V | — | Power | Button pull-up rail |
| GND | — | Common ground | Button & LED return path |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]**
**Define an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). Why must the `volatile` keyword be used for variables shared between an ISR and the main `loop()` in Arduino?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** is a special function that is automatically executed by the CPU in response to a hardware or software interrupt event. In Arduino, it is bound to a trigger condition using `attachInterrupt()`.

The **`volatile`** keyword must be used for any **global variable that is modified inside an ISR and read in the main program** because:

- **(1 Mark)** The compiler may otherwise cache the variable in a CPU register for optimization.
- **(1 Mark)** Since the ISR can change the variable at any unpredictable time, the main code would read a stale value.
- **(1 Mark)** `volatile` forces the compiler to always re-read the variable from RAM, ensuring correct data visibility between the ISR and the main program.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**List the four trigger modes available for `attachInterrupt()` in Arduino. Which mode is most suitable for detecting a single button press on a normally-open push button connected between the pin and GND?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four trigger modes are:

- **(1 Mark)** `LOW` — Interrupts while the pin is held LOW.
- **(1 Mark)** `CHANGE` — Interrupts on every transition (RISING and FALLING).
- **(1 Mark)** `RISING` — Interrupts on LOW-to-HIGH transition.
- **(1 Mark)** `FALLING` — Interrupts on HIGH-to-LOW transition.

For a normally-open button wired between the pin (with internal pull-up) and GND, the **most suitable mode is `FALLING`**, because the pin transitions from HIGH (idle) to LOW (pressed) exactly once per press, giving a clean single trigger per button event.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice Format

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete working of an external interrupt on the ATmega328P microcontroller, with reference to the registers **EICRA**, **EIMSK**, **EIFR**, and **SREG**. Include a clear block diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Arduino program to toggle an LED connected to Pin 13 every time a push button (connected to Pin 2 with a 10 kΩ pull-down) is pressed. Explain why `digitalPinToInterrupt(2)` is preferred over simply passing `2`.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — External Interrupt Working:**

The external interrupt mechanism on ATmega328P involves four key registers:

- **(2 Marks)** **EICRA (External Interrupt Control Register A):** Configures the **trigger condition** via bits `ISC01:ISC00` (for INT0) and `ISC11:ISC10` (for INT1). The four combinations are: `00`=LOW level, `01`=any CHANGE, `10`=FALLING edge, `11`=RISING edge.

- **(1 Mark)** **EIMSK (External Interrupt Mask Register):** Bits `INT1` and `INT0` act as **individual enable switches**. Setting a bit to `1` allows the corresponding interrupt; clearing to `0` masks it (silently ignored).

- **(1 Mark)** **EIFR (External Interrupt Flag Register):** Bits `INTF1` and `INTF0` are **hardware-set** when the trigger event occurs. The flag is automatically cleared when the ISR begins. Writing a logical `1` to the flag bit also clears it (used for edge-case clearing).

- **(1 Mark)** **SREG (Status Register), I-bit (bit 7):** The **global master switch**. If cleared, NO interrupt is serviced, regardless of EIMSK. Set using `sei()` or Arduino's `interrupts()` function. Cleared using `cli()` or `noInterrupts()`.

- **(2 Marks)** **Execution Sequence:** When a valid trigger occurs → EIFR flag sets → EIMSK checks mask → SREG I-bit checked → If all enabled, the CPU finishes the current instruction, pushes PC onto the **stack**, and jumps to the **Interrupt Vector Table** address (`0x0002` for INT0, `0x0004` for INT1). The ISR runs. The `RETI` instruction pops PC back and re-enables global interrupts, resuming the main program.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[External Event on Pin 2] --> B[Trigger Detection in EICRA]
    B --> C[Set INTF0 in EIFR]
    C --> D{EIMSK INT0 bit 1?}
    D -->|No| E[Interrupt Ignored]
    D -->|Yes| F{SREG I-bit 1?}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes| G[Save PC to Stack]
    G --> H[Jump to ISR Vector 0x0002]
    H --> I[Execute ISR Code]
    I --> J[RETI: Restore PC, Set I-bit]
    J --> K[Resume Main Program]
    style E fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style K fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**(b) [7 Marks] — Arduino Program:**

```cpp
const int LED_PIN = 13;
const int INT_PIN = 2;
volatile bool ledState = LOW;

void setup() {
    pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(INT_PIN, INPUT);                // External 10k pull-down
    attachInterrupt(digitalPinToInterrupt(INT_PIN), toggleLED, FALLING);
    Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
    // Main loop remains free for other tasks
}

void toggleLED() {
    ledState = !ledState;
    digitalWrite(LED_PIN, ledState);
    Serial.println(ledState ? "ON" : "OFF");
}
```

**Why `digitalPinToInterrupt(2)` is preferred:**

- **(3 Marks)** `digitalPinToInterrupt(pin)` is a **portability macro** that maps the Arduino pin number to the correct **interrupt number** for the specific board's MCU. On Arduino Uno, pin 2 maps to interrupt 0 and pin 3 to interrupt 1. On Arduino Mega, pin 2 is interrupt 4. Using the macro makes the code **portable across boards** without modification, whereas hardcoding `0` would fail on different architectures.

---

**Q3(B). [Alternative 14-Mark Choice]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **polling** and **interrupt-driven** I/O. Under what circumstances is polling preferred over interrupts?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design and explain the complete hardware circuit for the experiment — including the pull-down resistor calculation, LED current-limiting resistor calculation, and the ATmega328P pin mapping. Provide the circuit schematic in block form.

**Model Solution (Brief Outline for 14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]:**
- **Polling** = CPU continuously checks a flag/flag in a tight loop. CPU is **busy 100%** of the time. Simple, deterministic, no ISR overhead.
- **Interrupt** = CPU executes other tasks; only responds when hardware signals. **Event-driven**, low power, no CPU wastage.
- **Polling preferred when:** (i) The event must be serviced at a precise, fixed sample rate (e.g., ADC sampling at exactly 1 kHz); (ii) The interrupt rate is so high that ISR overhead would starve the main loop; (iii) The code must be 100% deterministic without ISR latency jitter.

**(b) [7 Marks]:** Circuit design with **220 Ω** LED resistor (derived in Section 3) and **10 kΩ** pull-down (standard rule: 10 kΩ is 100× larger than typical 100 Ω source impedance, ensuring clean logic levels).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Dec 2023 / June 2024 patterns):**
>
> - **-2 Marks:** Forgetting the `volatile` keyword on the shared flag variable — this is the **#1 reason for marks deduction** in the viva.
> - **-2 Marks:** Using `pinMode(INT_PIN, INPUT)` without specifying `INPUT_PULLUP` or wiring an external pull-down — leaving the pin **floating** causes random phantom interrupts.
> - **-2 Marks:** Calling `Serial.print()` inside the ISR without realizing that `Serial` itself uses interrupts, which can cause **deadlocks or missed characters** during fast toggling.
> - **-1 Mark:** Writing `attachInterrupt(2, ...)` instead of `attachInterrupt(digitalPinToInterrupt(2), ...)` — fails portability rubric.
> - **-1 Mark:** Forgetting to state the **EIFR register** in the theory answer — examiners specifically look for all four registers (EICRA, EIMSK, EIFR, SREG).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **External interrupts** on Arduino Uno are available **only on Pins 2 and 3** (INT0 and INT1).
- The four **trigger modes** are `LOW`, `CHANGE`, `RISING`, `FALLING` — chosen via `attachInterrupt()`.
- The **`volatile` keyword** is **non-negotiable** for any global variable touched by an ISR.
- Four **key registers**: `EICRA` (trigger config), `EIMSK` (per-interrupt mask), `EIFR` (pending flags), `SREG` (global I-bit master switch).
- **ISR execution sequence**: Event → Flag set → Mask check → Global check → Save PC → Jump to vector → Execute ISR → `RETI` → Resume.
- **Vector addresses** for ATmega328P: INT0 = `0x0002`, INT1 = `0x0004`.
- **Hardware rule**: Always use a **pull-up or pull-down resistor** on the interrupt pin to prevent floating-state noise.
- **LED current-limiting resistor** for 5V Arduino: standard value is **220 Ω** (yields ~13.6 mA for a red LED).
- **Never use `delay()` inside an ISR** — it relies on timer interrupts and will block indefinitely.
- **Keep ISRs short**: only set flags or toggle pins; do heavy processing in the main `loop()`.
- **`attachInterrupt()` with `digitalPinToInterrupt()`** ensures code portability across Arduino boards (Uno, Mega, Due, etc.).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Display the Hex digits 0 to F on a 7-segment LED interface with an appropriate delay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Display the Hex digits 0 to F on a 7-segment LED interface with an appropriate delay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):** A **7-Segment LED Display** is an electronic display device consisting of seven Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs) arranged in the shape of the numeral **8**, plus an optional eighth segment (decimal point, **dp**). Each LED is referred to as a *segment* and is labeled **a, b, c, d, e, f, g**, allowing the display of hexadecimal digits **0 through F** by selectively illuminating specific segment combinations.

In the context of the **PCCPL408 – Microcontrollers and IoT Laboratory**, this experiment focuses on interfacing a single **7-segment common-cathode (or common-anode) LED display** to the **8051 microcontroller** (typically the AT89C51 / P89V51RD2) and writing an embedded program to **sequentially cycle through the hexadecimal digits 0 to F** with a software-generated delay between successive digits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment tests the following Course Outcomes:
> - **CO2:** Develop embedded C / Assembly programs for 8051 I/O interfacing.
> - **CO3:** Interface and program peripheral devices (LEDs, 7-segment, LCD, keypad) to the 8051.
> - **CO4:** Analyze timing, delays, and real-time execution of embedded firmware.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Number Story"

Imagine an **old digital alarm clock** in your grandparents' house. The glowing red digits are made up of *seven small bars* (segments) that light up in different combinations. To show the number **"3"**, the clock lights up the **top, middle, bottom, and the two right-side bars** while keeping the left side dark.

In our experiment, the **microcontroller is the "clock's brain"** — it decides *which* bars (segments) glow and *for how long*. We instruct the microcontroller to display **0, then 1, then 2 … up to F**, each for a short duration (say 1 second), so the user sees the digits counting up in hexadecimal — just like a digital scoreboard.

| Real-World Object | 7-Segment Counter Equivalent |
|-------------------|------------------------------|
| Digital wall clock | Hex digit display (0-F) |
| Lift floor indicator | Single 7-segment display |
| Microwave timer | Multi-digit 7-segment cluster |
| Petrol pump display | Bright multiplexed 7-segment |

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Parameters used in KTU 8051 Lab Experiments:**
> - **Microcontroller:** AT89C51 / P89V51RD2 (**8-bit**, 40-pin DIP)
> - **Crystal Frequency (f<sub>osc</sub>):** **11.0592 MHz** (standard for serial baud-rate generation)
> - **One Machine Cycle (T):** **12 / f<sub>osc</sub> = 12 / 11.0592 × 10⁶ ≈ 1.085 µs**
> - **Vcc (Logic High):** **+5 V DC**
> - **LED Forward Voltage (V<sub>f</sub>):** **1.8 V – 2.2 V** (standard red LED)
> - **LED Forward Current (I<sub>f</sub>):** **10 mA – 20 mA** (typical)
> - **Current Limiting Resistor:** **R = (Vcc − V<sub>f</sub>) / I<sub>f</sub> ≈ 220 Ω – 330 Ω**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of a 7-Segment Display

A 7-segment display contains **8 LEDs** (seven bar segments + one decimal point). These LEDs are internally wired in one of two configurations:

### (a) Common Cathode (CC) — Most Common in KTU Labs
- All **cathode (–)** terminals are tied to a **common GND pin (pin 3 or pin 8)**.
- To light a segment, drive the corresponding anode pin **HIGH (+5 V)** through a current-limiting resistor.

### (b) Common Anode (CA)
- All **anode (+)** terminals are tied to a **common Vcc pin**.
- To light a segment, drive the corresponding cathode pin **LOW (0 V)** through a current-limiting resistor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule of Thumb:** If the problem statement does *not* specify the type, **assume Common Cathode** — it is the most widely used configuration in 8051 trainer kits.

### 2.2 Segment Identification Convention

```
        ___a___
       |       |
      f|       |b
       |___g___|
       |       |
      e|       |c
       |___d___|   • dp
```

- Segments **a, b, c, d, e, f** form the outer "8".
- Segment **g** is the middle horizontal bar.
- Segment **dp** is the decimal point (typically not used for hex digits).

### 2.3 KTU Hex-Digit Encoding Lookup Table (Common Cathode)

| Hex Digit | Segments Lit (a b c d e f g) | Bit Pattern (gfedcba) | Hex Code (P1) |
|:---------:|:---------------------------:|:---------------------:|:-------------:|
| **0**     | a, b, c, d, e, f            | 0 1 1 1 1 1 1         | **0x3F**      |
| **1**     | b, c                        | 0 0 0 0 1 1 0         | **0x06**      |
| **2**     | a, b, d, e, g               | 0 1 0 1 1 0 1         | **0x5B**      |
| **3**     | a, b, c, d, g               | 0 1 0 0 1 1 1         | **0x4F**      |
| **4**     | b, c, f, g                  | 0 1 1 0 0 1 1         | **0x66**      |
| **5**     | a, c, d, f, g               | 0 1 1 0 1 1 1         | **0x6D**      |
| **6**     | a, c, d, e, f, g            | 0 1 1 1 1 1 1         | **0x7D**      |
| **7**     | a, b, c                     | 0 0 0 0 1 1 1         | **0x07**      |
| **8**     | a, b, c, d, e, f, g         | 0 1 1 1 1 1 1         | **0x7F**      |
| **9**     | a, b, c, d, f, g            | 0 1 1 0 1 1 1         | **0x6F**      |
| **A**     | a, b, c, e, f, g            | 0 1 1 1 0 1 1         | **0x77**      |
| **B**     | c, d, e, f, g               | 0 1 1 1 1 1 0         | **0x7C**      |
| **C**     | a, d, e, f                  | 0 0 1 1 1 0 1         | **0x39**      |
| **D**     | b, c, d, e, g               | 0 1 0 1 1 1 0         | **0x5E**      |
| **E**     | a, d, e, f, g               | 0 1 1 1 1 0 1         | **0x79**      |
| **F**     | a, e, f, g                  | 0 1 1 1 0 0 1         | **0x71**      |

> [!NOTE]
> For **Common Anode** displays, simply **invert every bit** (XOR with 0xFF) of the above table — e.g., 0x3F becomes **0xC0**, 0x06 becomes **0xF9**, and so on.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value (8051 @ 11.0592 MHz) |
|:-------:|----------|---------|------------------------------------|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Period (T) | $T = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | $T \approx 1.085~\mu s$ |
| 2 | Current Limiting Resistor | $R = \dfrac{V_{cc} - V_f}{I_f}$ | $R \approx 220~\Omega \text{ to } 330~\Omega$ |
| 3 | Power Dissipated per Segment | $P_{seg} = V_f \times I_f$ | $P_{seg} \approx 20~mW$ |
| 4 | Total Delay (Two Nested Loops) | $T_{delay} = N_1 \times N_2 \times k \times T$ | Depends on N₁, N₂ |
| 5 | Bit Position Value (P1.x) | $2^{x}$ where x ∈ {0..7} | P1.0 = 1, P1.7 = 128 |
| 6 | Inversion (CC ↔ CA) | $Code_{CA} = \overline{Code_{CC}} = 0xFF \oplus Code_{CC}$ | 0x3F ↔ 0xC0 |

> **Notation Note:** Absolute values have been written as `\vert x \vert` in formulas above to prevent markdown table breakage.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this experiment critical in Industry?**
> - 7-segment displays are still the **cheapest, brightest, and most power-efficient** numeric displays used in **weighing scales, fuel pumps, industrial counters, microwave ovens, and digital thermometers**.
> - In an **IoT edge node**, a microcontroller may use such a display for *localized status readouts* (e.g., temperature, RPM, error codes) **without needing a full LCD or cloud connectivity** — saving cost, power, and BOM complexity.
> - Understanding **lookup-table-driven segment encoding** is a foundational technique used in **character LCD font generation, dot-matrix displays, and OLED graphics** in modern embedded systems.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Current Limiting Resistor

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Compute the correct series resistor value for each segment LED connected to an 8051 port pin (Vcc = 5 V).

By **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around a single LED + resistor branch:

$$V_{cc} = V_R + V_f$$

The voltage drop across the resistor is therefore:

$$V_R = V_{cc} - V_f$$

By **Ohm's Law**, $V_R = I_f \times R$, so:

$$R = \frac{V_{cc} - V_f}{I_f}$$

### Numerical Evaluation (Red LED, Standard KTU Trainer Kit)

Substituting the standard textbook values:

$$R = \frac{5.0~V - 2.0~V}{10~mA} = \frac{3.0~V}{0.010~A} = 300~\Omega$$

**Step-by-step justification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & V_{cc} = 5.0~V \quad \text{(standard 8051 logic supply)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & V_f = 2.0~V \quad \text{(typical red LED forward voltage drop)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & I_f = 10~mA = 0.010~A \quad \text{(safe LED operating current)} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & V_R = V_{cc} - V_f = 5.0 - 2.0 = 3.0~V \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_f} = \dfrac{3.0}{0.010} = 300~\Omega \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Nearest standard E12 resistor value} = 330~\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Check:** $P_R = I_f^2 \times R = (0.010)^2 \times 300 = 0.03~W$. A standard **¼ watt (0.25 W) resistor** is more than sufficient — a ⅛ W resistor is also acceptable.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Software Delay Loop Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive a software delay that produces an approximate **500 ms** blanking time between successive hex digits, suitable for human-eye perception (typically 1 Hz to 5 Hz refresh).

The 8051 uses **12 clock pulses per machine cycle**. With a crystal of 11.0592 MHz:

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085~\mu s$$

### Inner DJNZ Loop Analysis

A single `DJNZ Rn, rel` instruction takes **2 machine cycles**.

For a single-level inner loop of **255 iterations** (R2 = FFh):

$$T_{inner} = 255 \times 2 \times T_{mc} = 510 \times 1.085~\mu s \approx 553.35~\mu s$$

### Outer Loop Analysis

For a two-level nested loop where the **outer loop runs N₁ times**:

$$T_{total} = N_1 \times T_{inner}$$

Solving for **N₁** to achieve a target delay of 500 ms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{target} &= 500~ms = 500{,}000~\mu s \\
N_1 \times 553.35~\mu s &= 500{,}000~\mu s \\
N_1 &= \frac{500{,}000}{553.35} \approx 903.5 \\
N_1 &\approx 903 \text{ iterations}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Refined Three-Level Delay (with overhead compensation)

In practice, loop setup, `MOV`, `DJNZ` of the outer loop, and `RET` add overhead. A common KTU-tested three-level delay uses values **(255, 255, 5)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &\approx 5 \times 255 \times (255 \times 2 \times T_{mc}) \\
&= 5 \times 255 \times 553.35~\mu s \\
&\approx 705{,}521~\mu s \approx 706~ms
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields roughly **0.7 seconds** — a perfect human-perceptible delay between hex digits.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Program Logic Flow

| Step | Action | Register / Variable | Duration |
|:----:|--------|---------------------|----------|
| 1 | Initialize counter i = 0 | R0 / index variable | 1 machine cycle |
| 2 | Lookup hex pattern for digit i | P1 = lookup[i] | 2 machine cycles |
| 3 | Output pattern to Port 1 (P1) | MOV P1, A | 1 machine cycle |
| 4 | Generate ~500 ms delay | delay(500) | ~460,000 cycles |
| 5 | Increment counter i | INC R0 | 1 machine cycle |
| 6 | Check if i < 16 | CJNE R0, #10H, loop | 2 machine cycles |
| 7 | If not, wrap to 0 (i = 0) | SJMP MAIN | 2 machine cycles |
| 8 | Repeat forever | — | Infinite |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** The 8051 **I/O ports cannot source/sink large currents**. The maximum current per pin is **~10 mA** for AT89C51. Driving all 7 segments + dp simultaneously (e.g., digit "8") draws up to **80 mA total** — still within the port's 80 mA absolute maximum, but **never exceed this** in practical designs.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Common Anode and Common Cathode 7-segment displays. Which one is used in the standard 8051 trainer kit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Common Cathode (CC) | Common Anode (CA) |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Common Terminal | All cathodes tied to **GND** | All anodes tied to **Vcc (+5V)** |
| To Light a Segment | Drive anode pin **HIGH (+5V)** | Drive cathode pin **LOW (0V)** |
| Current Direction | Current flows from MCU → LED → GND | Current flows from Vcc → LED → MCU |
| KTU Trainer Kit | ✅ **Used (LTS-547, LTS-367)** | ❌ Less common |

> **Key Point (1 mark):** Standard KTU 8051 trainer kits use **Common Cathode** displays.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
**Why is a current limiting resistor required for each segment of a 7-segment LED display? Calculate its value for Vcc = 5 V, V<sub>f</sub> = 2 V, and I<sub>f</sub> = 10 mA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A current limiting resistor is required to **protect the LED from excessive current** that would otherwise burn it out, and to **limit the sourcing/sinking current** drawn from the microcontroller I/O pin beyond its safe limit (10 mA for AT89C51).

**Calculation:**

$$R = \frac{V_{cc} - V_f}{I_f} = \frac{5 - 2}{0.010} = 300~\Omega$$

The nearest standard resistor value is **330 Ω** (1 mark for formula + 1 mark for final answer).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, internally split as **7 + 7 marks** for sub-parts (a) and (b). Students must answer **either full Question A OR full Question B**.

### **Question A (14 Marks): Standard Implementation**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 – Embedded Interfacing]**

**(a)** Draw the interfacing circuit diagram to connect a **Common Cathode 7-segment display** to the **Port P1 of an 8051 microcontroller**. Explain the role of current limiting resistors and the connection of the common pin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an **8051 Embedded C program** to display the hexadecimal digits **0 to F** on the 7-segment display with a delay of **1 second** between each digit. The lookup table should be stored in **code memory** using the `code` keyword. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Interfacing Circuit Description (7 marks):**

1. **Port P1 (P1.0 – P1.7)** of the 8051 is configured as **OUTPUT** by writing 0x00 to it during initialization.
2. Each segment pin (a–g, dp) of the 7-segment display is connected to **P1.0 through P1.7** respectively.
3. A **220 Ω – 330 Ω current limiting resistor** is connected in **series** between each port pin and the corresponding segment pin to limit the LED current to **~10 mA**.
4. The **Common Cathode (CC) pin (pin 3 or pin 8)** of the display is connected to **GND (0V)**.
5. The 8051 is powered by **+5 V** on pins 40 (Vcc) and 40 with **0.1 µF decoupling capacitors** near the power pins.
6. A **11.0592 MHz crystal** is connected between pins 18 (XTAL1) and 19 (XTAL2) with two **33 pF capacitors** to ground.
7. **Pin 9 (RESET)** is pulled HIGH momentarily via a 10 µF capacitor + 10 kΩ pull-down to enable power-on reset.

**ASCII Block Diagram:**

```
  +5V ──[ 330Ω ]── a ─┐
  +5V ──[ 330Ω ]── b ─┤
  P1.0  P1.1 ...     ─┤── [ 7-Segment Display ]
  ...                 ─┤   (Common Cathode)
  +5V ──[ 330Ω ]── dp ─┘            │
                                     │
                                    GND
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Embedded C Program — 8051 (Keil C51 syntax):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>                 // SFR declarations for 8051

// Hex pattern lookup table stored in CODE memory (Flash)
unsigned char code hex_lookup[16] = {
    0x3F,   // 0 → segments a, b, c, d, e, f
    0x06,   // 1 → segments b, c
    0x5B,   // 2 → segments a, b, d, e, g
    0x4F,   // 3 → segments a, b, c, d, g
    0x66,   // 4 → segments b, c, f, g
    0x6D,   // 5 → segments a, c, d, f, g
    0x7D,   // 6 → segments a, c, d, e, f, g
    0x07,   // 7 → segments a, b, c
    0x7F,   // 8 → all segments ON
    0x6F,   // 9 → segments a, b, c, d, f, g
    0x77,   // A → segments a, b, c, e, f, g
    0x7C,   // b → segments c, d, e, f, g
    0x39,   // C → segments a, d, e, f
    0x5E,   // d → segments b, c, d, e, g
    0x79,   // E → segments a, d, e, f, g
    0x71    // F → segments a, e, f, g
};

// Software delay function — generates ~1 second delay @ 11.0592 MHz
void delay_one_sec(void) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < 900; i++)         // Outer loop
        for(j = 0; j < 1275; j++);   // Inner loop → ≈1.005 s
}

void main(void) {
    unsigned char digit;
    P1 = 0x00;                       // Configure Port 1 as OUTPUT
    while(1) {                       // Infinite loop
        for(digit = 0; digit < 16; digit++) {
            P1 = hex_lookup[digit];  // Display current hex digit
            delay_one_sec();         // 1-second human-perceptible delay
        }
    }
}
```

**Program Explanation (mark distribution):**

- Header file & lookup table definition — **2 marks**
- `delay_one_sec()` function logic — **2 marks**
- `main()` function with infinite loop & counter — **2 marks**
- Correctness, indentation, and comments — **1 mark**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Implementation**

**(a)** Design the **complete hardware schematic** (with port connections, resistor values, and power supply) to interface a **Common Anode 7-segment display** to **Port P0 of 8051**, using **74LS245 buffer** for current amplification. Justify the need for the buffer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **equivalent 8051 Assembly Language program** using the `MOVC` instruction and the `DPTR` lookup table technique to display the same hex digits 0 to F. Show the complete delay subroutine with cycle count analysis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Hardware Design (7 marks):**

| Signal Line | Connection | Reason |
|-------------|------------|--------|
| P0.0 – P0.7 | 74LS245 inputs (A0 – A7) | P0 has no internal pull-ups; needs buffer |
| 74LS245 outputs (B0 – B7) | 330 Ω resistors → segments a–g, dp | Current amplification + LED protection |
| Common Anode pin | +5 V via 100 Ω resistor | Current limit for entire display |
| 74LS245 DIR pin | GND (Direction = A → B) | Ensure unidirectional data flow |
| 74LS245 OE̅ pin | GND (Enable active) | Enable the buffer |

**Justification for 74LS245 Buffer (2 marks):**
- **P0 port of 8051** is an **open-drain** port (no internal pull-up resistors).
- Without a buffer, segments may not light reliably; external pull-ups (10 kΩ) waste current and reduce brightness.
- **74LS245** provides strong totem-pole outputs capable of sinking up to **24 mA per pin** — sufficient to drive multiple LEDs directly.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**8051 Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H              ; Reset vector
        LJMP    MAIN               ; Jump to main program

;----------------------------------------------------------------
; DELAY SUBROUTINE — Generates ~1 second delay @ 11.0592 MHz
; Inner loop: 255 × 2 cycles = 510 cycles = ~553 µs
; Outer loop: 255 iterations → 255 × 553 µs ≈ 141 ms
; 7 outer passes  → ~987 ms ≈ 1 second
;----------------------------------------------------------------
DELAY:  MOV     R1, #07H          ; Outer counter = 7
LOOP3:  MOV     R2, #0FFH         ; Middle counter = 255
LOOP2:  MOV     R3, #0FFH         ; Inner counter = 255
LOOP1:  DJNZ    R3, LOOP1         ; 2 cycles × 255 = 510 cycles
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP2         ; Decrement middle
        DJNZ    R1, LOOP3         ; Decrement outer
        RET                      ; Return to caller

;----------------------------------------------------------------
; HEX LOOKUP TABLE — Common Anode codes (inverted bits)
;----------------------------------------------------------------
        ORG     0300H
TABLE:  DB      0C0H, 0F9H, 0A4H, 0B0H, 99H, 92H, 82H, 0F8H
        DB      80H,  90H, 88H,  83H, 0C6H, 0A1H, 86H, 8EH

;----------------------------------------------------------------
; MAIN PROGRAM — Display 0 to F cyclically
;----------------------------------------------------------------
MAIN:   MOV     P0, #0FFH         ; Configure P0 as output (write 1s)
        MOV     DPTR, #TABLE      ; Load DPTR with table base address
        MOV     R0, #00H          ; Counter R0 = 0 (hex digit index)

DISPLAY:MOV     A, R0             ; A = current digit index
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR        ; A = hex pattern from code memory
        MOV     P0, A             ; Output pattern to 7-segment display
        ACALL   DELAY             ; 1-second delay
        INC     R0                ; Increment digit counter
        CJNE    R0, #10H, DISPLAY ; Loop until R0 = 16, then restart
        SJMP    MAIN              ; Wrap around and start again
        END
```

**Cycle Count Analysis for DELAY subroutine:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{LOOP1} &= 255 \times 2 = 510 \text{ cycles} \\
T_{LOOP2} &= (510 + 1 + 2) \times 255 = 513 \times 255 = 130{,}815 \text{ cycles} \\
T_{LOOP3} &= (130{,}815 + 1 + 2) \times 7 = 130{,}818 \times 7 = 915{,}726 \text{ cycles} \\
T_{DELAY} &= 915{,}726 \times 1.085~\mu s \approx 993.5~ms \approx 1 \text{ second}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mistake (1-2 marks lost):**
> - ❌ **Forgetting to invert the bit pattern** for Common Anode displays. CC hex "0" = 0x3F, but CA hex "0" = **0xC0** (inverted).
> - ❌ **Not clearing P0 first.** P0 is open-drain; if not initialized to 0xFF, random segments may glow.
> - ❌ **Wrong lookup-table size** — must contain exactly **16 entries** for hex digits 0–F, not just 0–9.
> - ❌ **Counter check error** — should be `CJNE R0, #10H, DISPLAY` (hex 10 = decimal 16), NOT `#16`.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Quick Recall — Pin This on Your Wall!**

- ✅ **7-segment display = 7 LEDs + 1 dp**, arranged in figure-of-8.
- ✅ **Common Cathode** → segments glow on **HIGH**; **Common Anode** → segments glow on **LOW**.
- ✅ Standard 8051 KTU lab uses **Common Cathode** (verify with your trainer kit).
- ✅ **Resistor formula:** $R = \dfrac{V_{cc} - V_f}{I_f}$; standard value = **330 Ω**.
- ✅ **Hex digit "0" (CC)** = **0x3F**; **Hex digit "0" (CA)** = **0xC0**.
- ✅ **One machine cycle** of 8051 = **12 / f<sub>osc</sub>** = **1.085 µs** (for 11.0592 MHz).
- ✅ `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` instruction is used to **read lookup tables stored in code memory**.
- ✅ The keyword **`code`** in Keil C51 places the array in **ROM/Flash** (saves RAM).
- ✅ Display digits sequentially using a `for` loop from **0 to 15** (i.e., `< 16`).
- ✅ Human-perceptible delay range = **200 ms to 1 second** (use ~500 ms to 1 s for clarity).
- ✅ Port P0 of 8051 is **open-drain** — always needs external pull-ups or a buffer (74LS245).
- ✅ **P1, P2, P3 are quasi-bidirectional** with internal pull-ups — safe to drive LEDs directly.
- ✅ All 16 hex digits (0–F) require **unique 7-segment codes**; use the **lookup table** approach, never recompute in real-time.
- ✅ In **Assembly**, set DPTR once outside the loop for performance optimization.
- ✅ Always include the `while(1)` infinite loop in `main()` — 8051 has no OS to return to.
- ✅ **Always ground the EA̅ pin** while using internal ROM (for AT89C51 with internal program memory).

> **End of Notes — Best of luck for your KTU 2024 Lab Exam!**
