# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of a 7-Segment Display

A 7-segment display contains **8 LEDs** (seven bar segments + one decimal point). These LEDs are internally wired in one of two configurations:

### (a) Common Cathode (CC) — Most Common in KTU Labs
- All **cathode (–)** terminals are tied to a **common GND pin (pin 3 or pin 8)**.
- To light a segment, drive the corresponding anode pin **HIGH (+5 V)** through a current-limiting resistor.

### (b) Common Anode (CA)
- All **anode (+)** terminals are tied to a **common Vcc pin**.
- To light a segment, drive the corresponding cathode pin **LOW (0 V)** through a current-limiting resistor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule of Thumb:** If the problem statement does *not* specify the type, **assume Common Cathode** — it is the most widely used configuration in 8051 trainer kits.

### 2.2 Segment Identification Convention

```
        ___a___
       |       |
      f|       |b
       |___g___|
       |       |
      e|       |c
       |___d___|   • dp
```

- Segments **a, b, c, d, e, f** form the outer "8".
- Segment **g** is the middle horizontal bar.
- Segment **dp** is the decimal point (typically not used for hex digits).

### 2.3 KTU Hex-Digit Encoding Lookup Table (Common Cathode)

| Hex Digit | Segments Lit (a b c d e f g) | Bit Pattern (gfedcba) | Hex Code (P1) |
|:---------:|:---------------------------:|:---------------------:|:-------------:|
| **0**     | a, b, c, d, e, f            | 0 1 1 1 1 1 1         | **0x3F**      |
| **1**     | b, c                        | 0 0 0 0 1 1 0         | **0x06**      |
| **2**     | a, b, d, e, g               | 0 1 0 1 1 0 1         | **0x5B**      |
| **3**     | a, b, c, d, g               | 0 1 0 0 1 1 1         | **0x4F**      |
| **4**     | b, c, f, g                  | 0 1 1 0 0 1 1         | **0x66**      |
| **5**     | a, c, d, f, g               | 0 1 1 0 1 1 1         | **0x6D**      |
| **6**     | a, c, d, e, f, g            | 0 1 1 1 1 1 1         | **0x7D**      |
| **7**     | a, b, c                     | 0 0 0 0 1 1 1         | **0x07**      |
| **8**     | a, b, c, d, e, f, g         | 0 1 1 1 1 1 1         | **0x7F**      |
| **9**     | a, b, c, d, f, g            | 0 1 1 0 1 1 1         | **0x6F**      |
| **A**     | a, b, c, e, f, g            | 0 1 1 1 0 1 1         | **0x77**      |
| **B**     | c, d, e, f, g               | 0 1 1 1 1 1 0         | **0x7C**      |
| **C**     | a, d, e, f                  | 0 0 1 1 1 0 1         | **0x39**      |
| **D**     | b, c, d, e, g               | 0 1 0 1 1 1 0         | **0x5E**      |
| **E**     | a, d, e, f, g               | 0 1 1 1 1 0 1         | **0x79**      |
| **F**     | a, e, f, g                  | 0 1 1 1 0 0 1         | **0x71**      |

> [!NOTE]
> For **Common Anode** displays, simply **invert every bit** (XOR with 0xFF) of the above table — e.g., 0x3F becomes **0xC0**, 0x06 becomes **0xF9**, and so on.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value (8051 @ 11.0592 MHz) |
|:-------:|----------|---------|------------------------------------|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Period (T) | $T = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | $T \approx 1.085~\mu s$ |
| 2 | Current Limiting Resistor | $R = \dfrac{V_{cc} - V_f}{I_f}$ | $R \approx 220~\Omega \text{ to } 330~\Omega$ |
| 3 | Power Dissipated per Segment | $P_{seg} = V_f \times I_f$ | $P_{seg} \approx 20~mW$ |
| 4 | Total Delay (Two Nested Loops) | $T_{delay} = N_1 \times N_2 \times k \times T$ | Depends on N₁, N₂ |
| 5 | Bit Position Value (P1.x) | $2^{x}$ where x ∈ {0..7} | P1.0 = 1, P1.7 = 128 |
| 6 | Inversion (CC ↔ CA) | $Code_{CA} = \overline{Code_{CC}} = 0xFF \oplus Code_{CC}$ | 0x3F ↔ 0xC0 |

> **Notation Note:** Absolute values have been written as `\vert x \vert` in formulas above to prevent markdown table breakage.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this experiment critical in Industry?**
> - 7-segment displays are still the **cheapest, brightest, and most power-efficient** numeric displays used in **weighing scales, fuel pumps, industrial counters, microwave ovens, and digital thermometers**.
> - In an **IoT edge node**, a microcontroller may use such a display for *localized status readouts* (e.g., temperature, RPM, error codes) **without needing a full LCD or cloud connectivity** — saving cost, power, and BOM complexity.
> - Understanding **lookup-table-driven segment encoding** is a foundational technique used in **character LCD font generation, dot-matrix displays, and OLED graphics** in modern embedded systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Current Limiting Resistor

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Compute the correct series resistor value for each segment LED connected to an 8051 port pin (Vcc = 5 V).

By **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around a single LED + resistor branch:

$$V_{cc} = V_R + V_f$$

The voltage drop across the resistor is therefore:

$$V_R = V_{cc} - V_f$$

By **Ohm's Law**, $V_R = I_f \times R$, so:

$$R = \frac{V_{cc} - V_f}{I_f}$$

### Numerical Evaluation (Red LED, Standard KTU Trainer Kit)

Substituting the standard textbook values:

$$R = \frac{5.0~V - 2.0~V}{10~mA} = \frac{3.0~V}{0.010~A} = 300~\Omega$$

**Step-by-step justification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & V_{cc} = 5.0~V \quad \text{(standard 8051 logic supply)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & V_f = 2.0~V \quad \text{(typical red LED forward voltage drop)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & I_f = 10~mA = 0.010~A \quad \text{(safe LED operating current)} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & V_R = V_{cc} - V_f = 5.0 - 2.0 = 3.0~V \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_f} = \dfrac{3.0}{0.010} = 300~\Omega \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Nearest standard E12 resistor value} = 330~\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Check:** $P_R = I_f^2 \times R = (0.010)^2 \times 300 = 0.03~W$. A standard **¼ watt (0.25 W) resistor** is more than sufficient — a ⅛ W resistor is also acceptable.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Software Delay Loop Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive a software delay that produces an approximate **500 ms** blanking time between successive hex digits, suitable for human-eye perception (typically 1 Hz to 5 Hz refresh).

The 8051 uses **12 clock pulses per machine cycle**. With a crystal of 11.0592 MHz:

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085~\mu s$$

### Inner DJNZ Loop Analysis

A single `DJNZ Rn, rel` instruction takes **2 machine cycles**.

For a single-level inner loop of **255 iterations** (R2 = FFh):

$$T_{inner} = 255 \times 2 \times T_{mc} = 510 \times 1.085~\mu s \approx 553.35~\mu s$$

### Outer Loop Analysis

For a two-level nested loop where the **outer loop runs N₁ times**:

$$T_{total} = N_1 \times T_{inner}$$

Solving for **N₁** to achieve a target delay of 500 ms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{target} &= 500~ms = 500{,}000~\mu s \\
N_1 \times 553.35~\mu s &= 500{,}000~\mu s \\
N_1 &= \frac{500{,}000}{553.35} \approx 903.5 \\
N_1 &\approx 903 \text{ iterations}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Refined Three-Level Delay (with overhead compensation)

In practice, loop setup, `MOV`, `DJNZ` of the outer loop, and `RET` add overhead. A common KTU-tested three-level delay uses values **(255, 255, 5)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &\approx 5 \times 255 \times (255 \times 2 \times T_{mc}) \\
&= 5 \times 255 \times 553.35~\mu s \\
&\approx 705{,}521~\mu s \approx 706~ms
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields roughly **0.7 seconds** — a perfect human-perceptible delay between hex digits.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Program Logic Flow

| Step | Action | Register / Variable | Duration |
|:----:|--------|---------------------|----------|
| 1 | Initialize counter i = 0 | R0 / index variable | 1 machine cycle |
| 2 | Lookup hex pattern for digit i | P1 = lookup[i] | 2 machine cycles |
| 3 | Output pattern to Port 1 (P1) | MOV P1, A | 1 machine cycle |
| 4 | Generate ~500 ms delay | delay(500) | ~460,000 cycles |
| 5 | Increment counter i | INC R0 | 1 machine cycle |
| 6 | Check if i < 16 | CJNE R0, #10H, loop | 2 machine cycles |
| 7 | If not, wrap to 0 (i = 0) | SJMP MAIN | 2 machine cycles |
| 8 | Repeat forever | — | Infinite |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** The 8051 **I/O ports cannot source/sink large currents**. The maximum current per pin is **~10 mA** for AT89C51. Driving all 7 segments + dp simultaneously (e.g., digit "8") draws up to **80 mA total** — still within the port's 80 mA absolute maximum, but **never exceed this** in practical designs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 7-Segment Display Pinout (Common Cathode)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Pin 1 - Segment e"]
    nodeB["Pin 2 - Segment d"]
    nodeC["Pin 3 - Common Cathode GND"]
    nodeD["Pin 4 - Segment c"]
    nodeE["Pin 5 - Decimal Point dp"]
    nodeF["Pin 6 - Segment b"]
    nodeG["Pin 7 - Segment a"]
    nodeH["Pin 8 - Common Cathode GND"]
    nodeI["Pin 9 - Segment f"]
    nodeJ["Pin 10 - Segment g"]
    nodeA --- segA["Segment e LED"]
    nodeB --- segB["Segment d LED"]
    nodeD --- segC["Segment c LED"]
    nodeF --- segD["Segment b LED"]
    nodeG --- segE["Segment a LED"]
    nodeI --- segF["Segment f LED"]
    nodeJ --- segG["Segment g LED"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — 8051 ↔ 7-Segment Interfacing

```mermaid
graph LR
    mcu1["AT89C51 Microcontroller"]
    portP1["Port 1 P1.0 to P1.7"]
    resBlock["Current Limiting Resistor Network 220 ohm x 8"]
    segDisp["7-Segment Common Cathode Display"]
    gnd1["GND Rail 0V"]
    vcc1["Vcc Rail plus 5V"]
    xtal1["11.0592 MHz Crystal Oscillator"]
    mcu1 --> portP1
    portP1 --> resBlock
    resBlock --> segDisp
    segDisp --> gnd1
    vcc1 --> mcu1
    vcc1 --> segDisp
    xtal1 --> mcu1
```

## 4.3 Program Flowchart — Sequential Hex Display 0 to F

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["START Power ON Reset"]
    initA["Initialize P1 as OUTPUT"]
    initA2["Set counter i equals 0"]
    lookupA["Read hex pattern from lookup table"]
    outputA["Send pattern to P1 Display digit i"]
    delayA["Call software delay routine 500 ms"]
    incA["Increment counter i"]
    checkA{"i less than 16?"}
    loopA["Loop back to lookup step"]
    endA["Wrap around i equals 0 Repeat forever"]
    startA --> initA
    initA --> initA2
    initA2 --> lookupA
    lookupA --> outputA
    outputA --> delayA
    delayA --> incA
    incA --> checkA
    checkA -- YES --> loopA
    loopA --> lookupA
    checkA -- NO --> endA
    endA --> initA2
```

## 4.4 Logical Segment-to-Port Pin Mapping Table

| Segment | Display Pin | 8051 Port Pin | Bit Position | Weight (2ⁿ) |
|:-------:|:-----------:|:-------------:|:------------:|:-----------:|
| a | 7 | P1.0 | bit 0 | 1 |
| b | 6 | P1.1 | bit 1 | 2 |
| c | 4 | P1.2 | bit 2 | 4 |
| d | 2 | P1.3 | bit 3 | 8 |
| e | 1 | P1.4 | bit 4 | 16 |
| f | 9 | P1.5 | bit 5 | 32 |
| g | 10 | P1.6 | bit 6 | 64 |
| dp | 5 | P1.7 | bit 7 | 128 |

> **Validation of Hex "0" (segments a, b, c, d, e, f — g OFF):**
> Binary 0b0111111 = 0x3F ✓ (matches the lookup table)
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Common Anode and Common Cathode 7-segment displays. Which one is used in the standard 8051 trainer kit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Common Cathode (CC) | Common Anode (CA) |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Common Terminal | All cathodes tied to **GND** | All anodes tied to **Vcc (+5V)** |
| To Light a Segment | Drive anode pin **HIGH (+5V)** | Drive cathode pin **LOW (0V)** |
| Current Direction | Current flows from MCU → LED → GND | Current flows from Vcc → LED → MCU |
| KTU Trainer Kit | ✅ **Used (LTS-547, LTS-367)** | ❌ Less common |

> **Key Point (1 mark):** Standard KTU 8051 trainer kits use **Common Cathode** displays.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
**Why is a current limiting resistor required for each segment of a 7-segment LED display? Calculate its value for Vcc = 5 V, V<sub>f</sub> = 2 V, and I<sub>f</sub> = 10 mA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A current limiting resistor is required to **protect the LED from excessive current** that would otherwise burn it out, and to **limit the sourcing/sinking current** drawn from the microcontroller I/O pin beyond its safe limit (10 mA for AT89C51).

**Calculation:**

$$R = \frac{V_{cc} - V_f}{I_f} = \frac{5 - 2}{0.010} = 300~\Omega$$

The nearest standard resistor value is **330 Ω** (1 mark for formula + 1 mark for final answer).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, internally split as **7 + 7 marks** for sub-parts (a) and (b). Students must answer **either full Question A OR full Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Standard Implementation**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 – Embedded Interfacing]**

**(a)** Draw the interfacing circuit diagram to connect a **Common Cathode 7-segment display** to the **Port P1 of an 8051 microcontroller**. Explain the role of current limiting resistors and the connection of the common pin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an **8051 Embedded C program** to display the hexadecimal digits **0 to F** on the 7-segment display with a delay of **1 second** between each digit. The lookup table should be stored in **code memory** using the `code` keyword. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Interfacing Circuit Description (7 marks):**

1. **Port P1 (P1.0 – P1.7)** of the 8051 is configured as **OUTPUT** by writing 0x00 to it during initialization.
2. Each segment pin (a–g, dp) of the 7-segment display is connected to **P1.0 through P1.7** respectively.
3. A **220 Ω – 330 Ω current limiting resistor** is connected in **series** between each port pin and the corresponding segment pin to limit the LED current to **~10 mA**.
4. The **Common Cathode (CC) pin (pin 3 or pin 8)** of the display is connected to **GND (0V)**.
5. The 8051 is powered by **+5 V** on pins 40 (Vcc) and 40 with **0.1 µF decoupling capacitors** near the power pins.
6. A **11.0592 MHz crystal** is connected between pins 18 (XTAL1) and 19 (XTAL2) with two **33 pF capacitors** to ground.
7. **Pin 9 (RESET)** is pulled HIGH momentarily via a 10 µF capacitor + 10 kΩ pull-down to enable power-on reset.

**ASCII Block Diagram:**

```
  +5V ──[ 330Ω ]── a ─┐
  +5V ──[ 330Ω ]── b ─┤
  P1.0  P1.1 ...     ─┤── [ 7-Segment Display ]
  ...                 ─┤   (Common Cathode)
  +5V ──[ 330Ω ]── dp ─┘            │
                                     │
                                    GND
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Embedded C Program — 8051 (Keil C51 syntax):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>                 // SFR declarations for 8051

// Hex pattern lookup table stored in CODE memory (Flash)
unsigned char code hex_lookup[16] = {
    0x3F,   // 0 → segments a, b, c, d, e, f
    0x06,   // 1 → segments b, c
    0x5B,   // 2 → segments a, b, d, e, g
    0x4F,   // 3 → segments a, b, c, d, g
    0x66,   // 4 → segments b, c, f, g
    0x6D,   // 5 → segments a, c, d, f, g
    0x7D,   // 6 → segments a, c, d, e, f, g
    0x07,   // 7 → segments a, b, c
    0x7F,   // 8 → all segments ON
    0x6F,   // 9 → segments a, b, c, d, f, g
    0x77,   // A → segments a, b, c, e, f, g
    0x7C,   // b → segments c, d, e, f, g
    0x39,   // C → segments a, d, e, f
    0x5E,   // d → segments b, c, d, e, g
    0x79,   // E → segments a, d, e, f, g
    0x71    // F → segments a, e, f, g
};

// Software delay function — generates ~1 second delay @ 11.0592 MHz
void delay_one_sec(void) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < 900; i++)         // Outer loop
        for(j = 0; j < 1275; j++);   // Inner loop → ≈1.005 s
}

void main(void) {
    unsigned char digit;
    P1 = 0x00;                       // Configure Port 1 as OUTPUT
    while(1) {                       // Infinite loop
        for(digit = 0; digit < 16; digit++) {
            P1 = hex_lookup[digit];  // Display current hex digit
            delay_one_sec();         // 1-second human-perceptible delay
        }
    }
}
```

**Program Explanation (mark distribution):**

- Header file & lookup table definition — **2 marks**
- `delay_one_sec()` function logic — **2 marks**
- `main()` function with infinite loop & counter — **2 marks**
- Correctness, indentation, and comments — **1 mark**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Implementation**

**(a)** Design the **complete hardware schematic** (with port connections, resistor values, and power supply) to interface a **Common Anode 7-segment display** to **Port P0 of 8051**, using **74LS245 buffer** for current amplification. Justify the need for the buffer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **equivalent 8051 Assembly Language program** using the `MOVC` instruction and the `DPTR` lookup table technique to display the same hex digits 0 to F. Show the complete delay subroutine with cycle count analysis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Hardware Design (7 marks):**

| Signal Line | Connection | Reason |
|-------------|------------|--------|
| P0.0 – P0.7 | 74LS245 inputs (A0 – A7) | P0 has no internal pull-ups; needs buffer |
| 74LS245 outputs (B0 – B7) | 330 Ω resistors → segments a–g, dp | Current amplification + LED protection |
| Common Anode pin | +5 V via 100 Ω resistor | Current limit for entire display |
| 74LS245 DIR pin | GND (Direction = A → B) | Ensure unidirectional data flow |
| 74LS245 OE̅ pin | GND (Enable active) | Enable the buffer |

**Justification for 74LS245 Buffer (2 marks):**
- **P0 port of 8051** is an **open-drain** port (no internal pull-up resistors).
- Without a buffer, segments may not light reliably; external pull-ups (10 kΩ) waste current and reduce brightness.
- **74LS245** provides strong totem-pole outputs capable of sinking up to **24 mA per pin** — sufficient to drive multiple LEDs directly.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**8051 Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H              ; Reset vector
        LJMP    MAIN               ; Jump to main program

;----------------------------------------------------------------
; DELAY SUBROUTINE — Generates ~1 second delay @ 11.0592 MHz
; Inner loop: 255 × 2 cycles = 510 cycles = ~553 µs
; Outer loop: 255 iterations → 255 × 553 µs ≈ 141 ms
; 7 outer passes  → ~987 ms ≈ 1 second
;----------------------------------------------------------------
DELAY:  MOV     R1, #07H          ; Outer counter = 7
LOOP3:  MOV     R2, #0FFH         ; Middle counter = 255
LOOP2:  MOV     R3, #0FFH         ; Inner counter = 255
LOOP1:  DJNZ    R3, LOOP1         ; 2 cycles × 255 = 510 cycles
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP2         ; Decrement middle
        DJNZ    R1, LOOP3         ; Decrement outer
        RET                      ; Return to caller

;----------------------------------------------------------------
; HEX LOOKUP TABLE — Common Anode codes (inverted bits)
;----------------------------------------------------------------
        ORG     0300H
TABLE:  DB      0C0H, 0F9H, 0A4H, 0B0H, 99H, 92H, 82H, 0F8H
        DB      80H,  90H, 88H,  83H, 0C6H, 0A1H, 86H, 8EH

;----------------------------------------------------------------
; MAIN PROGRAM — Display 0 to F cyclically
;----------------------------------------------------------------
MAIN:   MOV     P0, #0FFH         ; Configure P0 as output (write 1s)
        MOV     DPTR, #TABLE      ; Load DPTR with table base address
        MOV     R0, #00H          ; Counter R0 = 0 (hex digit index)

DISPLAY:MOV     A, R0             ; A = current digit index
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR        ; A = hex pattern from code memory
        MOV     P0, A             ; Output pattern to 7-segment display
        ACALL   DELAY             ; 1-second delay
        INC     R0                ; Increment digit counter
        CJNE    R0, #10H, DISPLAY ; Loop until R0 = 16, then restart
        SJMP    MAIN              ; Wrap around and start again
        END
```

**Cycle Count Analysis for DELAY subroutine:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{LOOP1} &= 255 \times 2 = 510 \text{ cycles} \\
T_{LOOP2} &= (510 + 1 + 2) \times 255 = 513 \times 255 = 130{,}815 \text{ cycles} \\
T_{LOOP3} &= (130{,}815 + 1 + 2) \times 7 = 130{,}818 \times 7 = 915{,}726 \text{ cycles} \\
T_{DELAY} &= 915{,}726 \times 1.085~\mu s \approx 993.5~ms \approx 1 \text{ second}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mistake (1-2 marks lost):**
> - ❌ **Forgetting to invert the bit pattern** for Common Anode displays. CC hex "0" = 0x3F, but CA hex "0" = **0xC0** (inverted).
> - ❌ **Not clearing P0 first.** P0 is open-drain; if not initialized to 0xFF, random segments may glow.
> - ❌ **Wrong lookup-table size** — must contain exactly **16 entries** for hex digits 0–F, not just 0–9.
> - ❌ **Counter check error** — should be `CJNE R0, #10H, DISPLAY` (hex 10 = decimal 16), NOT `#16`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Quick Recall — Pin This on Your Wall!**

- ✅ **7-segment display = 7 LEDs + 1 dp**, arranged in figure-of-8.
- ✅ **Common Cathode** → segments glow on **HIGH**; **Common Anode** → segments glow on **LOW**.
- ✅ Standard 8051 KTU lab uses **Common Cathode** (verify with your trainer kit).
- ✅ **Resistor formula:** $R = \dfrac{V_{cc} - V_f}{I_f}$; standard value = **330 Ω**.
- ✅ **Hex digit "0" (CC)** = **0x3F**; **Hex digit "0" (CA)** = **0xC0**.
- ✅ **One machine cycle** of 8051 = **12 / f<sub>osc</sub>** = **1.085 µs** (for 11.0592 MHz).
- ✅ `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` instruction is used to **read lookup tables stored in code memory**.
- ✅ The keyword **`code`** in Keil C51 places the array in **ROM/Flash** (saves RAM).
- ✅ Display digits sequentially using a `for` loop from **0 to 15** (i.e., `< 16`).
- ✅ Human-perceptible delay range = **200 ms to 1 second** (use ~500 ms to 1 s for clarity).
- ✅ Port P0 of 8051 is **open-drain** — always needs external pull-ups or a buffer (74LS245).
- ✅ **P1, P2, P3 are quasi-bidirectional** with internal pull-ups — safe to drive LEDs directly.
- ✅ All 16 hex digits (0–F) require **unique 7-segment codes**; use the **lookup table** approach, never recompute in real-time.
- ✅ In **Assembly**, set DPTR once outside the loop for performance optimization.
- ✅ Always include the `while(1)` infinite loop in `main()` — 8051 has no OS to return to.
- ✅ **Always ground the EA̅ pin** while using internal ROM (for AT89C51 with internal program memory).

> **End of Notes — Best of luck for your KTU 2024 Lab Exam!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interface a simple Switch and display its status through Relay, Buzzer and LED
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interface a simple Switch and display its status through Relay, Buzzer and LED

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **PCCPL408 - Microcontrollers and IoT Laboratory**, **Module Experiment 2** deals with **Digital Input and Output (I/O) Interfacing** using a general-purpose microcontroller (typically an **Arduino UNO** based on the **ATmega328P**). The experiment requires the student to read the logic state of a **momentary push-button switch (digital input)** and subsequently drive three distinct output actuators — a **Light Emitting Diode (LED)**, a **Piezoelectric Buzzer**, and an **Electromagnetic Relay** — all of which reflect the instantaneous status (HIGH or LOW) of the switch.

In formal KTU terminology, this is a classic implementation of a **Single-Input, Multi-Output (SIMO) embedded control loop**, where the microcontroller acts as the **Central Processing Element** that samples a binary sensor state and broadcasts a conditioned response to multiple heterogeneous loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment maps to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Implement interfacing of various input/output devices with microcontrollers."* It also touches **CO4**: *"Develop simple IoT-based applications."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **electrical switchboard** in your house. The wall switch (input) controls whether current flows to a lamp (LED), a doorbell chime (Buzzer), and a heavy motor (Relay). In this experiment, the **microcontroller replaces the direct human wiring** — it "reads" whether the switch is pressed, and then *intelligently* decides what each load should do.

- **LED** → Visual indicator (like a status light on a printer)
- **Buzzer** → Audible alert (like a microwave beep when food is ready)
- **Relay** → High-power switch (like the contactor that turns on an air conditioner)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Logic Levels (TTL/CMOS):**
> - **HIGH (Logic 1)** ≈ **+5 V** (on ATmega328P) → Switch is **OPEN** (with pull-up) or **CLOSED** (with pull-down)
> - **LOW (Logic 0)** ≈ **0 V (GND)** → Switch is **CLOSED** (with pull-up) or **OPEN** (with pull-down)

The default Arduino convention uses an **internal pull-up resistor (~20 kΩ to 50 kΩ)**, meaning the pin reads **HIGH when the switch is open** and **LOW when the switch is closed** (active-LOW logic).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Interface

**Step 1 — Configuring the Switch (Input Stage):**
- The push-button is wired between the **digital I/O pin** (e.g., D2) and **GND**.
- The internal pull-up resistor is enabled in software via `pinMode(pin, INPUT_PULLUP)`.
- This eliminates the need for an external pull-down resistor and prevents a **floating input** condition.

**Step 2 — Reading the Switch (Polling):**
- Inside the `loop()` function, the microcontroller continuously executes `digitalRead(switchPin)`.
- The result is a boolean value: `HIGH` (1) or `LOW` (0).
- This is the **sampling** phase of a real-time control system.

**Step 3 — Decision Logic (Control Algorithm):**
- A simple `if-else` structure routes the signal to the three output actuators.
- The "switch status" can be **mirrored, inverted, or toggled** depending on the application requirement (e.g., a security alarm might sound the buzzer only on a *change* of state, not on steady press).

**Step 4 — Driving the Outputs (Actuation Stage):**
- **LED** is driven directly (with a current-limiting resistor) from a digital pin capable of sourcing up to **40 mA** (absolute max per pin on ATmega328P, with **20 mA recommended**).
- **Buzzer** (active type) is driven directly with a transistor driver for louder piezo buzzers, or directly for low-current 5 V types.
- **Relay** **MUST** be driven through a **transistor switch (e.g., BC547)** because the relay coil typically draws **70 mA to 100 mA**, far exceeding the pin's safe current.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Variable Description | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LED Current-Limiting Resistor** | $R_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{source} - V_{F(LED)}}{I_{LED}}$ | $V_{source}$ = supply, $V_F$ = forward voltage drop of LED, $I_{LED}$ = desired forward current | $V_F \approx 2.0$ V (red), $I_{LED} \approx 15$ mA → $R \approx 220$ Ω |
| **Relay Coil Current** | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{supply}}{R_{coil}}$ | Current drawn by the relay electromagnet coil | 5 V / 70 mA typical |
| **Transistor Base Resistor** | $R_{B} = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_{BE}}{I_{B}}$ | $V_{in}$ = logic HIGH (5 V), $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V, $I_B$ = required base current | $I_B = I_{coil} / 10$ (using $\beta = 100$) |
| **Flyback Diode Rating** | $V_{R(Diode)} \geq V_{supply}$, $I_{F} \geq I_{coil}$ | Protects transistor from inductive kickback | **1N4007** is the industry standard |
| **Internal Pull-up Resistance** | $R_{PU}$ (ATmega328P) | Enabled via software | **20 kΩ to 50 kΩ** |
| **Arduino Pin Current Limit** | $I_{pin(max)}$ | Absolute max per I/O pin | **40 mA**, recommended **20 mA** |
| **Total Current per Port** | $\sum I_{pins}$ | Sum of currents from all pins of one port | **100 mA max** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is a Flyback Diode Mandatory across the Relay Coil?**
> When the relay coil is de-energized, the collapsing magnetic field induces a high-voltage reverse spike (back-EMF, $V = -L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$) that can easily exceed **100 V** for a few microseconds. This spike will destroy the driving transistor if not clamped. The **1N4007** flyback diode provides a freewheeling path.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This SIMO interfacing pattern is the **fundamental building block** of:
- **Industrial Control Panels** (start/stop push-buttons driving indicator lamps, horns, and motor contactors)
- **Home Automation Hubs** (a single wall switch controlling smart lights, audio alerts, and appliance relays via Wi-Fi/BLE)
- **Automotive dashboards** (a single sensor triggering multiple warnings: visual, audible, and a relay cut-off)
- **Security alarm systems** (a magnetic door switch triggering an LED, siren, and an SMS-relay)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: LED Current-Limiting Resistor

**Given:**
- Supply voltage from Arduino pin: $V_{source} = 5$ V
- Red LED forward voltage: $V_{F} = 2.0$ V
- Desired LED current: $I_{LED} = 15$ mA $= 0.015$ A

**Step 1:** Apply **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the loop containing the Arduino pin, the resistor, and the LED.

$$V_{source} = V_R + V_{F(LED)}$$

**Step 2:** Isolate the voltage drop across the resistor.

$$V_R = V_{source} - V_{F(LED)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values.

$$V_R = 5 - 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4:** Apply **Ohm's Law** to compute the required resistance.

$$R_{LED} = \frac{V_R}{I_{LED}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute numerical values.

$$R_{LED} = \frac{3.0}{0.015} = 200 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 6:** Choose the nearest **standard E12 resistor value** (the commercial tolerance series).

$$R_{LED(\text{chosen})} = 220 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 7 (Verification):** Recompute the actual current with the chosen resistor.

$$I_{LED(\text{actual})} = \frac{5 - 2.0}{220} = \frac{3.0}{220} \approx 13.6 \text{ mA}$$

This is **safely below the 20 mA recommended limit** and produces a bright, visible LED. ✔

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Transistor Base Resistor for the Relay

**Given:**
- Relay coil voltage: $V_{coil} = 5$ V
- Relay coil resistance: $R_{coil} = 70$ Ω
- Transistor: **BC547 NPN** with DC current gain $\beta_{(hFE)} = 100$ (typical, from datasheet)
- $V_{BE(sat)} \approx 0.7$ V
- Logic input voltage: $V_{in} = 5$ V (Arduino HIGH)

**Step 1:** Calculate the **collector current** (which equals the relay coil current once the transistor saturates).

$$I_C = I_{coil} = \frac{V_{coil}}{R_{coil}} = \frac{5}{70} \approx 71.4 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the **minimum base current** required to drive the transistor into saturation. We use a **forced beta** of $\beta_{forced} = 10$ (a common engineering rule of thumb to ensure hard saturation, also called **overdrive factor of 10**).

$$I_{B(\text{min})} = \frac{I_C}{\beta_{forced}} = \frac{71.4 \text{ mA}}{10} = 7.14 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3:** Apply KVL across the **input loop** (Arduino pin → base resistor → base-emitter junction).

$$V_{in} = V_{R_B} + V_{BE}$$

**Step 4:** Isolate the resistor voltage drop.

$$V_{R_B} = V_{in} - V_{BE} = 5 - 0.7 = 4.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5:** Apply Ohm's Law to compute the base resistor.

$$R_B = \frac{V_{R_B}}{I_B} = \frac{4.3}{7.14 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 602 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 6:** Choose the nearest standard E12 value (with some safety margin for component tolerances).

$$R_{B(\text{chosen})} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 7 (Verification):** With 1 kΩ, the base current becomes:

$$I_{B(\text{actual})} = \frac{4.3}{1000} = 4.3 \text{ mA}$$

This still drives the transistor into saturation because $I_{B(\text{actual})} > I_{B(\text{min})}$ ($4.3$ mA $> 7.14$ mA? No — 4.3 mA < 7.14 mA, so let me re-verify).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Note:** With 1 kΩ, the base current of **4.3 mA** is actually *below* the required **7.14 mA** for guaranteed saturation. Either:
> - **(a)** Use a **680 Ω** resistor instead, OR
> - **(b)** Use a **lower forced beta** of 5 (less safe margin but more conservative for $\beta$ variation).
>
> For a clean KTU lab answer, state: **"Choose $R_B = 680 \ \Omega$ (nearest standard value below 602 Ω) for guaranteed saturation."** A 1 kΩ resistor *may* work because the actual $\beta$ of a saturated BC547 is often much higher than the datasheet minimum, but it is **not guaranteed by design**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Truth Table for Switch-to-Actuator Mapping

| Switch State (D2) | `digitalRead()` | LED (D8) | Buzzer (D9) | Relay (D10) | System Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Released (Open)** | `HIGH` (1) | OFF | Silent | De-energized (NC contact) | Idle standby |
| **Pressed (Closed)** | `LOW` (0) | ON | Beeping/Tone | Energized (NO contact closed) | Active alarm/work mode |
| **Bouncing (transient)** | Oscillates | Flickers | Chatters | Chatters | **Debounce required!** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Switch Bounce:** Mechanical switches do not transition cleanly. The contacts physically bounce for **5 ms to 20 ms**, causing spurious readings. The Arduino `digitalRead()` is fast enough (~100 ns) to catch these bounces. A **software debounce** delay of **50 ms** or the use of a **hardware RC filter** (10 kΩ + 100 nF → $\tau = 1$ ms) is the standard mitigation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circuit Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUPPLY["POWER_DOMAIN"]
        v5["ARDUINO 5V"]
        gnd["ARDUINO GND"]
    end

    subgraph INPUT_BLOCK["INPUT_SECTION"]
        sw["PUSH_BUTTON_SWITCH"]
        pullup["INTERNAL_PULLUP_20K_TO_50K"]
    end

    subgraph MICRO_BLOCK["ATMEGA328P_MCU"]
        d2["DIGITAL_PIN_D2_INPUT"]
        d8["DIGITAL_PIN_D8_OUTPUT_PWM"]
        d9["DIGITAL_PIN_D9_OUTPUT_PWM"]
        d10["DIGITAL_PIN_D10_OUTPUT"]
    end

    subgraph LED_BLOCK["LED_OUTPUT_STAGE"]
        rled["R_LED_220_OHM"]
        dled["RED_LED"]
    end

    subgraph BUZ_BLOCK["BUZZER_OUTPUT_STAGE"]
        rbuz["R_BUZZ_OPTIONAL_100_OHM"]
        buzz["PIEZO_BUZZER_5V"]
    end

    subgraph RELAY_BLOCK["RELAY_DRIVER_STAGE"]
        rb["R_BASE_680_OHM"]
        q1["BC547_NPN_TRANSISTOR"]
        d1["FLYBACK_DIODE_1N4007"]
        coil["RELAY_COIL_5V"]
    end

    sw -->|ONE_TERMINAL| d2
    d2 -->|ENABLES| pullup
    sw -->|OTHER_TERMINAL| gnd

    d8 --> rled
    rled --> dled
    dled --> gnd

    d9 --> buzz
    buzz --> gnd

    d10 --> rb
    rb --> q1
    q1 -->|COLLECTOR| coil
    coil --> v5
    q1 -->|EMITTER| gnd
    d1 -->|PARALLEL_TO_COIL| coil
```

### 4.2 Program Flow Logic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START_PROGRAM]) --> initA["pinMode_D2_INPUT_PULLUP"]
    initA --> initB["pinMode_D8_D9_D10_OUTPUT"]
    initB --> initC["digitalWrite_ALL_PINS_LOW"]
    initC --> loopA([ENTER_LOOP])

    loopA --> readSw["digitalRead_switchPin_D2"]
    readSw --> decisionA{"switchState_LOW?"}

    decisionA -->|YES_PRESSED| activateA["digitalWrite_D8_HIGH_LED_ON"]
    activateA --> activateB["tone_D9_1000Hz_BUZZER_ON"]
    activateB --> activateC["digitalWrite_D10_HIGH_RELAY_ON"]
    activateC --> delayA["delay_200ms_DEBOUNCE"]
    delayA --> loopA

    decisionA -->|NO_RELEASED| deactivateA["digitalWrite_D8_LOW_LED_OFF"]
    deactivateA --> deactivateB["noTone_D9_BUZZER_OFF"]
    deactivateB --> deactivateC["digitalWrite_D10_LOW_RELAY_OFF"]
    deactivateC --> loopA
```

### 4.3 Reference Arduino Sketch (C/C++ — Appropriate for the Course)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This experiment is a **hardware-interfacing laboratory** in the *Microcontrollers and IoT* course. The Arduino sketch is provided in **C/C++ (Arduino language)**, which is the official language of the ATmega328P platform — **not Python**.

```cpp
// Experiment: Interface a Switch and Display its Status through Relay, Buzzer and LED
// Platform  : Arduino UNO (ATmega328P)
// Course    : PCCPL408 - Microcontrollers and IoT Laboratory

const int switchPin = 2;   // Digital Input (active-LOW with internal pull-up)
const int ledPin    = 8;   // Digital Output -> LED
const int buzzerPin = 9;   // PWM Output    -> Buzzer
const int relayPin  = 10;  // Digital Output -> Relay (via BC547)

void setup() {
  pinMode(switchPin, INPUT_PULLUP);  // Enable internal pull-up
  pinMode(ledPin,    OUTPUT);
  pinMode(buzzerPin, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(relayPin,  OUTPUT);

  // Initialise all outputs to OFF
  digitalWrite(ledPin,   LOW);
  noTone(buzzerPin);
  digitalWrite(relayPin, LOW);
}

void loop() {
  int state = digitalRead(switchPin);

  if (state == LOW) {                 // Switch is PRESSED
    digitalWrite(ledPin,   HIGH);
    tone(buzzerPin, 1000);            // 1 kHz beep
    digitalWrite(relayPin, HIGH);
    delay(200);                       // Software debounce
  } else {                            // Switch is RELEASED
    digitalWrite(ledPin,   LOW);
    noTone(buzzerPin);
    digitalWrite(relayPin, LOW);
    delay(50);
  }
}
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*What is the purpose of enabling `INPUT_PULLUP` on a switch input pin in Arduino, and what is the typical internal resistance value?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) `INPUT_PULLUP` enables an **internal pull-up resistor** inside the ATmega328P, connecting the pin to Vcc through a resistance.
- (1 Mark) This **prevents a floating input condition** (undefined logic levels due to high-impedance) when the switch is open, by defaulting the pin to **HIGH (5 V)**.
- (1 Mark) The internal pull-up resistance is in the range of **20 kΩ to 50 kΩ (typically ~30–50 kΩ)** per the ATmega328P datasheet.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Why is a flyback diode connected across a relay coil, and what is the typical part number used in lab experiments?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) When the relay coil is de-energized, the collapsing magnetic flux induces a **high reverse voltage spike (back-EMF)**, given by $V = -L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$, which can exceed **100 V** and destroy the driving transistor.
- (1 Mark) The **flyback diode** (reverse-biased during normal operation) provides a freewheeling current path that safely dissipates this stored magnetic energy as heat.
- (1 Mark) The standard lab component is the **1N4007** (1000 V reverse voltage, 1 A forward current — more than sufficient for a 5 V / 70 mA relay).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Module Experiment 2 — Choice Question A**

**Question A(a). [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Design the interface circuit for a 5 V DC relay (coil resistance 70 Ω) to be driven by an Arduino UNO digital pin through a BC547 transistor. Calculate the base resistor value for guaranteed saturation and explain the role of the flyback diode with relevant equations.*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**
- (2 Marks) **Driver Topology**: Arduino digital pin → Base resistor $R_B$ → Base of BC547 NPN. Collector connected to one end of the relay coil, the other end to **+5 V**. Emitter to **GND**. A flyback diode (1N4007) is connected **cathode to +5 V, anode to collector** (i.e., reverse-biased during normal operation).
- (2 Marks) **Current Calculation**:
  $$I_C = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_{coil}} = \frac{5}{70} \approx 71.4 \text{ mA}$$
  Using a forced $\beta$ of 10 (overdrive factor for hard saturation):
  $$I_{B(\text{min})} = \frac{I_C}{\beta_{forced}} = \frac{71.4}{10} = 7.14 \text{ mA}$$
- (2 Marks) **Base Resistor**:
  $$R_B = \frac{V_{in} - V_{BE}}{I_{B(\text{min})}} = \frac{5 - 0.7}{7.14 \text{ mA}} \approx 602 \text{ }\Omega$$
  Choose the **nearest lower standard value: 560 Ω** (or 680 Ω for slight design margin).
- (1 Mark) **Flyback Diode Explanation**: When the transistor switches OFF, the relay coil's inductance generates a back-EMF of $V = -L \cdot (di/dt)$, which appears as a large negative voltage at the collector. The 1N4007 becomes forward-biased and clamps this spike to roughly **−0.7 V**, protecting the transistor's collector-emitter junction from reverse breakdown.

---

**Question A(b). [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Write a complete Arduino sketch to read a push-button switch on pin D2 (with internal pull-up) and toggle an LED on D8, a buzzer on D9, and a relay on D10 simultaneously when the switch is pressed. Include proper debouncing.*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**
- (2 Marks) **Setup function**: Configure D2 as `INPUT_PULLUP`, D8/D9/D10 as `OUTPUT`. Initialise all outputs to `LOW` and call `noTone(9)`.
- (3 Marks) **Loop function with debounce**:
  - Read the switch state using `digitalRead(2)`.
  - If state is `LOW` (pressed), set all three outputs to active state, then `delay(50)` for debounce.
  - If state is `HIGH` (released), deactivate all three outputs, then `delay(50)`.
  - Use a `lastState` variable and millis-based non-blocking debounce for a professional implementation.
- (2 Marks) **Complete Code**:
  ```cpp
  const int sw = 2, led = 8, bz = 9, rl = 10;
  int last = HIGH;
  void setup() {
    pinMode(sw, INPUT_PULLUP);
    pinMode(led, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(bz, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(rl, OUTPUT);
  }
  void loop() {
    int cur = digitalRead(sw);
    if (cur != last) {
      delay(50);                       // debounce
      if (cur == LOW) {
        digitalWrite(led, HIGH);
        tone(bz, 1000);
        digitalWrite(rl, HIGH);
      } else {
        digitalWrite(led, LOW);
        noTone(bz);
        digitalWrite(rl, LOW);
      }
    }
    last = cur;
  }
  ```

---

> **Module Experiment 2 — Choice Question B**

**Question B(a). [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain the concept of switch bouncing in mechanical push-buttons. Compare hardware debouncing (RC filter) and software debouncing techniques. Design an RC low-pass filter to debounce a switch with a bounce time of 10 ms.*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**
- (2 Marks) **Switch Bounce**: Mechanical contacts do not make a clean transition. They physically **bounce multiple times for 5–20 ms** after closing, producing spurious `HIGH/LOW` transitions.
- (2 Marks) **Comparison Table** (verbal):
  - *Hardware RC filter*: passive, real-time, no CPU load, but adds components and introduces propagation delay $\tau = RC$.
  - *Software debounce*: no extra components, flexible timing, but uses CPU cycles and may block (if using `delay()`).
- (3 Marks) **RC Design**:
  - Choose $C = 0.1 \ \mu F$ (standard noise-immune value).
  - Set time constant $\tau = RC \approx 1$ to $2 \times$ bounce time. For 10 ms bounce, $\tau = 10$ ms.
  - $R = \dfrac{\tau}{C} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$.
  - Use a **Schmitt trigger** (e.g., 74HC14) after the RC filter to provide a clean, snap-action digital output. The Arduino pin has a Schmitt trigger on its input, so it is sufficient on its own for moderate-bounce applications.

---

**Question B(b). [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*With the help of a block diagram, describe the interfacing of a push-button switch with an Arduino to control an LED, a buzzer, and a 5 V relay (with transistor driver). Explain the role of each component and write the pseudo-code / Arduino sketch for the same.*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**
- (2 Marks) **Block Diagram Description**: Switch → Arduino D2 (with internal pull-up). Arduino D8 → 220 Ω resistor → LED → GND. Arduino D9 → 5 V piezo buzzer → GND. Arduino D10 → 680 Ω base resistor → BC547 base; collector → 5 V relay coil → +5 V; emitter → GND. **1N4007 flyback diode** across the coil (cathode to +5 V).
- (3 Marks) **Role of each component**:
  - *Switch*: Human-machine input device (sensor) — **input transducer**.
  - *220 Ω resistor*: Limits LED current to safe value $I = (5-2)/220 = 13.6$ mA.
  - *Buzzer*: Audible output indicator — **auditory transducer**.
  - *BC547*: Current amplifier; boosts the ~5 mA pin output to the ~70 mA needed by the relay coil.
  - *Flyback diode*: Suppresses the inductive kickback from the relay coil.
  - *Relay*: An **electromechanically isolated switch** capable of switching high-voltage / high-current AC or DC loads (e.g., 230 V AC fans, lights) using a low-voltage DC control signal.
- (2 Marks) **Pseudo-code / Sketch** is identical to Question A(b) above and earns full credit.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Mistakes in KTU Lab Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the base resistor** between the Arduino pin and the transistor base → instant destruction of the Arduino pin and the transistor. **−2 marks** if the schematic omits $R_B$.
> 2. **Drawing the flyback diode in the wrong polarity** (forward-biased during normal operation) → it will short-circuit the 5 V supply and burn out. Examiners *do* check this. **−1 to −2 marks.**
> 3. **Confusing INPUT vs. INPUT_PULLUP** → without pull-up, the pin **floats** when the switch is open, producing random LED/buzzer/Relay toggling. Examiners frequently ask: *"What happens if you don't use a pull-up resistor?"* — be ready.
> 4. **No debounce logic in the code** → the relay chatters audibly during the 5–20 ms bounce window. Examiners may consider this a **functional failure**. **−1 mark** if not addressed.
> 5. **Inverted logic error**: students often write `if (state == HIGH)` assuming "pressed = HIGH", but with `INPUT_PULLUP`, pressed = **LOW**. This reverses the entire system behavior. **−2 marks** (most critical mistake).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Switch Pin (D2)**: Configured as `INPUT_PULLUP` — pressed = **LOW**, released = **HIGH** (active-LOW).
- ✅ **LED Pin (D8)**: Driven directly through a **220 Ω current-limiting resistor** ($I \approx 13.6$ mA, safe).
- ✅ **Buzzer Pin (D9)**: Use `tone(pin, frequency)` to generate square wave; `noTone(pin)` to stop. PWM-capable pin recommended.
- ✅ **Relay Pin (D10)**: **MUST** use a transistor driver (BC547) + base resistor (560 Ω–1 kΩ) + **1N4007 flyback diode**.
- ✅ **Debounce**: Use **50 ms delay** (software) or **100 kΩ + 0.1 µF RC** (hardware) to suppress mechanical bounce.
- ✅ **Pin current limit**: **40 mA absolute max**, **20 mA recommended** per Arduino pin (ATmega328P).
- ✅ **Relay coil current**: $I_{coil} = V/R_{coil}$ — typically **70–100 mA** at 5 V.
- ✅ **Back-EMF formula**: $V_{spike} = -L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ — clamped by the flyback diode.
- ✅ **Mapping to KTU COs**: This experiment satisfies **CO2** (Interfacing I/O devices) and supports **CO4** (IoT application development).
- ✅ **Common examiner keywords**: *pull-up, debounce, flyback diode, saturation, forced beta, current limiting resistor, active-LOW*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarization with Arduino/Raspberry Pi and perform necessary software installation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization with Arduino/Raspberry Pi and perform necessary software installation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Definition of Arduino

**Arduino** is an open-source electronics platform based on easy-to-use hardware and software. It consists of a microcontroller board (typically based on the ATmega328P for the Arduino Uno) and a development environment (Arduino IDE) used to write, compile, and upload code to the board.

Formally, in KTU 2024 syllabus terms:
> **Arduino** is a single-board microcontroller development platform that uses an 8-bit AVR (or 32-bit ARM Cortex-M0+) RISC architecture processor, designed to read inputs (sensors, buttons) and convert them into outputs (LEDs, motors, displays).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For PCCPL408, the Arduino Uno R3 is the default reference board. Key specifications to memorize: **16 MHz** clock, **14 digital I/O pins (6 PWM)**, **6 analog input pins**, **32 KB Flash memory**, **2 KB SRAM**, **1 KB EEPROM**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Worker" Metaphor

Think of Arduino as a **dedicated factory worker** who is very good at performing a small, repetitive set of tasks very quickly and reliably. You give this worker:
- **Sensors** (eyes and ears) → inputs
- **Actuators** (arms and legs) → outputs
- **A written instruction manual** (your code) → that strictly defines every single action

The worker **cannot improvise** — they follow the instruction manual line-by-line, every single time. This is the deterministic, real-time, low-power nature of microcontrollers.

## 1.2 Definition of Raspberry Pi

**Raspberry Pi** is a low-cost, credit-card sized single-board computer (SBC) that plugs into a monitor, keyboard, and mouse. It runs a full operating system (typically Linux-based, such as Raspberry Pi OS, formerly Raspbian) and is built around a powerful Broadcom BCM2837/BCM2711 system-on-chip (SoC) containing an ARM Cortex-A quad-core processor.

> **Raspberry Pi** is a Linux-capable, general-purpose single-board computer (SBC) featuring a multi-core 64-bit ARM Cortex-A processor, GPU, RAM, and standard peripheral interfaces (USB, HDMI, Ethernet, GPIO), designed to function as a complete mini-desktop computer for embedded Linux, IoT, and multimedia applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Specification Memory Aid:** Raspberry Pi 4 Model B uses a **Broadcom BCM2711** SoC with **Quad-core Cortex-A72 @ 1.8 GHz**, **2/4/8 GB LPDDR4 RAM**, **40-pin GPIO header**, dual-band Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth 5.0.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Personal Computer" Metaphor

Imagine Raspberry Pi as a **miniature, complete desktop computer** (like a slim laptop, but smaller and cheaper). Unlike Arduino (the dedicated factory worker), Raspberry Pi is more like a **knowledgeable office manager** who:
- Runs a **full operating system** (Windows/Linux equivalent)
- Can browse the internet, play video, run Python scripts
- **Multitasks** — can run multiple programs simultaneously
- Uses its **GPIO pins** to talk to electronics, but also speaks **HDMI, USB, Ethernet**

## 1.3 Microcontroller vs. Microcomputer — The Core Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule for Exams:** A *microcontroller* (Arduino) is a CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O all in **one chip**, optimized for one task. A *microcomputer / SBC* (Raspberry Pi) is a **full computer** that boots an OS, designed to run many applications.

| Feature | Arduino (Uno) | Raspberry Pi 4 |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Microcontroller board | Single-Board Computer (SBC) |
| Processor | ATmega328P (8-bit AVR) | BCM2711 (64-bit ARM Cortex-A72) |
| Clock Speed | **16 MHz** | **1.8 GHz** (quad-core) |
| RAM | **2 KB** SRAM | **2/4/8 GB** LPDDR4 |
| OS Required | None (bare-metal) | Linux (Raspberry Pi OS) |
| Power | **~40 mA** | **~3 A @ 5 V** (3 W typical) |
| Boot Time | Instant (~100 ms) | 20–45 seconds |
| Cost (INR) | ~₹500 | ~₹4,500 |

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Arduino Architecture — Block-Level Breakdown

The Arduino Uno R3 architecture can be decomposed into the following functional blocks:

1. **ATmega328P Microcontroller (MCU)** — the "brain" executing instructions from Flash memory.
2. **Power Supply Section** — accepts either 7–12 V DC via barrel jack or 5 V via USB Type-B; regulates to stable **+5 V** and **+3.3 V** rails using linear regulators.
3. **USB-to-Serial Converter** — typically the **ATmega16U2** chip translates USB signals to UART (serial) for programming.
4. **Voltage Translator / Level Shifter** — provides 5 V logic on digital pins; analog pins read 0–5 V (10-bit ADC → 0–1023).
5. **Crystal Oscillator** — **16 MHz** quartz crystal providing the system clock; multiplied internally to drive timers.
6. **Reset Circuit** — manual reset button + auto-reset during programming via the DTR line of the USB chip.
7. **ICSP Header** — In-Circuit Serial Programming pins for low-level AVR programming bypassing the bootloader.

### The "Why & How" of Each Block

- **Why a crystal at 16 MHz?** — Provides a stable, accurate clock; AVR instructions typically execute in 1 clock cycle, so **16 MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second)**.
- **Why a USB-to-Serial chip?** — Modern PCs no longer have RS-232 ports; the ATmega16U2 bridges USB (PC side) to UART (MCU side) at standard baud rates (commonly **9600, 115200 bps**).
- **Why 10-bit ADC?** — The 10-bit Analog-to-Digital Converter yields $\vert 2^{10} \vert = \vert 1024 \vert$ discrete steps; the resolution per step is:

$$V_{step} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{5\text{ V}}{1023} \approx 4.89 \text{ mV per LSB}$$

## 2.2 Raspberry Pi Architecture — Block-Level Breakdown

The Raspberry Pi 4 Model B is built around a **System-on-Chip (SoC)** design:

1. **Broadcom BCM2711 SoC** — integrates:
   - **Quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 (64-bit)** CPU @ 1.8 GHz
   - **VideoCore VI GPU** for OpenGL ES 3.0 / H.265 4K decode
   - **DDR memory controller**
2. **LPDDR4 RAM (2/4/8 GB)** — directly mounted on the bottom of the board (Package-on-Package with SoC).
3. **GPIO Header (40-pin)** — 26 GPIO + power/ground pins, supporting I²C, SPI, UART, PWM.
4. **Connectivity** — 2× USB 3.0, 2× USB 2.0, Gigabit Ethernet, Wi-Fi 802.11ac, Bluetooth 5.0.
5. **Display / Camera** — 2× micro-HDMI (4K @ 60 Hz), 2-lane MIPI CSI/DSI.
6. **Storage** — microSD card slot (acts as the "hard disk" for the OS).

### The "Why & How" of the SoC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SoC?** Integrating CPU, GPU, RAM controller, and peripherals into a single die reduces power consumption, cost, and physical size — critical for embedded and IoT applications.

## 2.3 Software Installation — The Two Stacks

### Stack A: Arduino Software Installation

The Arduino development requires two components:
- **Arduino IDE (Integrated Development Environment)** — the editor + compiler + uploader
- **Board Drivers (CH340 or ATmega16U2)** — for USB-to-serial communication

**Installation Procedure (Windows 10/11):**

| Step | Action | Expected Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Download `Arduino IDE 2.x` from `arduino.cc/en/software` | `.exe` installer file |
| 2 | Run installer as Administrator | IDE installed in `C:\Program Files\Arduino IDE` |
| 3 | Connect Arduino Uno via USB Type-B | Device Manager shows new COM port |
| 4 | Install CH340 driver (for clone boards) | `USB-SERIAL CH340 (COMx)` appears |
| 5 | Launch IDE → **Tools → Board → Arduino AVR Boards → Arduino Uno** | Board selected |
| 6 | **Tools → Port → COMx** | Serial port selected |
| 7 | Upload **Blink** example (File → Examples → 01.Basics → Blink) → Click **Upload (→)** | Onboard LED (Pin 13) blinks at 1 Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Linux Users:** The IDE is available as an **AppImage** or via `sudo apt install arduino`. The `dialout` group membership is required to access the serial port: `sudo usermod -a -G dialout $USER`.

### Stack B: Raspberry Pi OS Installation

The Raspberry Pi uses a microSD card as its primary storage. The standard installation procedure is:

| Step | Action | Tool |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Download **Raspberry Pi Imager** from `raspberrypi.com/software` | Pi Imager (cross-platform) |
| 2 | Insert microSD card (≥16 GB, Class 10) into PC | Card reader |
| 3 | Launch Imager → Select OS (Raspberry Pi OS 64-bit) → Select Storage | GUI tool |
| 4 | Click the **gear icon** to pre-configure Wi-Fi, hostname, SSH, username | Advanced settings |
| 5 | Click **Write** | Image flashed (~5–10 min) |
| 6 | Insert card into Pi → Connect HDMI, keyboard, mouse → Power on | First boot (~30 s) |
| 7 | Run `sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade` | System up-to-date |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall to Avoid:** Never interrupt the flashing process! Pulling the card mid-write **will corrupt the filesystem** and require re-imaging.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| ADC Resolution | $V_{step} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | V | $n = 10$ for Arduino Uno |
| Arduino Clock Frequency | $f_{clk} = \mathbf{16 \text{ MHz}}$ | Hz | Crystal oscillator |
| Instruction Throughput | $1 \text{ MIPS per MHz}$ | MIPS | AVR: 1 cycle per instruction |
| PWM Frequency (Uno Pins 5,6) | $f_{PWM} \approx \mathbf{980 \text{ Hz}}$ | Hz | Timer0 |
| PWM Frequency (Uno Pins 9,10,11) | $f_{PWM} \approx \mathbf{490 \text{ Hz}}$ | Hz | Timer1/Timer2 prescaler |
| Baud Rate (UART) | $UBRR = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{16 \times BAUD} - 1$ | — | For asynchronous normal mode |
| Raspberry Pi SoC | **BCM2711** | — | Broadcom |
| Pi Clock Speed | $f_{CPU} = \mathbf{1.8 \text{ GHz}}$ | Hz | Cortex-A72 quad-core |
| Digital Read Resolution | $\vert 2^{10} \vert = \mathbf{1024}$ | levels | 0 to 1023 inclusive |
| Arduino Logic HIGH | $V_{IH} \geq \mathbf{0.6 \times V_{CC}}$ | V | 3.0 V at 5 V supply |
| Arduino Logic LOW | $V_{IL} \leq \mathbf{0.3 \times V_{CC}}$ | V | 1.5 V at 5 V supply |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in IoT?** In any IoT architecture, you typically need a **two-tier hierarchy**:
> - **Edge Tier (Arduino):** Real-time sensor sampling, motor control, deterministic timing — must be **low-power** and **responsive in microseconds**.
> - **Gateway/Cloud Tier (Raspberry Pi):** Data aggregation, network protocols (MQTT/HTTP), AI inference, dashboard hosting — must be **computationally capable** and run an OS.
>
> Understanding when to deploy each is the **core competency** assessed in PCCPL408.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Reading to Physical Voltage Conversion

**Problem Statement:** An Arduino Uno reads an analog value of **732** on pin A0 from a sensor. The reference voltage is the default **+5 V**. Calculate the corresponding input voltage.

### Step 1 — Identify the ADC Parameters

The Arduino Uno's ATmega328P contains a 10-bit successive-approximation ADC. This means the digital output is represented by:

$$n = 10 \text{ bits} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Number of discrete levels} = 2^{10} = 1024$$

The levels are numbered $0, 1, 2, \ldots, 1023$ (i.e., $2^{n} - 1$ steps).

### Step 2 — State the Reference Voltage

The default ADC reference voltage is the AVCC pin, which equals the board's regulated +5 V rail:

$$V_{ref} = V_{CC} = 5.0 \text{ V}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Resolution (LSB Size)

The voltage represented by one LSB (Least Significant Bit) of the ADC is given by the standard ADC resolution formula:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{5.0 \text{ V}}{1023}$$

$$V_{LSB} = 0.004887586 \text{ V/LSB} \approx 4.8876 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Input Voltage

The digital output $D$ maps to input voltage via linear interpolation:

$$V_{in} = D \times V_{LSB} = 732 \times \frac{5.0}{1023}$$

$$V_{in} = \frac{732 \times 5.0}{1023} = \frac{3660}{1023} = 3.5777... \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{in} \approx 3.578 \text{ V}}$$

### Step 5 — Verification (Percentage of Full Scale)

Percentage of full scale:

$$\%_{FS} = \frac{D}{1023} \times 100\% = \frac{732}{1023} \times 100\% \approx 71.55\%$$

Cross-check via voltage:

$$V_{in} = 0.7155 \times 5.0 = 3.5775 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

The two methods agree to within rounding error. This confirms the linear relationship.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pin Function Comparison

| Pin Group | Arduino Uno | Raspberry Pi 4 | Logic Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Digital I/O | 14 pins (D0–D13) | 26 GPIO (GPIO2–GPIO27) | 5 V vs **3.3 V** |
| PWM Capable | 6 pins (D3,5,6,9,10,11) | All GPIO support hardware PWM | — |
| Analog Input | 6 pins (A0–A5), 10-bit | **None** (requires external ADC like MCP3008) | — |
| I²C | A4 (SDA), A5 (SCL) | GPIO2 (SDA), GPIO3 (SCL) | 5 V vs 3.3 V |
| SPI | D10–D13 (SS, MOSI, MISO, SCK) | GPIO9 (MISO), GPIO10 (MOSI), GPIO11 (SCLK), GPIO8 (CE0) | 5 V vs 3.3 V |
| UART | D0 (RX), D1 (TX) | GPIO14 (TX), GPIO15 (RX) | 5 V vs 3.3 V |
| Max Current per pin | **40 mA** (abs. max) | **16 mA** (recommended) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Compatibility Issue:** The Raspberry Pi GPIO is **3.3 V tolerant ONLY**. Connecting a 5 V Arduino output directly to a 3.3 V Pi GPIO will **damage the Pi's SoC**. A **bidirectional logic level converter** is mandatory for Arduino-to-Pi interfacing.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: UART Baud-Rate Register Calculation

**Problem:** Configure the Arduino Uno's UART to communicate at **9600 baud** with the host PC.

### Step 1 — Identify Clock Source

The UART baud-rate generator on the ATmega328P is clocked by the system clock, $f_{clk} = 16 \text{ MHz}$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Baud-Rate Formula

The standard formula for the USART Baud Rate Register (UBRR) in asynchronous normal mode is:

$$UBRR = \frac{f_{clk}}{16 \times \text{BAUD}} - 1$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Values

$$UBRR = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 9600} - 1 = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{153600} - 1$$

$$UBRR = 104.1667 - 1 = 103.1667$$

### Step 4 — Round to Nearest Integer

The UBRR register must hold an integer (16-bit on ATmega328P). We round to **103**:

$$UBRR = 103$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Resulting Actual Baud Rate

The actual baud rate achieved with UBRR = 103 is:

$$\text{BAUD}_{actual} = \frac{f_{clk}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 104} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{1664}$$

$$\text{BAUD}_{actual} = 9615.38 \text{ bps}$$

### Step 6 — Calculate the Percent Error

$$\%_{\text{error}} = \frac{\vert \text{BAUD}_{actual} - \text{BAUD}_{target} \vert}{\text{BAUD}_{target}} \times 100\%$$

$$\%_{\text{error}} = \frac{\vert 9615.38 - 9600 \vert}{9600} \times 100\% = 0.16\%$$

$$\boxed{\%_{\text{error}} \approx 0.16\% \quad (\text{well within the 2\% tolerance for UART})}$$

This is why 9600 baud is the conventional default for Arduino projects.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Arduino Uno R3 — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    USBport[USB Type B Port] --> SerialChip[ATmega16U2 USB to Serial]
    SerialChip --> UART[UART Lines RX TX]
    PowerJack[DC Barrel Jack 7 to 12V] --> Reg5V[5V Regulator]
    PowerJack --> Reg3V3[3.3V Regulator]
    Reg5V --> MCU[ATmega328P MCU]
    Reg3V3 --> MCU
    Crystal[16 MHz Crystal] --> MCU
    MCU --> DigitalPins[Digital Pins D0 to D13]
    MCU --> AnalogPins[Analog Pins A0 to A5]
    MCU --> PWM[PWM Channels 6 pins]
    MCU --> ICSP[ICSP Header 6 pins]
    ResetBtn[Reset Button] --> MCU
    LED13[Onboard LED Pin 13] --> DigitalPins
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Power enters either via USB (5 V) or the barrel jack (7–12 V → regulated to 5 V/3.3 V). The crystal provides the master clock. The MCU drives all I/O pins; the 16U2 chip serves solely as a USB-to-serial bridge for programming and serial monitor communication.

## 4.2 Raspberry Pi 4 — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SoC[BCM2711 System on Chip]
        CPU[Quad Core ARM Cortex A72 1.8 GHz]
        GPU[VideoCore VI GPU]
        MEMCTRL[Memory Controller]
        IOPERIP[IO Peripherals I2C SPI UART PWM]
    end
    RAM[LPDDR4 RAM 2 to 8 GB] --- MEMCTRL
    CPU --- MEMCTRL
    GPU --- MEMCTRL
    IOPERIP --> GPIO[40 pin GPIO Header]
    USB3[2x USB 3.0] --> IOPERIP
    USB2[2x USB 2.0] --> IOPERIP
    ETH[Gigabit Ethernet] --> IOPERIP
    WIFI[Wi Fi Bluetooth 5.0] --> IOPERIP
    HDMI[2x micro HDMI 4K output] --> GPU
    SDCard[microSD Card OS Storage] --> IOPERIP
    PMU[Power Management Unit] --> SoC
    USB-C[USB C Power 5V 3A] --> PMU
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The BCM2711 SoC is the heart; everything (CPU, GPU, memory, peripherals) lives on a single die. External RAM is mounted via a high-bandwidth Package-on-Package interface. The microSD card holds the OS image; the USB-C port supplies the 5 V/3 A power rail.

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions for Question B

### **Solution for Question B — Part (a)**

#### Raspberry Pi 4 Architecture (Block Diagram — Refer Section 4.2)

The Raspberry Pi 4 Model B is built around the **Broadcom BCM2711 SoC** which integrates:

- **Quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 64-bit CPU** @ 1.8 GHz
- **VideoCore VI GPU** supporting OpenGL ES 3.0, H.265 4Kp60 hardware decode
- **DDR memory controller** interfacing with LPDDR4 RAM (2/4/8 GB)
- **Peripheral controllers** for USB, Ethernet, GPIO, I²C, SPI, UART, I²S, PWM

External interfaces include:

- **2× micro-HDMI** ports (4K @ 60 Hz)
- **2× USB 3.0 + 2× USB 2.0** ports
- **Gigabit Ethernet** (RJ45)
- **40-pin GPIO header**
- **microSD card slot** (OS storage)
- **USB Type-C** power input (5 V / 3 A recommended)

#### Comparison: GPIO Features

| Feature | Arduino Uno | Raspberry Pi 4 |
|---|---|---|
| Total GPIO pins | 14 digital + 6 analog | 26 usable GPIO |
| Logic level | **5 V** | **3.3 V** (NOT 5 V tolerant) |
| Max pin current | 40 mA | 16 mA (recommended) |
| Analog input | Yes (6 channels, 10-bit) | No (requires external ADC) |
| PWM | Software/HW on 6 pins | Hardware PWM on all GPIO |
| Built-in buses | I²C, SPI, UART | I²C, SPI, UART, I²S, PCM |

(7 marks: 4 for architecture explanation, 3 for comparison table.)

---

### **Solution for Question B — Part (b)**

#### Step 1 — Identify ADC Parameters

The Arduino Uno's ATmega328P features a 10-bit ADC:

$$n = 10 \text{ bits} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Total levels} = 2^{10} = 1024$$

Number of discrete steps: $\vert 2^{10} - 1 \vert = 1023$.

#### Step 2 — State the Reference Voltage

The default $V_{ref}$ is the AVCC rail equal to the +5 V supply:

$$V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate the LSB (Resolution)

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{5.0}{1023} = 4.8876 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V/LSB}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the Input Voltage

Given digital reading $D = 645$:

$$V_{in} = D \times V_{LSB} = 645 \times \frac{5.0}{1023}$$

$$V_{in} = \frac{3225}{1023} = 3.1524 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{in} \approx 3.152 \text{ V}}$$

#### Step 5 — ADC Resolution — Conceptual Explanation

**ADC resolution** refers to the **smallest change in input voltage** that the ADC can detect and represent as a change in the digital output code. For a 10-bit ADC with a 5 V reference, the resolution is **4.8876 mV per LSB**. This means any input voltage change smaller than ~4.89 mV will not produce a change in the digital reading. Higher resolution (12-bit, 16-bit) allows finer measurement of small signals at the cost of conversion time.

(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for calculation, 2 for concept explanation, 1 for final boxed answer with units.)

> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PCCPL408 Lab Records & Viva:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in numeric answers** — Always write "V" or "mV" after a voltage. An answer of "3.152" without units loses **0.5–1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing analog pins with digital pins on Arduino** — Pin A0–A5 are analog **input only**; they do not support `digitalWrite()`. Saying otherwise is a factual error.
> 3. **Calling Raspberry Pi a microcontroller** — This is the most common viva mistake! It is a **single-board computer (SBC)** or **microcomputer**, not a microcontroller.
> 4. **5 V → 3.3 V interfacing** — Students often forget that Pi GPIOs are **NOT 5 V tolerant**. Drawing a direct wire from Arduino 5 V output to Pi GPIO will **permanently damage the SoC**. Always mention a **level shifter** in any interfacing diagram.
> 5. **Skipping the bootloader mention in Arduino architecture** — Mentioning the **optiboot bootloader** in the ATmega328P architecture question shows depth and earns bonus marks.
> 6. **Wrong baud rate in the UART derivation** — The factor of 16 (not 2) in the UBRR formula is the most frequent calculation error. Remember: the baud-rate generator is divided by 16 in asynchronous normal mode.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Pin This to Your Wall!**

- [ ] **Arduino Uno = Microcontroller** | **Raspberry Pi 4 = Single-Board Computer (SBC)**
- [ ] Arduino clock: **16 MHz** | Pi clock: **1.8 GHz** (Cortex-A72 quad-core, 64-bit)
- [ ] Arduino RAM: **2 KB SRAM** | Pi RAM: **2/4/8 GB LPDDR4**
- [ ] Arduino ADC: **10-bit, 6 channels** | Pi: **NO built-in ADC**
- [ ] Arduino logic level: **5 V** | Pi logic level: **3.3 V (NOT 5 V tolerant)**
- [ ] **ADC resolution formula:** $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$
- [ ] For 5 V reference, 10-bit: $V_{LSB} \approx 4.89 \text{ mV}$
- [ ] Arduino IDE download source: **`arduino.cc/en/software`**
- [ ] Raspberry Pi Imager download: **`raspberrypi.com/software`**
- [ ] Default Raspberry Pi OS boot medium: **microSD card (≥16 GB, Class 10)**
- [ ] Pi first boot time: **20–45 seconds**; Arduino boot: **~100 ms** (no OS)
- [ ] **UBRR formula for 9600 baud @ 16 MHz = 103** (actual 9615 bps, 0.16% error)
- [ ] Clone Arduino boards use the **CH340** USB-to-serial chip (driver often required)
- [ ] Arduino typically used for: **real-time sensor/actuator control**
- [ ] Pi typically used for: **gateway, networking, OS-hosted applications, AI**
- [ ] Always use a **logic level converter** when interfacing 5 V Arduino with 3.3 V Pi
- [ ] Arduino's `analogRead()` returns **0–1023** (NOT 0–1024)
- [ ] Total GPIO on Pi 4 header: **40 pins (26 usable GPIO, rest are power/ground)**
- [ ] Recommended Pi 4 PSU: **5 V / 3 A via USB-C**
- [ ] **NEVER** interrupt microSD card flashing — corrupts the filesystem
- [ ] UART in ATmega328P: asynchronous mode uses **÷16** prescaler (factor of 16, not 2)

---

*End of PCCPL408 — Module Experiment 2 Notes — Familiarization with Arduino/Raspberry Pi and Software Installation*
