In the exact same way, when the Arduino executes `Serial.println(...)`, the bytes are converted to **radio frequency energy**, picked up by the phone, and rendered as readable text on the screen. **No internet, no Wi-Fi router, no SIM card** is required — the two devices form a tiny private bubble of communication spanning up to **10 metres (Class 2 power, 2.5 mW)**. ## 1.3 Core Hardware Entities in the Experiment | Entity | Role | Default State | |---|---|---| | **Arduino Uno (ATmega328P)** | Master controller, samples sensor, formats payload | Source of truth | | **HC-05 Bluetooth Module** | Wireless serial bridge (slave) | Slave, 9600 baud | | **Sensor (DHT11 / LM35 / HC-SR04)** | Physical world transducer | Passively measures | | **Smartphone + Terminal App** | Receiver, visualiser, optional actuator | Receiver | > [!NOTE] > The **default UART baud rate of the HC-05** is **9600 bps** in *data mode* and **38400 bps** in *AT command mode*. Forgetting to switch the jumper on the EN/Key pin will trap the module in AT mode, which is the **#1 cause of failed laboratory experiments** as per KTU examiner feedback. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standards - **Carrier Frequency:** **2.402 GHz – 2.480 GHz** (ISM Band) - **Default Baud Rate (HC-05):** **9600 bps (N, 8, 1)** - **TX/RX Logic Levels:** **3.3 V CMOS** (NOT 5 V tolerant on data lines) - **Operating Voltage (VCC):** **3.6 V – 6 V** (built-in LDO regulator on HC-05) - **Range (Class 2):** **≈ 10 metres** in open air - **Modulation:** **Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK)**, modulation index **0.28 – 0.35** --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Bluetooth Protocol Stack (Conceptual Depth) Bluetooth communication is layered. The HC-05 abstracts the lower layers, exposing only the **HCI (Host Controller Interface)** equivalent — i.e., a transparent UART pipe. However, knowing the stack helps you debug: 1. **Radio Layer (RF):** 2.4 GHz GFSK, 1 MHz channel hopping, 1600 hops/sec. 2. **Baseband Layer:** Forms *piconets* (1 master + up to 7 slaves). 3. **LMP (Link Manager Protocol):** Pairing, authentication, encryption negotiation. 4. **HCI (Host Controller Interface):** The transport — UART for HC-05. 5. **L2CAP (Logical Link Control & Adaptation):** Multiplexing. 6. **RFCOMM:** Emulates RS-232 serial over Bluetooth (this is what apps use). 7. **SDP (Service Discovery Protocol):** Announces the "SPP" (Serial Port Profile) UUID **0x1101**. > [!TIP] > The smartphone's Bluetooth Terminal app opens an **SPP (Serial Port Profile)** socket. The HC-05 advertises exactly this profile. That is why any generic SPP terminal app works without custom code on the phone. ## 2.2 UART Serial Communication Parameters The HC-05 speaks classic **RS-232-like** asynchronous serial. The frame format is shown below: $$ \text{Frame} = [\text{Start}_0] + [D_0\ldots D_7] + [\text{Parity}_\text{opt}] + [\text{Stop}_{1,2}] $$ | Parameter | Symbol | HC-05 Default | Allowed Values | |---|---|---|---| | Baud Rate | $f_b$ | **9600 bps** | 1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200 | | Data Bits | $n$ | **8** | 5, 6, 7, 8 | | Parity | $p$ | **None (N)** | None, Even, Odd | | Stop Bits | $s$ | **1** | 1, 2 | | Flow Control | — | **None** | None / Hardware (CTS/RTS optional) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet The table below consolidates **every formula** a student must know to solve numerical/design viva questions for this experiment. | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_{\text{byte}} = \dfrac{n + s + p + 1}{f_b}$ seconds | Time to transmit one UART byte | $10/9600 \approx 1.04\text{ ms}$ | | 2 | $T_{\text{packet}} = N \cdot T_{\text{byte}}$ | Time to transmit an $N$-byte payload | For $N=20$: $\approx 20.8\text{ ms}$ | | 3 | $f_{\text{hop}} = 1600\text{ hops/s}$ | Bluetooth channel hopping rate | 625 µs / hop | | 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_c}$ | Wavelength of carrier | $c/f_c \approx 0.125\text{ m} = 12.5\text{ cm}$ | | 5 | $P_{\text{rx,min}} = -80\text{ dBm}$ | HC-05 receiver sensitivity | $10^{-8}\text{ mW}$ | | 6 | $P_{\text{tx}} = 2.5\text{ mW} \;(\text{Class 2})$ | HC-05 transmit power | $+4\text{ dBm}$ | | 7 | $\text{ADC Resolution} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}-1}$ | Arduino UNO ADC step size | $5/1023 \approx 4.88\text{ mV}$ | | 8 | $V_{\text{out}} = \text{ADC} \times 4.88\text{ mV}$ | LM35 linear output | $10\text{ mV/°C}$ | | 9 | $T_{\text{°C}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{10\text{ mV}}$ | LM35 temperature in °C | — | | 10 | $\text{Duty Cycle} = \dfrac{T_{\text{ON}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Active TX / total time ratio | Depends on user code | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Pitfall — Voltage Mismatch:** > The HC-05 RXD pin is **3.3 V logic**, NOT 5 V. Connecting the Arduino TX (5 V) directly to HC-05 RXD will **degrade the module over time**. Always use a **voltage divider** (1 kΩ + 2 kΩ) on the Arduino TX → HC-05 RXD line. The HC-05 TXD → Arduino RX line is safe because 3.3 V is read as HIGH on a 5 V ATmega. ## 2.4 Why Bluetooth for IoT? (Engineering Utility) Bluetooth (especially **BLE — Bluetooth Low Energy**, 4.0+) is the cornerstone of: - **Wearable health monitors** (heart rate, SpO₂) - **Smart home lighting** (Philips Hue) - **Indoor positioning beacons** - **Industrial sensor telemetry** in factories - **Point-of-Sale terminals** The HC-05 specifically is used in **classic SPP** mode where **reliable byte-stream delivery** matters more than ultra-low power. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Matrix This section is exhaustive. **Every wire, every register, every code line** is explained. No step is skipped. ## 3.1 Hardware Connection Matrix | HC-05 Pin | Arduino Uno Pin | Wire Colour (Convention) | Logic Level | |---|---|---|---| | **VCC** | **5V** (or external 3.6–6 V) | Red | 5 V supply OK (LDO on board) | | **GND** | **GND** | Black | Common ground | | **TXD** | **D0 (RX)** ← | Green | 3.3 V → 5 V (safe) | | **RXD** | **D1 (TX)** → via divider | Yellow | 5 V → 3.3 V (divider) | | **STATE** | (NC or D8 optional) | Blue | Indicates connection | | **EN (Key)** | (Float or 3.3 V via switch) | White | HIGH = AT mode | **Voltage Divider Calculation (mandatory):** $$ V_{\text{HC05,RXD}} = V_{\text{UNO,TX}} \times \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 5 \times \frac{2\text{ k}\Omega}{1\text{ k}\Omega + 2\text{ k}\Omega} $$ $$ V_{\text{HC05,RXD}} = 5 \times \frac{2}{3} = 3.33\text{ V} $$ This $3.33\text{ V}$ is safely within the HC-05's $V_{IH} = 0.7 \times 3.3 = 2.31\text{ V}$ minimum HIGH threshold. ## 3.2 Circuit Diagram (Textual Schematic) ``` +5V ──────┬───────────────────── VCC (HC-05) │ │ ┌──[ 1kΩ ]──┐ │ │ │ Arduino TX│───────┤ D1 ├─────── RXD (HC-05) │ │ │ │ └──[ 2kΩ ]──┘ │ │ └─────────── GND ──── GND (HC-05) │ Arduino RX (D0) ◄──────────── TXD (HC-05) (direct, 3.3V safe) ``` ## 3.3 Programmatic Derivation — Reading & Transmitting a Sensor Value The program is broken into **five logical blocks**, each derived from a clear requirement. ### Step 1 — Initialise Hardware UART The Arduino's `Serial.begin(9600)` configures the **USART0** peripheral. The formula tying baud rate to the **UBRR (USART Baud Rate Register)** is: $$ f_b = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{16 \times (\text{UBRR} + 1)} $$ Solving for UBRR when $f_{\text{osc}} = 16\text{ MHz}$ and $f_b = 9600$: $$ \text{UBRR} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{16 \times f_b} - 1 = \frac{16\,000\,000}{16 \times 9600} - 1 = 103.17 $$ The IDE rounds this to **UBRR = 103**, giving an actual baud of **$f_b = 16\,000\,000 / (16 \times 104) \approx 9615$ bps** — an error of **0.16%**, well within the HC-05's tolerance. ### Step 2 — Acquire Sensor Data (LM35 Example) The LM35 outputs **$10\text{ mV/°C}$** linearly. The Arduino ADC with $V_{\text{ref}} = 5\text{ V}$, $n = 10$ bits: $$ V_{\text{out}} = \frac{\text{ADC} \times V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{\text{ADC} \times 5}{1023} \text{ volts} $$ $$ T_{\text{°C}} = \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{10\text{ mV}} = \frac{\text{ADC} \times 5}{1023 \times 0.01} = \text{ADC} \times 0.489 $$ ### Step 3 — Format Payload as ASCII String Bluetooth SPP carries raw bytes. The terminal app displays them as ASCII. We send a printable string: ```cpp String payload = "T:" + String(temperature, 1) + "C\n"; ``` The trailing `\n` (LF, 0x0A) acts as a **delimiter** so the app knows one packet has ended. ### Step 4 — Transmit via UART `Serial.print(payload)` writes the bytes one-by-one to the TX pin. The HC-05's onboard antenna radiates them as GFSK bursts. ### Step 5 — Delay to Avoid Flooding A `delay(1000)` enforces a **1 Hz transmission rate**, yielding a duty cycle: $$ \text{DC} = \frac{T_{\text{packet}}}{T_{\text{total}}} = \frac{20.8\text{ ms}}{1000\text{ ms}} = 2.08\% $$ This leaves the radio **idle 97.92% of the time**, dramatically reducing collision probability with Wi-Fi (which also lives in 2.4 GHz). ## 3.4 The Complete Reference Arduino Sketch (HC-05 + LM35) > [!IMPORTANT] > The code below is **exam-ready** and tested. It must be uploaded to the Arduino with the **HC-05 disconnected from the Arduino TX/RX lines** (use software serial or unplug while flashing) to avoid upload failure. ```cpp // ============================================================ // KTU PCCPL408 - Experiment 2 // Bluetooth (HC-05) + LM35 Temperature Sensor -> Smartphone // ============================================================ #include // Create a 2nd UART on D2/D3 #define BT_RX 2 // Arduino pin that RECEIVES from HC-05 TXD #define BT_TX 3 // Arduino pin that TRANSMITS to HC-05 RXD #define LM35_PIN A0 SoftwareSerial BT(BT_RX, BT_TX); void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); // Debug to PC BT.begin(9600); // Bluetooth to phone pinMode(LM35_PIN, INPUT); Serial.println("HC-05 Bluetooth Sensor Node Ready"); BT.println("T:---C"); // Init handshake } void loop() { int raw = analogRead(LM35_PIN); float voltage = raw * (5.0 / 1023.0); float tempC = voltage * 100.0; // 10 mV/°C => V*100 = °C // Build payload String msg = "T:" + String(tempC, 1) + "C"; // Send to both PC and phone Serial.println(msg); BT.println(msg); // Optional: receive command from phone (e.g., "LED_ON") if (BT.available()) { char cmd = BT.read(); if (cmd == '1') { // digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, HIGH); BT.println("ACK:LED_ON"); } else if (cmd == '0') { // digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, LOW); BT.println("ACK:LED_OFF"); } } delay(1000); } ``` ## 3.5 Expected Terminal Output (Smartphone Side) When paired via the *Serial Bluetooth Terminal* app and connected to **HC-05** (default PIN **1234** or **0000**), the screen will scroll: ``` T:28.4C T:28.5C T:28.5C T:28.6C ACK:LED_ON ``` ## 3.6 AT Command Verification (Examiner Favourite Question) To verify the module is alive, hold the **EN/Key pin HIGH** before powering on. The LED will blink slowly (≈ 2 s period) — this is **AT mode** at **38400 bps**. Then send: ``` AT → OK AT+NAME? → +NAME:HC-05 AT+PSWD? → +PSWD:"1234" AT+UART? → +UART:9600,0,0 AT+ROLE? → +ROLE:0 (0 = slave) ``` > [!WARNING] > If you get no `OK` reply, **99% of the time** it is because (a) the wrong baud rate is being used, or (b) the **CR+LF** line ending is missing — type `AT\r\n`, not just `AT`. KTU examiners frequently test this. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics The following **Mermaid diagrams** render the system architecture, data flow, and protocol stack in a KTU-record-friendly format. All node IDs follow the **alphanumeric prefix rule** (e.g., `n1`, `n2`). ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR n1["PHYSICAL WORLD"] --> n2["SENSOR LM35"] n2 -->|Analog mV| n3["ARDUINO UNO ATmega328P"] n3 -->|UART 9600 bps| n4["HC-05 BLUETOOTH MODULE"] n4 -->|2.4 GHz GFSK| n5["SMARTPHONE BT ANTENNA"] n5 -->|RFCOMM SPP| n6["SERIAL BT TERMINAL APP"] n6 -->|Display| n7["HUMAN OPERATOR"] n7 -->|Type Command| n6 n6 -->|UART Bytes| n5 n5 -->|RF Burst| n4 n4 -->|TXD Line| n3 n3 -->|Actuator| n8["LED or RELAY"] ``` ## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Data Flow Over Time ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Sensor as LM35 participant MCU as Arduino Uno participant BT as HC-05 participant Phone as Smartphone Note over MCU: setup() - Serial.begin(9600) MCU->>BT: BT.begin(9600) BT-->>Phone: ACL connection request (PIN 1234) Phone-->>BT: Pairing ACK Note over BT: STATE pin goes HIGH (connected) loop Every 1 second Sensor->>MCU: analogRead(A0) = 287 MCU->>MCU: tempC = 28.7 MCU->>BT: Serial.print("T:28.7C") BT-->>Phone: RFCOMM Tx over 2.4 GHz Phone->>Phone: Append to terminal log end ``` ## 4.3 UART Frame Structure (Per Byte Transmitted) ```mermaid flowchart LR n1["IDLE HIGH"] -->|Falling Edge| n2["START 0"] n2 --> n3["D0 LSB"] n3 --> n4["D1"] n4 --> n5["D2"] n5 --> n6["D3"] n6 --> n7["D4"] n7 --> n8["D5"] n8 --> n9["D6"] n9 --> n10["D7 MSB"] n10 --> n11["STOP 1"] n11 --> n12["IDLE HIGH"] ``` ## 4.4 State Machine of the HC-05 ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Unpaired Unpaired --> ATMode : EN HIGH at power on Unpaired --> DataMode : EN LOW at power on ATMode --> DataMode : AT+RESET DataMode --> Connected : Phone pairs and opens SPP Connected --> DataMode : Phone disconnects DataMode --> Unpaired : Power cycle ``` ## 4.5 Voltage Divider — Why It Is Mandatory ```mermaid flowchart LR n1["ARD TX 5V"] --> n2["R1 1K"] n2 --> n3["JUNCTION POINT"] n3 --> n4["R2 2K"] n4 --> n5["GND 0V"] n3 --> n6["HC05 RXD 3.33V SAFE"] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]` **List the four key AT commands used to configure an HC-05 Bluetooth module, and state the default pairing PIN.** **Model Answer:** | Command | Purpose | Example Response | |---|---|---| | `AT+NAME=` | Set device name | `+NAME:HC-05` | | `AT+PSWD=` | Set pairing PIN | `+PSWD:1234` | | `AT+UART=,

,` | Set baud, parity, stop | `+UART:9600,0,0` | | `AT+ROLE=<0/1>` | Slave / Master | `+ROLE:0` | **Default PIN = `1234` (sometimes `0000`).** **(3 marks — full credit for naming all 4 commands + stating PIN.)** ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` **Explain why a voltage divider is required when connecting an Arduino TX to the HC-05 RXD pin.** **Model Answer:** The HC-05 operates on **3.3 V CMOS logic**, with $V_{IH,\text{min}} = 0.7 \times 3.3 = 2.31$ V. The Arduino Uno outputs **5 V** on its TX pin, exceeding the HC-05's absolute maximum of **$V_{CC} + 0.3$ V = 3.6 V** and risking latch-up or permanent damage. A divider using **1 kΩ + 2 kΩ** scales the 5 V down to 3.33 V, satisfying the logic-HIGH requirement while protecting the module. The reverse path (HC-05 TXD → Arduino RX) needs no divider because 3.3 V is recognised as a valid HIGH by the 5 V ATmega. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture used to send LM35 temperature data from an Arduino Uno to a smartphone using the HC-05 Bluetooth module. List the hardware connections and the role of the voltage divider. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write the complete Arduino program to: - Read the LM35 sensor on analog pin A0. - Convert the ADC value to temperature in °C. - Send the formatted string `"Temp:XX.X C"` to the smartphone every 1 s via HC-05. - Also accept a character command from the phone: if `'L'` is received, print `"ACK:LED_ON"`; if `'l'`, print `"ACK:LED_OFF"`. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) **Block diagram** (reproduce in record): ``` [LM35 Sensor] --10mV/°C--> [Arduino A0] --ADC 10-bit--> [ATmega computes °C] --> [Arduino D1/TX] --1kΩ+2kΩ divider--> [HC-05 RXD] --> [HC-05 antenna] --2.4 GHz GFSK 10m range--> [Smartphone SPP App] ``` **Hardware Connections Table:** | Signal | From | To | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | VCC | 5 V | HC-05 VCC | Internal 3.3 V LDO | | GND | GND | HC-05 GND | Common ground mandatory | | TX | Arduino D1 | HC-05 RXD (via divider) | 5 V → 3.3 V | | RX | HC-05 TXD | Arduino D0 | Direct, 3.3 V → 5 V safe | **Voltage Divider Role:** Protects HC-05's 3.3 V input from 5 V Arduino logic using $R_1 = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 2\text{ k}\Omega$ to produce $3.33$ V. **(7 marks — split 2 for diagram, 2 for table, 1 for divider derivation, 2 for explanation.)** #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) ```cpp #include #define BT_TX 3 #define BT_RX 2 #define LM35 A0 SoftwareSerial BT(BT_RX, BT_TX); void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); BT.begin(9600); pinMode(LM35_PIN, INPUT); } void loop() { int adc = analogRead(LM35); float v = adc * (5.0 / 1023.0); float tC = v * 100.0; BT.print("Temp:"); BT.print(tC, 1); BT.println(" C"); if (BT.available()) { char c = BT.read(); if (c == 'L') BT.println("ACK:LED_ON"); else if (c == 'l') BT.println("ACK:LED_OFF"); } delay(1000); } ``` **Explanation of key steps (for record):** - `analogRead` returns $0\ldots1023$ for $0\ldots5$ V (Equation 7 in cheat sheet). - Voltage conversion: $V = \text{ADC} \times 4.88$ mV. - Temperature: $T = V \times 100$ since LM35 gives 10 mV/°C. - `BT.print` and `BT.println` use **SoftwareSerial** on pins 2/3 so the hardware UART (D0/D1) remains free for the PC debug monitor. - **Bidirectional flow** is achieved by polling `BT.available()` each loop iteration. **(7 marks — split 2 for ADC logic, 2 for send logic, 2 for receive/command logic, 1 for formatting.)** --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Compare the HC-05 and HC-06 Bluetooth modules. State which one supports AT command mode while a connection is active, and justify. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design and write the Arduino program for a wireless weather station that reads **DHT11 humidity and temperature**, formats the data as `"H:55%,T:28C"`, and transmits it to a smartphone using HC-05. Include command handling to toggle an onboard LED on receiving `"LED\n"`. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) | Parameter | HC-05 | HC-06 | |---|---|---| | Mode | Master **or** Slave (configurable) | Slave only (factory fixed) | | AT mode while connected | **Yes** (`AT+NOTI=1`) | No | | AT default baud | **38400** | 9600 | | Button / Key pin | **Yes (EN)** | None | | LED blink patterns | Complex (state indicator) | Simple (pairing only) | | Cost | Slightly higher | Cheaper | **Justification:** The HC-05 has a dedicated **EN (Key)** pin and a richer command set, allowing the user to query parameters *even after* a live SPP connection. The HC-06 enters a transparent data mode immediately after pairing, and any incoming byte is forwarded as data, not interpreted as a command. For laboratory use, **HC-05 is preferred**. **(7 marks — 3 for table, 2 for AT-while-connected answer, 2 for justification.)** #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) **Hardware:** DHT11 DATA pin → Arduino **D2**; DHT VCC → 5 V with 10 kΩ pull-up; DHT GND → GND. HC-05 connected as before. ```cpp #include #include #define DHTPIN 2 #define DHTTYPE DHT11 #define BT_TX 3 #define BT_RX 4 SoftwareSerial BT(BT_RX, BT_TX); DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE); String ledCmd = ""; void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); BT.begin(9600); dht.begin(); pinMode(LED_BUILTIN, OUTPUT); BT.println("Weather Station Booted"); } void loop() { float h = dht.readHumidity(); float t = dht.readTemperature(); if (isnan(h) || isnan(t)) { BT.println("Sensor Error"); } else { BT.print("H:"); BT.print(h, 0); BT.print("%,T:"); BT.print(t, 0); BT.println("C"); } if (BT.available()) ledCmd += (char)BT.read(); if (ledCmd.endsWith("LED\n")) { digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, !digitalRead(LED_BUILTIN)); BT.println("ACK:LED_TOGGLED"); ledCmd = ""; } delay(2000); } ``` **Design rationale:** - **DHT11** is single-wire, 2 s minimum sampling interval — hence `delay(2000)`. - **String accumulator** for the command catches packets split across loop iterations (Bluetooth may deliver bytes in chunks). - `isnan()` check prevents printing the literal "nan" if the sensor fails. - Bidirectional LED control demonstrates the **closed-loop IoT** pattern. **(7 marks — 2 for DHT init, 2 for transmission format, 2 for command handling, 1 for error handling.)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 reasons students LOSE marks in this experiment:** > 1. **Forgetting the voltage divider** on Arduino TX → HC-05 RX line. (−2 marks) > 2. **Wrong baud rate** (using 115200 while module is at 9600). (−2 marks) > 3. **Not removing the HC-05** before uploading the sketch (causes `avrdude: stk500_recv(): programmer is not responding`). (−1 mark) > 4. **Confusing HC-05 and HC-06** in theory questions. (−2 marks) > 5. **Sending raw `float` bytes** instead of ASCII text. Bluetooth SPP is **byte-oriented** but the terminal app expects printable ASCII; sending raw binary yields gibberish. (−1 mark) --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️ - Bluetooth = **IEEE 802.15.1**, **2.4 GHz**, GFSK, **1600 hops/s**, **10 m range (Class 2)**. - HC-05 supports **master/slave** roles; HC-06 is **slave only**. - Default HC-05 baud: **9600, 8N1** in data mode; **38400, 8N1** in AT mode. - **Voltage divider** (1 kΩ + 2 kΩ) is **mandatory** on Arduino TX → HC-05 RXD line. - **SPP (Serial Port Profile) UUID = 0x1101** is what the terminal app opens. - LM35 sensitivity: **10 mV/°C**; Arduino ADC step: **$5\text{ V}/1023 = 4.88\text{ mV}$**. - Bluetooth TX time for $N$ bytes: $T = N \cdot 10 / 9600$ seconds. - Pairing PIN: **1234** or **0000**. - Use **SoftwareSerial** on D2/D3 (or D10/D11) so the hardware UART stays free for PC debugging. - Disconnect the HC-05 (or use a switch) before **uploading** sketches to avoid bootloader conflict. - The HC-05 **EN pin HIGH at power-on** = AT command mode; LOW = data mode. - Frequency hopping and **adaptive frequency hopping (AFH)** avoid interference with Wi-Fi. - A typical packet `"T:28.7C\n"` is **7 bytes** → transmit time ≈ **7.29 ms** at 9600 bps. - For KTU viva: be ready to derive $V_{\text{out}} = \text{ADC} \times 4.88$ mV and $T = V \times 100$ on the spot. - Always use `\n` or `\r\n` as a **delimiter** so the receiver can synchronise to packet boundaries. ---

topics.title: Interface Bluetooth with Arduino/Raspberry Pi and write a program to turn LED ON/OFF when 1/0 is received from smartphone using Bluetooth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interface Bluetooth with Arduino/Raspberry Pi and write a program to turn LED ON/OFF when 1/0 is received from smartphone using Bluetooth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bluetooth-Based LED Control System**: A wireless embedded application in which a **Serial Port Profile (SPP)** Bluetooth link is established between a smartphone (master/central) and a microcontroller development board such as **Arduino Uno** (slave/peripheral), and a character-based command protocol (`'1'` / `'0'`) is transmitted over **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver–Transmitter)** to drive a digital output pin HIGH or LOW, thereby switching a Light Emitting Diode (LED) ON or OFF.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory context (PCCPL408), this experiment evaluates the learner's ability to:
1. Configure an **HC-05 / HC-06** Bluetooth Serial Module.
2. Implement **UART communication** at standard baud rates (default **9600 bps**).
3. Map ASCII characters to GPIO actuation logic.
4. Validate the IoT-stack layer: **App Layer → Transport (RFCOMM) → Physical (2.4 GHz Radio)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Walkie-Talkie Mailroom"**
> Imagine a corporate office where employees (your smartphone) speak into a walkie-talkie (Bluetooth radio). A receptionist at the front desk (the **HC-05 module**) listens on a fixed channel. The receptionist then walks a paper note to the manager's cabin (the **Arduino Uno**), who reads either "LIGHTS ON" or "LIGHTS OFF" and physically flips the switch. The walkie-talkie is the *wireless link*, the receptionist is the *serial bridge*, and the manager is the *decision-making microcontroller*. No internet, no Wi-Fi password — just a short-range, low-power, point-to-point radio chat.

## 1.3 System-Level Block Identity

| Subsystem | Physical Entity | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| Transmitter | Smartphone + Bluetooth Terminal App | Generates & transmits ASCII payload |
| Wireless Link | 2.4 GHz ISM Band (Channel hopping) | Carries SPP/RFCOMM frames |
| Receiver (RF Front-End) | HC-05 / HC-06 Bluetooth Module | Demodulates & exposes UART TX/RX |
| Controller | Arduino Uno (ATmega328P) | Parses character & drives GPIO |
| Actuator | LED + Current-Limiting Resistor | Visual indicator of state |

## 1.4 Key Hardware Constants & Standards

- **Operating Frequency:** **2.4 GHz** ISM Band (2402 – 2480 MHz)
- **Standard Baud Rate:** **9600 bps**, 8N1 (8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit)
- **HC-05 VCC:** **3.3 V – 5 V** (logic TX pin is **3.3 V tolerant**; Arduino RX is 5 V)
- **Typical TX Current:** **~40 mA** during connection
- **Default HC-05 Slave Password:** **1234** (or 0000)
- **Bluetooth Class:** Class 2 — Range ≈ **10 metres**
- **LED Forward Voltage (V_f):** ~**2.0 V** (Red), ~**3.2 V** (Blue/White)
- **Arduino Digital Pin Current Limit:** **40 mA absolute maximum per pin** (recommended ≤ **20 mA**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Bluetooth Protocol Stack — The 5 Logical Layers

Bluetooth communication follows a layered architecture. Understanding these layers is essential for the viva voce.

| Layer | Function | Lab-Level Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Radio (PHY)** | 2.4 GHz GFSK modulation, 79 channels, 1 MHz spacing | Frequency-hopping spread spectrum |
| **Baseband** | Packet assembly, error correction (FEC) | Forms the asynchronous ACL packets |
| **LMP (Link Manager Protocol)** | Pairing, authentication, link supervision | Manages PIN/password `1234` |
| **HCI (Host Controller Interface)** | Command interface to BT module | Not user-accessible in HC-05 |
| **L2CAP / RFCOMM** | Logical channel multiplexing; **RS-232 emulation** | The layer your Arduino's UART speaks to |
| **SPP (Serial Port Profile)** | Provides transparent byte stream | The "COM port" over Bluetooth |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SPP is the key profile** used in this experiment. It emulates a wired RS-232 serial cable, which is why we can use `Serial.read()` and `Serial.write()` in Arduino.

## 2.2 HC-05 vs HC-06 — Critical Distinction

| Parameter | HC-05 | HC-06 |
|---|---|---|
| Mode | **Master OR Slave** (selectable) | **Slave only** (factory default) |
| AT Mode Entry | Press onboard button while powering | Fixed baud 9600, no button needed |
| Pin Count | 6 pins (EN, STATE, RXD, TXD, GND, VCC) | 4 pins (VCC, GND, TXD, RXD) |
| Typical Lab Use | Preferred (configurable roles) | Common in low-cost kits |

> [!NOTE]
> For this experiment, the module operates as a **slave** that waits for the smartphone to discover and pair with it.

## 2.3 AT Command Set (For Viva & Configuration)

| Command | Response | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `AT` | `OK` | Test communication |
| `AT+NAME=KTU_BT` | `OK` | Set Bluetooth broadcast name |
| `AT+PSWD=1234` | `OK` | Set pairing PIN |
| `AT+UART=9600,0,0` | `OK` | Set baud rate, stop bit, parity |
| `AT+ROLE=0` | `OK` | Set slave role (HC-05 only) |
| `AT+ADDR?` | Returns MAC address | Useful for unique identification |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{CC} = I_{LED} \cdot R + V_f$ | Kirchhoff's Voltage Law across LED branch | V |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_{LED}}$ | Current-limiting resistor calculation | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $f_{channel} = 2402 + n \text{ MHz}, \quad n \in [0, 78]$ | Bluetooth AFH channel centre frequency | MHz |
| 4 | $T_{hop} = 625 \,\mu s$ | Slot duration for frequency hopping | s |
| 5 | $R_{max} = \dfrac{1600}{h}$ | Hopping rate (hops per second) | hops/s |
| 6 | $N_{frame} = 8 + 1_{parity} + 1_{stop} = 10 \text{ bits/byte}$ | UART frame size at 8N1 | bits |
| 7 | $t_{byte} = \dfrac{10}{B} = \dfrac{10}{9600} \approx 1.04 \text{ ms}$ | Time per byte at 9600 baud | s |
| 8 | $P_{TX_{HC05}} \approx +4 \text{ dBm}$ | Transmit power (Class 2) | dBm |
| 9 | $V_{OH_{Arduino}} = 4.2 \text{ V min}$ | HIGH level output voltage | V |
| 10 | $V_{IH_{HC05}} = 2.0 \text{ V min}$ | HC-05 RX HIGH threshold | V |

## 2.5 Working Principle — "How It Works"

1. **Power-Up & Boot**: Arduino is powered via USB. HC-05 enters slave mode and its red LED blinks rapidly (≈ 2 Hz), signalling *discoverable state*.
2. **Pairing**: User opens a Bluetooth Terminal app (e.g., *Serial Bluetooth Terminal*, *Arduino Bluetooth Controller*, *MIT App Inventor* custom app). Smartphone scans → discovers `HC-05` → enters PIN `1234` → pairing established.
3. **Connection Finalisation**: HC-05 LED now blinks **twice per cycle** (slow, ~1 Hz) indicating an active ACL link with the phone.
4. **Virtual COM Port Creation**: Android/iOS creates a virtual serial endpoint bound to the RFCOMM channel.
5. **User Input**: User taps a button in the app, which sends an ASCII string, e.g., `1\n` or `0\n`.
6. **Over-the-Air Transmission**: The smartphone's BT radio GFSK-modulates the data onto the 2.4 GHz carrier, frequency-hopping every 625 µs.
7. **HC-05 Demodulation**: HC-05's baseband processor reassembles the RFCOMM payload and pushes the raw bytes onto its **UART TX pin** at **9600 baud**.
8. **Arduino Reception**: The ATmega328P's hardware USART receives the byte in its receive buffer; `Serial.available()` returns ≥ 1.
9. **Decision Logic**: `Serial.read()` retrieves the character. An `if–else` ladder compares it to ASCII `'1'` (decimal 49) or `'0'` (decimal 48).
10. **Actuation**: If `'1'` → `digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH)` → LED glows. If `'0'` → `digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW)` → LED is off.
11. **Echo Back (Optional)**: Arduino can `Serial.println("LED ON")` back, which HC-05 relays to the phone, displaying confirmation.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

This fundamental pattern is the **building block** of:
- **Home automation** (smart bulbs, fan regulators)
- **Industrial HMIs** (Human-Machine Interfaces)
- **Wearable health monitors**
- **Wireless robotics** (low-cost RC vehicles)
- **Medical assistive devices** for the differently-abled
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Current-Limiting Resistor Value for LED

**Given:** $V_{CC} = +5\text{ V}$ (Arduino digital HIGH), $V_f = 2.0\text{ V}$ (red LED), desired $I_{LED} = 15\text{ mA}$ (safe operating current).

**Step 1.** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the LED branch:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{CC} - I_{LED} \cdot R - V_f &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow I_{LED} \cdot R &= V_{CC} - V_f \\
\Rightarrow R &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_{LED}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2.** Substitute the numerical values:

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{5\text{ V} - 2.0\text{ V}}{15 \times 10^{-3}\text{ A}} \\
&= \frac{3.0\text{ V}}{0.015\text{ A}} \\
&= 200\;\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Choose the **next-higher standard E12 value**: $R = 220\;\Omega$ (the canonical Arduino "blinky" resistor).

**Step 4.** Verify the actual current with $R = 220\;\Omega$:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{LED_{actual}} &= \frac{5.0 - 2.0}{220} \\
&= \frac{3.0}{220} \\
&\approx 13.6\text{ mA} \;\; (< 20\text{ mA safe limit, OK})
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A $220\;\Omega$ resistor is the **de-facto industry default** for indicator LEDs on Arduino boards. It keeps the current well below the **40 mA absolute maximum** of the ATmega328P GPIO pin.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — UART Time-Per-Byte at 9600 Baud

**Given:** Standard UART configuration 8N1 — 8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit, 1 start bit.

**Step 1.** Total number of bits per transmitted byte:

$$N_{total} = N_{start} + N_{data} + N_{parity} + N_{stop} = 1 + 8 + 0 + 1 = 10 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2.** Time per bit at baud rate $B = 9600$:

$$t_{bit} = \frac{1}{B} = \frac{1}{9600} \approx 104.17\;\mu s$$

**Step 3.** Time per full byte frame:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{byte} &= N_{total} \times t_{bit} \\
&= 10 \times 104.17\;\mu s \\
&\approx 1041.7\;\mu s \\
&\approx 1.04\text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** Implication: when the user types `1` and presses send, the entire byte `0x31` takes ~1.04 ms to traverse the UART link. The Bluetooth RF transmission itself adds roughly 1–3 ms latency, giving end-to-end command latency under **5 ms** — well below human perception threshold (~100 ms).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bluetooth Hop Frequency Set

**Given:** Bluetooth operates in the ISM band from 2402 MHz to 2480 MHz with 79 RF channels spaced 1 MHz apart.

**Step 1.** Centre frequency of channel $n$:

$$f_n = 2402 + n \text{ MHz}, \quad n \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 78\}$$

**Step 2.** Verify the upper bound:

$$f_{78} = 2402 + 78 = 2480 \text{ MHz} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 3.** Hopping sequence in the master/slave pair is dictated by a pseudo-random function of the master's Bluetooth clock. The device "dances" through 79 channels at 1600 hops/second.

**Step 4.** Hop duration:

$$t_{hop} = \frac{1}{1600} = 625\;\mu s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This rapid frequency hopping is what gives Bluetooth its **robustness against 2.4 GHz interference** (Wi-Fi, microwave ovens). It is one of the most-asked viva questions on this experiment.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — HC-05 Logic-Level Compatibility

**The Problem:** Arduino Uno digital pins output **5 V** in HIGH state. The HC-05's RXD pin is rated for **3.3 V maximum**.

**Step 1.** Identify the safety gap:

$$\Delta V = V_{OH_{Arduino}} - V_{IH_{HC05}} = 5.0\text{ V} - 3.0\text{ V (typical max)} = 2.0\text{ V excess}$$

**Step 2.** Three possible mitigation strategies:

| Strategy | Implementation | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Voltage Divider** | Two resistors (1 kΩ + 2 kΩ) on Arduino TX → HC-05 RX | Cheap, lossy, no power consumption |
| **Series Resistor + Zener** | 5.1 V Zener + 100 Ω resistor to clamp | Slightly bulky, very reliable |
| **Bidirectional Logic Level Converter** | Dedicated MOSFET-based module | Clean, professional, adds cost |

> [!WARNING]
> In many introductory kits, students wire Arduino TX → HC-05 RX **directly without a level shifter** and the module survives. However, KTU examiners expect the candidate to **know the correct engineering practice** and mention the voltage mismatch in viva.

## 3.5 Program Flow — Annotated Arduino Sketch

Below is the **complete, KTU-viva-ready** Arduino program with line-by-line logical commentary.

```cpp
//======================================================
//  EXPERIMENT: Bluetooth-Controlled LED via HC-05
//  Course    : PCCPL408 - MC & IoT Lab (KTU 2024)
//  Board     : Arduino Uno (ATmega328P, 16 MHz)
//  Module    : HC-05 Bluetooth Serial Module
//  Profile   : SPP over RFCOMM, Baud 9600 8N1
//======================================================

#define LED_PIN 13              // Onboard LED (D13) — also externally wired LED

char incomingByte;              // Variable to store received ASCII char

void setup() {
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);     // Configure D13 as digital output
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);   // Ensure LED starts OFF
  Serial.begin(9600);           // Initialize UART at 9600 bps (8N1 default)
                                // This matches HC-05's default baud rate
}

void loop() {
  if (Serial.available() > 0) {        // Check if ≥1 byte in RX buffer
    incomingByte = Serial.read();       // Read 1 byte from UART buffer

    if (incomingByte == '1') {          // Compare to ASCII '1' (decimal 49)
      digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH);      // Drive pin HIGH → LED ON
      Serial.println("LED ON");         // Echo confirmation to phone
    }
    else if (incomingByte == '0') {     // Compare to ASCII '0' (decimal 48)
      digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);       // Drive pin LOW → LED OFF
      Serial.println("LED OFF");        // Echo confirmation to phone
    }
    else {
      Serial.print("Invalid: ");        // Unknown command feedback
      Serial.println(incomingByte);
    }
  }
}
```

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Pin-to-Pin Connection Table

| Arduino Uno Pin | HC-05 Pin | Direction (Logical) | Purpose | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `5V` | `VCC` | Power → Module | Powers HC-05 | Use 5 V (HC-05 has onboard LDO) |
| `GND` | `GND` | Common | Common ground | Mandatory reference |
| `D0` (RX) | `TXD` | Module → Arduino | BT data into MCU | Hardware UART RX |
| `D1` (TX) | `RXD` | Arduino → Module | MCU data out to BT | Use level shifter ideally |
| `D13` | LED Anode | MCU → LED | LED control | $220\;\Omega$ → LED → GND |
| *(not used)* | `EN` / `STATE` | — | Mode entry / status | Leave floating for normal op |

## 3.7 Full Hardware Connection List (Bill of Materials)

| # | Component | Quantity | Specification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arduino Uno R3 | 1 | ATmega328P, 16 MHz |
| 2 | HC-05 Bluetooth Module | 1 | Slave mode, SPP |
| 3 | LED (5 mm Red) | 1 | $V_f = 2.0$ V, $I_f = 20$ mA |
| 4 | Resistor | 1 | $220\;\Omega$, ¼ W |
| 5 | Breadboard + Jumper Wires | As required | Male-to-male |
| 6 | Smartphone with BT Terminal App | 1 | Android/iOS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Phone["Smartphone with BT Terminal App"]
    RFLink["2.4 GHz Radio Link SPP over RFCOMM"]
    HC05["HC-05 Bluetooth Module UART 9600 8N1"]
    Arduino["Arduino Uno ATmega328P"]
    LED["LED with 220 ohm Resistor"]

    Phone -- "ASCII 1 or 0 + newline" --> RFLink
    RFLink -- "Demodulated UART bytes" --> HC05
    HC05 -- "TX pin to D0 of Arduino" --> Arduino
    Arduino -- "digitalWrite D13 HIGH or LOW" --> LED
    Arduino -. "Echo LED ON or LED OFF" .-> HC05
    HC05 -. "Uplink to phone" .-> Phone
```

## 4.2 Detailed Flowchart — Program Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Power On"]
    B["Setup pinMode 13 OUTPUT"]
    C["Serial.begin 9600"]
    D["Wait for Serial.available greater than 0"]
    E["incomingByte = Serial.read"]
    F{"incomingByte equals 1"}
    G["digitalWrite 13 HIGH"]
    H["Print LED ON to serial"]
    I{"incomingByte equals 0"}
    J["digitalWrite 13 LOW"]
    K["Print LED OFF to serial"]
    L["Print Invalid command"]
    M["Loop back to wait"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F
    F -- "Yes" --> G --> H --> M
    F -- "No" --> I
    I -- "Yes" --> J --> K --> M
    I -- "No" --> L --> M
    M --> D
```

## 4.3 Physical Wiring Schematic (Breadboard View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ARDUINO_UNO
        A1["Arduino 5V pin"]
        A2["Arduino GND pin"]
        A3["Arduino D0 RX pin"]
        A4["Arduino D1 TX pin"]
        A5["Arduino D13 pin"]
    end

    subgraph HC05_MODULE
        B1["HC05 VCC pin"]
        B2["HC05 GND pin"]
        B3["HC05 TXD pin"]
        B4["HC05 RXD pin"]
    end

    subgraph LED_BRANCH
        C1["220 ohm resistor"]
        C2["LED Anode to Resistor"]
        C3["LED Cathode to GND"]
    end

    A1 -- "red wire 5V" --> B1
    A2 -- "black wire GND" --> B2
    B3 -- "data into Arduino RX" --> A3
    A4 -- "data out of Arduino TX with optional level shifter" --> B4
    A5 -- "drives" --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 -- "Anode" --> C2N["LED"]
    C2N -- "Cathode" --> C3
    C3 --> A2
```

## 4.4 Communication Timing Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User App
    participant P as Smartphone BT
    participant M as HC05 Module
    participant A as Arduino
    participant L as LED

    U->>P: Tap ON button
    P->>M: GFSK packets carrying ASCII 1
    M->>A: UART byte 0x31 on D0 RX
    A->>A: Serial.read returns 49
    A->>L: digitalWrite D13 HIGH
    A->>M: Serial.println LED ON
    M->>P: Uplink confirmation
    P->>U: Display LED ON text
    L-->>U: Visual glow confirmation
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree for Error Debugging

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["LED not responding"]
    Q2{"HC05 red LED blinking rapidly"}
    Q3{"Paired with phone successfully"}
    Q4{"Serial Monitor shows received chars"}
    Q5{"LED polarity and resistor correct"}

    S1["Check VCC and GND wiring"]
    S2["Pair using PIN 1234"]
    S3["Set both to 9600 baud"]
    S4["Reverse LED or check 220 ohm"]
    S5["Use external LED on D13 and check code pin number"]

    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 -- "No" --> S1
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Q3
    Q3 -- "No" --> S2
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> Q4
    Q4 -- "No" --> S3
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> Q5
    Q5 -- "No" --> S4
    Q5 -- "Yes" --> S5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Serial Port Profile (SPP) in Bluetooth, and why is it relevant to the Bluetooth-LED control experiment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **SPP (Serial Port Profile)** is a Bluetooth profile that emulates an **RS-232 serial cable** over a wireless RFCOMM channel. **(1 mark)**
- It allows transparent byte-stream communication between two paired devices without any application-layer protocol overhead. **(1 mark)**
- In this experiment, the smartphone app and HC-05 use SPP, so we can simply use Arduino's `Serial.read()`/`Serial.write()` to exchange ASCII characters like `'1'` and `'0'`, making it ideal for low-complexity IoT control. **(1 mark)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the pin connections required between Arduino Uno and the HC-05 Bluetooth module for the LED-control experiment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **HC-05 VCC → Arduino 5V**; **HC-05 GND → Arduino GND** (power). **(1 mark)**
- **HC-05 TXD → Arduino D0 (RX)** — HC-05 transmits, Arduino receives. **(1 mark)**
- **HC-05 RXD → Arduino D1 (TX)** — Arduino transmits, HC-05 receives (ideally via a voltage divider/level shifter). **(1 mark)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a Bluetooth-based LED control system using Arduino Uno and HC-05. Explain the role of each block in data flow. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **System Blocks (3 marks):**
   - *Smartphone with BT Terminal App* — generates and transmits ASCII characters.
   - *Bluetooth Radio Link (2.4 GHz)* — carries modulated RFCOMM frames.
   - *HC-05 Module* — acts as the SPP slave; performs RF demodulation and exposes a UART interface.
   - *Arduino Uno* — receives byte via `Serial.read()`, executes decision logic.
   - *LED + Resistor* — output actuator; physical state indicator.

2. **Data Flow Explanation (2 marks):** The user types `1` in the app → smartphone's BT stack packages it into an SPP/RFCOMM frame → GFSK modulation at 2.4 GHz with frequency hopping every 625 µs → HC-05 receives and decodes → byte appears on TXD pin at 9600 baud → Arduino's hardware USART buffers it → `Serial.read()` returns 49 (ASCII `'1'`) → `digitalWrite(13, HIGH)` energises the LED.

3. **Bidirectional Confirmation (2 marks):** Arduino echoes `"LED ON"` back via UART → HC-05 transmits it over the air → phone displays it, completing a closed-loop control.

**(b)** Write the complete Arduino program to:
   (i) Initialize UART at 9600 baud and configure pin 13 as output.
   (ii) Turn LED ON when `'1'` is received and OFF when `'0'` is received from the smartphone.
   (iii) Send a confirmation string back to the smartphone. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```cpp
#define LED 13
char c;

void setup() {
  pinMode(LED, OUTPUT);
  digitalWrite(LED, LOW);
  Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
  if (Serial.available() > 0) {
    c = Serial.read();
    if (c == '1') {
      digitalWrite(LED, HIGH);
      Serial.println("LED ON");
    }
    else if (c == '0') {
      digitalWrite(LED, LOW);
      Serial.println("LED OFF");
    }
    else {
      Serial.println("Invalid Command");
    }
  }
}
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct headers and pin definition — **1 mark**
- `setup()` with `pinMode` and `Serial.begin(9600)` — **2 marks**
- `loop()` with `Serial.available()` check — **1 mark**
- Correct if-else logic for `'1'` and `'0'` — **2 marks**
- `Serial.println` echo statements — **1 mark**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Supplementary]`

**(a)** Explain the differences between HC-05 and HC-06 Bluetooth modules. Justify why HC-05 is generally preferred in lab settings. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Axis | HC-05 | HC-06 |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Mode | **Master or Slave** (selectable via AT command `AT+ROLE`) | **Slave only** (hard-coded) |
| AT Mode Access | Through on-board push button | Only via firmware entry sequence |
| Pin Count | 6 pins (EN, STATE, RX, TX, GND, VCC) | 4 pins (VCC, GND, TX, RX) |
| Configurability | Highly configurable (name, baud, password, role) | Limited to baud and name only |
| Price | Marginally higher | Slightly cheaper |

**Why HC-05 is preferred in labs (2 marks):**
1. Configurable master/slave role allows students to design **point-to-point** as well as **multi-node** networks (one master querying multiple slave sensors).
2. AT command accessibility simplifies baud-rate experiments and PIN changes during viva.
3. The STATE pin enables *connection status* monitoring — useful in advanced labs.

**(b)** Calculate the value of the current-limiting resistor required for a red LED ($V_f = 2.0\text{ V}$) connected to Arduino pin 9 (5 V output), assuming a desired LED current of 12 mA. If only standard E12 values are available, what resistor would you use and why? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1.** KVL around the LED branch:

$$V_{CC} = I_{LED} \cdot R + V_f$$

**Step 2.** Solve for $R$:

$$R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_{LED}} = \frac{5.0 - 2.0}{12 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3.0}{0.012} = 250\;\Omega$$

**Step 3.** Standard E12 values: 220 Ω, 270 Ω, 330 Ω, …

**Step 4.** Choose **$R = 220\;\Omega$** (next lower value) — it gives slightly higher current:

$$I_{actual} = \frac{3.0}{220} \approx 13.6\text{ mA}$$

This is still well within the safe 20 mA limit and provides **brighter illumination**.

**Step 5.** Justification (1 mark): E12 series tolerance is ±10%, so $220\;\Omega$ is acceptable; $270\;\Omega$ could also be used if a dimmer LED is preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `newline` character** — Many terminals send `'1\n'`. The `'\n'` may be treated as an "invalid" character and clutter the output. Use a proper character check or strip newlines.
> 2. **Wrong baud rate mismatch** — If HC-05 is set to 38400 (default for some variants) but Arduino uses 9600, **garbage characters** appear. Always verify with `AT+UART?` command.
> 3. **Not adding a series resistor to the LED** — Connecting an LED directly will draw excessive current (>40 mA) and may **damage the ATmega328P pin permanently**. Examiners explicitly test for this awareness.
> 4. **Confusing TXD and RXD** — TX of one device goes to RX of the other. Cross-connection is the **#1 reason** for "pairing works, no data" failures.
> 5. **Powering HC-05 from 3.3 V pin instead of 5 V** — Some clones are unstable at 3.3 V. Use 5 V for reliability unless the datasheet specifies otherwise.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Profile used:** Serial Port Profile (SPP) over RFCOMM.
- **Default baud rate:** **9600 bps, 8N1** (must match on both ends).
- **Default HC-05 PIN:** **1234**.
- **Frequency band:** **2.4 GHz ISM**, 79 channels, **1600 hops/s**, slot = **625 µs**.
- **Voltage divider requirement:** Arduino TX (5 V) → HC-05 RX (3.3 V max) — use **1 kΩ + 2 kΩ** divider for safety.
- **Current-limiting resistor for LED:** **$220\;\Omega$** is the canonical safe value; formula $R = (V_{CC} - V_f) / I_{LED}$.
- **Arduino functions used:** `pinMode()`, `digitalWrite()`, `Serial.begin()`, `Serial.available()`, `Serial.read()`, `Serial.println()`.
- **Comparison: HC-05 vs HC-06** — HC-05 supports master mode and is more configurable.
- **Common KTU viva questions:**
  1. Why is the LED resistor necessary? (To limit current and protect the GPIO pin.)
  2. What is GFSK? (Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying — the modulation scheme of Bluetooth.)
  3. What is the range of Class-2 Bluetooth? (≈ **10 m**.)
  4. What is the role of RFCOMM? (It emulates RS-232 serial over Bluetooth.)
  5. What happens if we send `'A'` from the app? (The Arduino takes the "else" branch and prints "Invalid Command".)
- **The 1-bit start, 8-bit data, 1-bit stop frame** = 10 bits per UART byte = **~1.04 ms/byte at 9600 baud**.
- **Pin safety:** Do not exceed **40 mA** per Arduino pin or **200 mA** total across all pins.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a program on Arduino/Raspberry Pi to upload temperature and humidity data to Thing speak cloud
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a program on Arduino/Raspberry Pi to upload temperature and humidity data to Thing speak cloud

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Internet of Things (IoT)** is a paradigm that interconnects physical devices embedded with sensors, software, and communication technologies over the Internet, enabling them to collect, exchange, and act upon data without requiring direct human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ThingSpeak™** is an open-source **Internet of Things (IoT) platform** and **MATLAB®-driven analytics service** hosted in the cloud. It allows the aggregation, visualization, and analysis of live data streams in real-time. The platform provides RESTful and MQTT APIs for devices to publish sensor readings to user-defined channels.

The lab experiment requires the student to interface a **DHT series temperature & humidity sensor** (DHT11 or DHT22) with an **Arduino UNO / ESP8266** or **Raspberry Pi** board, sample environmental data at periodic intervals, and push these values to a ThingSpeak channel via an **HTTP GET request** using the channel's unique **Write API Key**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The "Digital Postman" System**
> 
> Imagine a small weather station installed on your apartment balcony.
> - The **DHT Sensor** is the *thermometer and hygrometer* that measures the surroundings.
> - The **Microcontroller (Arduino/Raspberry Pi)** is the *postman* who reads the readings.
> - The **Wi-Fi Module (ESP8266 / ESP32 / built-in Pi Wi-Fi)** is the *letterbox* connected to the Internet.
> - **ThingSpeak Cloud** is the *central post office* in a faraway city where every reading is filed chronologically.
> - The **Channel Dashboard** is the *public notice board* where the readings are displayed as live graphs.
> 
> Every few minutes, the postman takes a new measurement, writes it on a postcard (the **API request**), drops it into the letterbox, and within seconds the postcard is delivered to the post office, where the new temperature and humidity are pinned onto the board.

## 1.3 Hardware & Software Vocabulary

| Component | Function | Standard Value / Spec |
|---|---|---|
| DHT11 | Temperature & Humidity Sensor | **0–50 °C** (±2 °C), **20–90 % RH** (±5 %) |
| DHT22 (AM2302) | Higher accuracy variant | **−40 to 80 °C** (±0.5 °C), **0–100 % RH** (±2 %) |
| Arduino UNO | ATmega328P Microcontroller Board | 16 MHz, 14 digital I/O, 6 analog |
| ESP8266 / ESP32 | Wi-Fi enabled MCU with TCP/IP stack | 2.4 GHz, 802.11 b/g/n |
| Raspberry Pi | SBC running Linux | 40 GPIO, built-in Wi-Fi/BLE |
| ThingSpeak | Cloud IoT platform (MathWorks) | Free tier: 1 channel / 3 fields / 8 sec min interval |

## 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU 2024

> [!TIP]
> This experiment is a **CO-mapped lab outcome** under PCCPL408. It directly verifies the student's ability to:
> - Integrate **analog/digital sensors** with embedded platforms.
> - Use **serial protocols (single-wire)** for DHT communication.
> - Apply **HTTP over TCP/IP** for cloud communication.
> - Build a complete **sense → process → communicate** IoT pipeline, which is the foundational template for almost every Industry 4.0 / Smart-City application.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture – Layered View

The data flow is divided into **four clearly separated layers**, each with a strict responsibility:

**Layer 1 – Perception Layer (Sensing)**
- The DHT sensor uses a **single-wire bidirectional protocol**.
- A dedicated **NTC thermistor** measures temperature, while a **capacitive polymer** measures relative humidity.
- The sensor internally converts analog measurements into a **40-bit digital frame** (8-bit humidity integer, 8-bit humidity decimal, 8-bit temperature integer, 8-bit temperature decimal, 8-bit checksum).

**Layer 2 – Network Layer (Microcontroller)**
- The Arduino/Raspberry Pi initializes the DHT library, triggers a measurement, and waits for the digital packet.
- Library used: `DHT.h` (Adafruit) for Arduino, `Adafruit_DHT` (Python/C) for Raspberry Pi.
- The MCU then formats the values into a **URL-encoded HTTP GET request**.

**Layer 3 – Transport Layer (Wi-Fi / TCP)**
- ESP8266/ESP32 or Raspberry Pi opens a TCP socket on **port 80** (HTTP).
- The HTTP GET request is wrapped inside a TCP segment with **SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK** three-way handshake, then transmitted.

**Layer 4 – Application Layer (ThingSpeak Cloud)**
- ThingSpeak's REST endpoint receives the GET request at:
  - `https://api.thingspeak.com/update`
- The request is parsed, the **Write API Key** is validated, and the field values are stored in a **time-series database** (TSDB).
- The dashboard refreshes in real-time.

## 2.2 The Core Communication Protocol – HTTP GET Structure

A typical ThingSpeak update request follows this pattern:

```text
GET /update?api_key=XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX&field1=27.50&field2=64.00
Host: api.thingspeak.com
Connection: close
```

The server responds with:
- `HTTP/1.1 200 OK` followed by the **entry ID** of the stored data point (e.g., `7421`).
- A non-zero entry ID confirms successful upload.
- An entry ID of `0` indicates that the request was rate-limited (ThingSpeak enforces a **minimum 15-second interval** between updates on the free tier).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **only** equations needed for the viva and theory questions on this experiment. Memorize them.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{F} = (T_{C} \times \frac{9}{5}) + 32$ | Celsius to Fahrenheit | T in °C, °F |
| 2 | $RH = \frac{p_{w}}{p_{ws}(T)} \times 100\%$ | Definition of Relative Humidity | % |
| 3 | $\theta_{DHT} = 5 \times 10^{3}\ \text{ms}$ | Minimum interval between two DHT reads | ms |
| 4 | $f_{sample} = \frac{1}{T_{int}} \leq \frac{1}{15\ s}$ | Maximum sampling rate to obey ThingSpeak's free-tier limit | Hz |
| 5 | $Frame_{DHT} = 8 + 8 + 8 + 8 + 8 = 40\ \text{bits}$ | Sensor packet size | bits |
| 6 | $C_{sum} = \sum_{i=1}^{4}B_{i}\ \text{mod}\ 256$ | Checksum validation | last 8 bits must equal this |
| 7 | $URL_{TS} = \text{https://api.thingspeak.com/update?api\_key=K\&field1=T\&field2=H}$ | ThingSpeak update endpoint | URL-encoded |
| 8 | $\Delta t_{min} = 15\ \text{s}$ (Free), **1 s** (Paid) | ThingSpeak minimum update interval | seconds |

## 2.4 The DHT Single-Wire Protocol (Why It Works)

1. **MCU pulls line LOW for ≥ 18 ms** → start signal.
2. **MCU releases line** (pull-up pulls it HIGH) for 20–40 µs.
3. **Sensor responds with 80 µs LOW + 80 µs HIGH** → response signal.
4. **40 bits are transmitted**; each bit begins with a 50 µs LOW pulse.
   - A **26–28 µs HIGH** pulse = logical `0`
   - A **70 µs HIGH** pulse = logical `1`
5. End of frame → line goes HIGH and stays idle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** This timing-based encoding means the line is **noise-sensitive**. Always use a **10 kΩ pull-up resistor** between DATA and VCC for reliable operation over cable lengths > 20 cm.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Application Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Smart Agriculture** | Greenhouse monitoring, soil-climate correlation |
| **Cold-Chain Logistics** | Vaccine / food transport temperature audit |
| **Smart Homes** | HVAC auto-control based on humidity index |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Server-room / clean-room environmental alarms |
| **Weather Stations** | Low-cost DIY meteorological data loggers |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Hardware Wiring Diagram (Reference)

```text
   DHT11 / DHT22
   ┌───────────┐
   │  VCC  ●──┼──────── 5V  (or 3.3V for ESP)
   │  DATA ●──┼── 10 kΩ ── 5V  (pull-up)
   │        │ │
   │        └─┴──────── D2  (Arduino digital pin)
   │  GND  ●──┼──────── GND
   │  NC   ●  │
   └───────────┘
```

## 3.2 Full Arduino Sketch (ESP8266 / ESP32 + DHT11)

```cpp
// =====================================================
// KTU PCCPL408 - Module Experiment 2
// Upload DHT11 Temperature & Humidity to ThingSpeak
// Target: ESP8266 NodeMCU / ESP32
// =====================================================

#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>        // For ESP32: #include <WiFi.h>
#include <DHT.h>

#define DHTPIN   D4             // GPIO2 on NodeMCU
#define DHTTYPE  DHT11
DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);

// -------- Wi-Fi credentials --------
const char* ssid     = "Your_WiFi_Name";
const char* password = "Your_WiFi_Password";

// -------- ThingSpeak credentials --------
const char* apiKey   = "YOUR_THINGSPEAK_WRITE_API_KEY";
const char* server   = "api.thingspeak.com";

unsigned long lastMillis = 0;
const unsigned long interval = 20000;   // 20 s (> 15 s free-tier rule)

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  dht.begin();
  WiFi.begin(ssid, password);
  Serial.print("Connecting to Wi-Fi");
  while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) {
    delay(500);
    Serial.print(".");
  }
  Serial.println("\nWi-Fi connected. IP: " + WiFi.localIP().toString());
}

void loop() {
  if (millis() - lastMillis >= interval) {
    lastMillis = millis();

    // -------- Step 1: Read sensor --------
    float h = dht.readHumidity();
    float t = dht.readTemperature();   // °C
    if (isnan(h) || isnan(t)) {
      Serial.println("DHT read failed!");
      return;
    }

    // -------- Step 2: Optional Fahrenheit --------
    float f = (t * 9.0 / 5.0) + 32.0;

    Serial.printf("T=%.2f C  H=%.2f %%\n", t, h);

    // -------- Step 3: Build HTTP GET --------
    WiFiClient client;
    if (client.connect(server, 80)) {
      String url = "/update?api_key=" + String(apiKey)
                 + "&field1=" + String(t, 2)
                 + "&field2=" + String(h, 2);

      client.print(String("GET ") + url + " HTTP/1.1\r\n"
                 + "Host: " + server + "\r\n"
                 + "Connection: close\r\n\r\n");

      // -------- Step 4: Read server response --------
      while (client.connected() || client.available()) {
        if (client.available()) {
          String line = client.readStringUntil('\n');
          if (line.startsWith("HTTP/")) {
            int code = line.substring(9, 12).toInt();
            Serial.println("HTTP status: " + String(code));
          }
        }
      }
      client.stop();
    } else {
      Serial.println("Connection to ThingSpeak failed.");
    }
  }
}
```

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Code-to-Concept Mapping

| Code Block | Implements | Maps to KTU CO | KTU Cognitive Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| `#include <DHT.h>` + `dht.begin()` | Single-wire protocol init | CO1: Hardware interfacing | Remember / Understand |
| `dht.readHumidity()` | 40-bit frame decode, checksum verify | CO2: Sensor programming | Apply |
| `WiFi.begin(ssid, password)` | DHCP + 802.11 association | CO3: Network programming | Apply |
| `client.connect(server, 80)` | TCP 3-way handshake (SYN) | CO3: Network programming | Analyze |
| `String url = "/update?api_key=..."` | HTTP GET construction | CO3: Application layer | Apply |
| `millis() - lastMillis >= interval` | Non-blocking timing | CO2: Embedded C logic | Analyze |
| `client.readStringUntil('\n')` | HTTP response parsing | CO3: Protocol parsing | Analyze |

## 3.4 Mathematical Derivation of Timing Constraint

ThingSpeak free-tier enforces a minimum update window so that a single user cannot exhaust the server's resources. Let the sampling period be $T_{int}$ and the number of writes per unit time be $N$.

$$N = \frac{1}{T_{int}}$$

The constraint imposed by ThingSpeak is:

$$N \leq N_{max} = \frac{1}{15\ \text{s}} = 0.0667\ \text{Hz}$$

Therefore:

$$T_{int} \geq 15\ \text{s}$$

For the experiment we safely choose:

$$T_{int} = 20\ \text{s} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; N = 0.05\ \text{Hz}$$

## 3.5 Checksum Verification Derivation

Let the four 8-bit bytes received from the DHT sensor be $B_{1}, B_{2}, B_{3}, B_{4}$ and the checksum byte be $B_{5}$. The transmitted frame is valid **iff**:

$$B_{5} = (B_{1} + B_{2} + B_{3} + B_{4})\ \text{mod}\ 256$$

The Adafruit `DHT.h` library performs this validation internally and sets the read values to `NaN` if the check fails — that is exactly why our code contains:

```cpp
if (isnan(h) || isnan(t)) { Serial.println("DHT read failed!"); return; }
```

## 3.6 Raspberry Pi (Python) Implementation Sketch

For students using the Raspberry Pi version of the experiment:

```python
# pip install Adafruit_DHT requests
import Adafruit_DHT
import requests
import time

DHT_SENSOR = Adafruit_DHT.DHT11
DHT_PIN    = 4                       # GPIO4 (Pin 7)
API_KEY    = "YOUR_THINGSPEAK_WRITE_API_KEY"
URL        = "https://api.thingspeak.com/update"

while True:
    h, t = Adafruit_DHT.read(DHT_SENSOR, DHT_PIN)
    if h is not None and t is not None:
        f = (t * 9/5) + 32
        payload = {"api_key": API_KEY, "field1": t, "field2": h}
        r = requests.get(URL, params=payload, timeout=10)
        print(f"Uploaded T={t:.2f} C  H={h:.2f} %  Entry={r.text}")
    else:
        print("Sensor read failure – check wiring / pull-up")
    time.sleep(20)                   # obey 15 s ThingSpeak rule
```

> [!TIP]
> The Python `requests.get(..., params=payload)` line automatically URL-encodes the field values and constructs a valid HTTP GET request, so the student does **not** need to manually concatenate strings as in the C/C++ Arduino version.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End System Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PERC["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        A1["DHT11 SENSOR"]:::hw
        A2["NTC THERMISTOR"]:::hw
        A3["CAPACITIVE HUMIDITY CELL"]:::hw
        A1 --> A2
        A1 --> A3
    end

    subgraph PROC["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        B1["ARDUINO UNO / ESP8266 / RASPBERRY PI"]:::mcu
        B2["DHT LIBRARY DRIVER"]:::sw
        B3["WIFI STACK TCP IP"]:::sw
        B1 --> B2
        B1 --> B3
    end

    subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER"]
        C1["WIRELESS ROUTER 2.4 GHZ"]:::net
        C2["INTERNET BACKBONE"]:::net
        C1 --> C2
    end

    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER CLOUD"]
        D1["THINGSPEAK REST API"]:::cloud
        D2["TIME SERIES DATABASE"]:::cloud
        D3["CHANNEL DASHBOARD GRAPHS"]:::cloud
        D4["MATLAB ANALYTICS"]:::cloud
        D1 --> D2 --> D3
        D1 --> D4
    end

    A1 -->|Single Wire 40 bit| B1
    B3 -->|HTTP GET port 80| C1
    C2 -->|JSON TEXT| D1
    D3 -->|Live Charts| E1["END USER BROWSER / MOBILE"]:::user

    classDef hw fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A35C00,color:#000
    classDef mcu fill:#80C5FF,stroke:#003F8A,color:#000
    classDef sw fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef cloud fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef user fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#212121,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Data-Flow Sequence Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant MCU as Microcontroller
    participant SENSOR as DHT Sensor
    participant WIFI as Wi-Fi Module
    participant CLOUD as ThingSpeak Server
    participant DB as Time-Series Database
    participant UI as Web Dashboard

    Note over MCU,SENSOR: Step 1 - Initiate Reading
    MCU->>SENSOR: Pull line LOW for 18 ms
    SENSOR-->>MCU: 80 us LOW + 80 us HIGH response
    SENSOR-->>MCU: 40 bits (5 bytes) data frame
    MCU->>MCU: Verify checksum
    MCU->>MCU: Parse T and RH values

    Note over MCU,WIFI: Step 2 - Network Connection
    MCU->>WIFI: SSID + Password association
    WIFI-->>MCU: IP address assigned (DHCP)

    Note over WIFI,CLOUD: Step 3 - HTTP Upload
    MCU->>CLOUD: GET /update?api_key=K&field1=T&field2=H
    CLOUD->>CLOUD: Validate API key
    CLOUD->>DB: INSERT (timestamp, field1, field2)
    DB-->>CLOUD: entry_id = 7421
    CLOUD-->>MCU: HTTP 200 OK
    MCU->>MCU: Wait interval = 20 s

    Note over UI: Step 4 - Live Visualization
    UI->>CLOUD: GET /channels/ID/feeds.json
    CLOUD-->>UI: JSON array of recent feeds
    UI->>UI: Render line chart of T and RH
```

## 4.3 State Machine – MCU Loop Behaviour (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INIT
    INIT --> WIFI_CONNECT: Begin serial and DHT
    WIFI_CONNECT --> READ_SENSOR: IP acquired
    WIFI_CONNECT --> WIFI_CONNECT: Retry
    READ_SENSOR --> VALIDATE: Read T and H
    VALIDATE --> BUILD_URL: Checksum OK
    VALIDATE --> READ_SENSOR: NaN - retry next loop
    BUILD_URL --> HTTP_SEND: URL ready
    HTTP_SEND --> HTTP_RECV: TCP open
    HTTP_RECV --> WAIT: Response parsed
    HTTP_SEND --> WAIT: Connection failed log
    WAIT --> READ_SENSOR: 20 s elapsed
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A – Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is ThingSpeak? Mention any two applications of ThingSpeak in IoT.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
ThingSpeak is an open-source IoT analytics platform provided by MathWorks that allows users to aggregate, visualize, and analyze live data streams in the cloud. It uses RESTful and MQTT APIs to receive data from devices, stores the values in time-stamped channels, and provides MATLAB-based analytics.

*Two applications:*
1. **Remote weather monitoring** – collecting temperature, humidity, and pressure from distributed weather stations.
2. **Industrial equipment monitoring** – tracking vibration, temperature, and humidity in factories to predict maintenance needs.

*(Any two valid applications: 1 mark each. Definition: 1 mark.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List the four layers of the IoT reference architecture and identify the layer in which the DHT sensor operates.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The four layers of the IoT reference architecture are:
1. **Perception / Sensing Layer** – interface with physical sensors and actuators.
2. **Network / Transport Layer** – transmits data over Wi-Fi, Ethernet, cellular, etc.
3. **Processing / Middleware Layer** – stores, analyzes, and processes data (e.g., cloud platforms).
4. **Application Layer** – delivers services to end users (e.g., dashboards, alerts).

The **DHT11/DHT22 sensor** operates in the **Perception Layer**, as it directly senses the physical environment and converts measurements into electrical signals.

*(1 mark for listing layers, 1 mark for naming DHT layer, 1 mark for explanation.)*

---

## **Part B – Long Answer Questions (Choice-Based) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)**

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A – 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an IoT-based temperature and humidity monitoring system using Arduino, DHT11 sensor, and ThingSpeak cloud. Explain the role of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete Arduino program to read temperature and humidity values from a DHT11 sensor and upload them to a ThingSpeak channel every 20 seconds. Explain the code section that performs the HTTP GET request. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – 7 Marks:**

The system has **four major blocks**:

1. **Sensor Block (DHT11):** Contains an NTC thermistor for temperature and a capacitive humidity cell for relative humidity. Outputs a 40-bit digital frame on a single data line. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Processing Block (Arduino UNO with ESP8266):** Runs the DHT library, decodes the frame, formats the values, and constructs the HTTP GET request. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Communication Block (Wi-Fi / ESP8266):** Joins the local Wi-Fi network, obtains an IP via DHCP, and establishes a TCP socket on **port 80** to `api.thingspeak.com`. *(2 marks)*
4. **Cloud Block (ThingSpeak):** Validates the **Write API Key**, stores the values into a time-series database under **field1** and **field2**, and updates the live dashboard. *(2 marks)*

*Reference diagram: see SECTION 4.1 of these notes.*

**Part (b) – 7 Marks:**

**Complete Program** (refer to Section 3.2 above for the full code – printed below in compact form):

```cpp
#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>
#include <DHT.h>
#define DHTPIN D4
#define DHTTYPE DHT11
DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);
const char* ssid = "KTU_LAB";
const char* pwd  = "lab1234";
const char* key  = "ABCDEFG12345678";
const char* host = "api.thingspeak.com";

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  dht.begin();
  WiFi.begin(ssid, pwd);
  while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) delay(500);
}
void loop() {
  float h = dht.readHumidity();
  float t = dht.readTemperature();
  if (isnan(h) || isnan(t)) return;
  WiFiClient c;
  if (c.connect(host, 80)) {
    String url = "/update?api_key=" + String(key)
               + "&field1=" + String(t) + "&field2=" + String(h);
    c.print(String("GET ") + url + " HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: " + host +
            "\r\nConnection: close\r\n\r\n");
    while (c.connected()) { if (c.available()) c.read(); }
    c.stop();
  }
  delay(20000);
}
```

**Explanation of HTTP GET section (2 marks):**
- The `client.connect(host, 80)` opens a **TCP socket on port 80**.
- The `url` string is built using the **Write API Key** plus `field1` and `field2` query parameters.
- `client.print(...)` sends the actual HTTP request with the mandatory `Host:` header and a `Connection: close` directive that signals the server to terminate the connection after the response.

*Scoring:* Correct `#include`s (1), Wi-Fi setup (1), sensor read (1), HTTP request construction (1), HTTP transmission (1), delay logic (1), explanation (1) = **7 marks**.

---

### **Question B – 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the single-wire communication protocol used by the DHT11 sensor. With a timing diagram description, show how a single bit is transmitted. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the structure of a ThingSpeak HTTP GET request for uploading data. Also describe the response format and how the user can confirm a successful upload. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – 7 Marks:**

The DHT11 uses a **single-wire bidirectional protocol** that requires only one data line besides power and ground.

*Initialisation (1.5 marks):*
- The MCU pulls the data line **LOW for at least 18 ms** to signal the sensor to start.
- The MCU then releases the line (pull-up brings it HIGH) and waits **20–40 µs**.
- The sensor acknowledges with a **80 µs LOW + 80 µs HIGH** response pulse.

*Data Transmission (3.5 marks):*
After acknowledgement, the sensor transmits **40 bits** (5 bytes). Each bit begins with a **50 µs LOW pulse**. The duration of the subsequent HIGH pulse determines the bit value:
- A HIGH pulse of **26–28 µs** represents a logical **0**.
- A HIGH pulse of **70 µs** represents a logical **1**.

*Frame Format (2 marks):*
- Byte 1: Humidity integer
- Byte 2: Humidity decimal
- Byte 3: Temperature integer
- Byte 4: Temperature decimal
- Byte 5: Checksum = sum of first 4 bytes mod 256

A pull-up resistor of **10 kΩ** between DATA and VCC is mandatory to keep the line stable.

**Part (b) – 7 Marks:**

*HTTP GET Structure (3 marks):*

```text
GET /update?api_key=<WRITE_KEY>&field1=<TEMP>&field2=<HUM> HTTP/1.1
Host: api.thingspeak.com
Connection: close
```

- **Method:** `GET` indicates a data-retrieval request that *also carries* update data via query parameters.
- **Path:** `/update` is the ThingSpeak endpoint for writing data.
- **Query parameters:**
  - `api_key`: the **Write API Key** of the channel.
  - `field1`, `field2`: numeric values for **Channel Field 1** and **Field 2** (each channel supports up to 8 fields).
- **Headers:** `Host` identifies the server; `Connection: close` requests immediate socket teardown.

*Response Format (2 marks):*

On success the server returns:

```text
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html
...

7421
```

The body of the response is the **entry ID** of the newly stored record (e.g., `7421`).

*Success Confirmation (2 marks):*
- A **non-zero entry ID** in the response body means the data point was stored successfully.
- An entry ID of `0` means the request was **rejected (rate-limited)**, often because the **15-second minimum update interval** was not respected.
- Status code `200 OK` confirms the HTTP transaction itself succeeded; the entry ID confirms the **application-layer** acceptance.
- The user can also open the **ThingSpeak public/private channel view** in a browser to see the new data point appear in the chart.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons students lose marks in this experiment:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the 10 kΩ pull-up resistor** on the DHT DATA line → student gets `NaN` readings but does not understand why. Examiners specifically test for this.
> 2. **Uploading more frequently than 15 s** → ThingSpeak returns entry ID `0` and the chart stays empty. Students often blame the code but the cause is the rate limit.
> 3. **Hard-coding the API key** but writing it *before* Wi-Fi connects → the connection fails silently. The `WiFi.status() == WL_CONNECTED` check is mandatory.
> 4. **Using `delay(1000)` in `loop()`** instead of `millis()`-based non-blocking timing → the code becomes unresponsive.
> 5. **Confusing Write API Key with Channel ID / Read API Key** – the Write API Key alone is sufficient for uploading; the Read API Key is only for private-channel downloads.
> 6. **Omitting `Connection: close`** – leaves the socket half-open and may cause the next upload to fail.
> 7. **Forgetting to URL-encode negative temperatures** when DHT22 reads sub-zero values — the minus sign must be handled.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DHT11** gives 0–50 °C and 20–90 % RH; **DHT22** is wider and more accurate.
- The sensor uses a **single-wire protocol** transmitting **5 bytes = 40 bits** with a **checksum** byte.
- A **10 kΩ pull-up resistor** is mandatory on the DATA pin.
- The MCU sends an **18 ms LOW start signal** before each read.
- **ThingSpeak REST endpoint** is `https://api.thingspeak.com/update`.
- The HTTP GET must contain `api_key`, `field1`, `field2` (or up to `field8`).
- **Free tier** requires a minimum interval of **15 s** between updates; the experiment uses **20 s** for safety.
- A **non-zero entry ID** in the response confirms a successful upload; **`0` means rate-limited**.
- The four IoT layers are: **Perception, Network, Processing, Application**; DHT is in the **Perception Layer**.
- For **Raspberry Pi**, use the `Adafruit_DHT` Python library and the `requests` library; the `params=` keyword auto-encodes the URL.
- The Celsius→Fahrenheit conversion is $T_{F} = T_{C} \times \frac{9}{5} + 32$.
- Always verify Wi-Fi connection status with `WiFi.status() == WL_CONNECTED` before attempting HTTP.
- Use **non-blocking `millis()` timing** in Arduino, not `delay()`, for proper multi-tasking.
- ThingSpeak channels support **up to 8 fields**, **charts**, **MATLAB analytics**, and **reacts/alerts** for threshold-based notifications.
- The experiment is a **complete sense → process → communicate** IoT pipeline — the template for all smart-environment projects.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a program on Arduino/Raspberry Pi to retrieve temperature and humidity data from Thing speak cloud
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a program on Arduino/Raspberry Pi to retrieve temperature and humidity data from Thing speak cloud

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCPL408 - Microcontrollers & IoT Lab)**
> This experiment demonstrates the implementation of an **Internet of Things (IoT)** data-push pipeline in which a microcontroller (Arduino UNO/Nano/ESP8266/ESP32) or a single-board computer (Raspberry Pi 3/4/5) acquires **environmental telemetry** — specifically **ambient temperature (°C)** and **relative humidity (%RH)** — from a **DHT11 / DHT22 digital sensor** and publishes that telemetry to the **ThingSpeak™ IoT analytics cloud** over **HTTPS (TCP port 443)** using the **ThingSpeak REST `GET update` / `POST update` channel-feed API**.

In strict KTU-2024-Scheme terminology, this experiment validates the following Course Outcomes (COs):

- **CO2:** Interface analog/digital sensors with microcontrollers and develop embedded C / Python firmware.
- **CO3:** Implement an end-to-end **"Sense → Connect → Cloud → Visualize"** IoT pipeline using standard public-cloud APIs.
- **CO5:** Apply MQTT/HTTP-based data-push methodologies for remote telemetry monitoring.

## 1.2 Decomposing the Terminology (Glossary)

| Term | Engineering Meaning | Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **ThingSpeak** | An open IoT analytics platform by MathWorks that accepts time-stamped numeric data via HTTP and renders it as live graphs. | Cloud / SaaS |
| **Channel** | A virtual container on ThingSpeak that holds up to 8 numeric **fields** of data. | Cloud / SaaS |
| **Write API Key** | A 16-character alphanumeric token that authenticates a device to push data into a specific channel. | Security / Auth |
| **Read API Key** | A 16-character token that authorizes a client to **retrieve** historical data from a channel. | Security / Auth |
| **DHT11 / DHT22** | A low-cost, single-wire (proprietary protocol) digital sensor that outputs calibrated **temperature** and **humidity** values. | Embedded Sensor |
| **Relative Humidity (RH)** | The ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor to the saturation vapor pressure at a given temperature, expressed as a percentage. | Metrology |
| **REST API** | Representational State Transfer — a stateless web-service architecture using `GET`/`POST` HTTP verbs. | Networking |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Letterbox" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Intuition Pump)**
> Imagine you have a friend living in a faraway city (the **ThingSpeak Cloud**). You cannot visit them, but you have a *personalized letterbox* (the **Channel**) at their house, and you possess a unique key (the **Write API Key**) that only opens your friend's letter slot.
