Every morning, you (the **Arduino / Raspberry Pi**) measure the temperature in your city using a small digital thermometer (the **DHT sensor**), write the number on a postcard, drop the postcard into the letterbox, and the friend pins it to a notice board. Now, *anyone* in the world with the **Read API Key** can walk up to the board, read your latest postcard, and also see the historical graph of all past postcards. This entire act of "writing a postcard, sealing it, addressing it to the cloud, and dropping it into the letterbox" is what your **Arduino Sketch / Python Program** does in the experiment.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

> [!TIP]
> - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure** = **101.325 kPa** (used in saturation-vapor-pressure calculations).
> - **Absolute Zero** = **−273.15 °C** (lower bound for temperature).
> - **DHT11 Measurement Range** = **0 – 50 °C** (temperature), **20 – 90 %RH** (humidity), **±2 °C** / **±5 %RH** accuracy.
> - **DHT22 (AM2302) Range** = **−40 – 80 °C**, **0 – 100 %RH**, **±0.5 °C** / **±2–5 %RH** accuracy.
> - **HTTP Request Cycle** typical latency = **100 – 800 ms** over Wi-Fi.
> - **ThingSpeak Free-Tier Update Limit** = **15-second minimum interval** between successive `update` calls.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Experiment

The complete IoT data-acquisition-and-push pipeline consists of four sequential stages:

1. **Sense Stage** — The DHT sensor samples the air, performs internal ADC conversion, and exposes a 40-bit calibrated digital frame on its DATA pin.
2. **Process Stage** — The Arduino/Pi parses the 40-bit frame (8-bit integral RH + 8-bit decimal RH + 8-bit integral T + 8-bit decimal T + 8-bit checksum) and converts raw integers to engineering units.
3. **Connect Stage** — The MCU joins a Wi-Fi AP, resolves `api.thingspeak.com` via DNS, opens a TCP socket on **port 443 (HTTPS)** or **port 80 (HTTP)**, and assembles an `HTTP GET` request.
4. **Cloud Stage** — ThingSpeak authenticates the `Write API Key`, stores the new datapoint under the configured channel fields, and rebroadcasts the updated graph on the public/private channel page.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Each Stage

### Stage 1 — Sense: Why DHT11/DHT22?

- **Why:** It provides a *digital* (already-calibrated) output, eliminating the need for an external ADC and complex look-up-table compensation.
- **How:** Internally, the DHT uses a **capacitive polymer humidity sensor** and a **negative-temperature-coefficient (NTC) thermistor**. The on-board ASIC converts the analog signals to digital words and applies factory calibration coefficients stored in its OTP memory.

### Stage 2 — Process: Why this exact 40-bit protocol?

The 40-bit single-wire protocol guarantees *integrity* via a **parity checksum**.

$$
\text{Checksum} = \text{(Int\_RH + Dec\_RH + Int\_T + Dec\_T)} \bmod 256
$$

If the parity fails, the MCU discards the reading and retries — a robust error-handling pattern that KTU examiners expect you to mention.

### Stage 3 — Connect: Why HTTP GET instead of MQTT?

ThingSpeak historically standardized on **HTTP REST**. Although an MQTT broker is now also available, the KTU 2024 lab manual still mandates the REST path. The constructed URL is:

```
GET /update?api_key=XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX&field1=25.40&field2=60.10 HTTP/1.1
Host: api.thingspeak.com
Connection: close
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rate-Limiting Theorem for ThingSpeak**
> Let $T_{\text{update}}$ be the inter-update interval. ThingSpeak enforces:
>
> $$T_{\text{update}} \geq 15 \text{ seconds (Free Tier)}$$
>
> $$T_{\text{update}} \geq 1 \text{ second (Academic / Licensed)}$$
>
> Violating this returns `HTTP 0` and the datapoint is silently dropped.

### Stage 4 — Cloud: Why separate `field1` and `field2`?

ThingSpeak channels expose up to **8 numeric fields** per channel. The standard convention is:

| ThingSpeak Field | Typical Sensor Mapping |
|---|---|
| `field1` | Temperature in **°C** |
| `field2` | Humidity in **%RH** |
| `field3 – field8` | Reserved (e.g., pressure, light, gas) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Equations for the Lab Record / Viva**

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{actual}} = T_{\text{raw}} + \dfrac{T_{\text{dec}}}{10}$ | Temperature conversion from 16-bit DHT frame to °C | °C |
| 2 | $RH_{\text{actual}} = RH_{\text{raw}} + \dfrac{RH_{\text{dec}}}{10}$ | Humidity conversion from 16-bit DHT frame to %RH | %RH |
| 3 | $P_{\text{sat}}(T) = 6.112 \cdot e^{\frac{17.67 \cdot T}{T + 243.5}}$ | August-Roche-Magnus approximation for saturation vapor pressure (Magnus formula) | hPa |
| 4 | $RH = \dfrac{P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{sat}}(T)} \cdot 100$ | Definition of relative humidity | % |
| 5 | $T_{\text{dew}} = \frac{b \cdot \gamma(T, RH)}{a - \gamma(T, RH)}$ | Dew-point temperature (where $a=17.625$, $b=243.04$, $\gamma = \ln(RH/100) + \frac{aT}{b+T}$) | °C |
| 6 | $D_{\text{frame}} = 5 \times 8 = 40 \text{ bits}$ | DHT11/DHT22 single-wire data frame width | bits |
| 7 | $f_{\text{sample, max}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{update}}}$ | Maximum effective sampling rate, dictated by cloud rate-limit | Hz |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{pipeline}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{accepted}}}{N_{\text{sent}}}$ | End-to-end packet delivery ratio (must be 1.0 ideally) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do the first two formulas carry a division by 10?**
> The DHT22 transmits the *decimal* component as a 1-digit BCD value in the range **0–9**. To combine it with the integer byte, the MCU appends one decimal place — hence the $\div 10$ scaling. DHT11's decimal byte is always zero, so its $T_{\text{dec}}$ byte is ignored.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This "sensor → cloud" architecture underpins:
- **Smart agriculture** (precision greenhouses requiring temperature/humidity telemetry)
- **Cold-chain logistics** (pharma cold-storage compliance)
- **Smart HVAC** (Building Management Systems, BMS)
- **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)** monitoring in schools, hospitals, data centres
- **Meteorological Citizen-Science** networks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners in 2024-25 ESE viva sessions specifically ask students to map each lab experiment to **one NEP-2020-aligned Sustainable Development Goal (SDG)**. The correct mapping for this experiment is **SDG-13: Climate Action** (because the same pipeline can be scaled to micro-climate monitoring stations).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Hardware Components Required

| Qty | Component | Specification | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arduino UNO R3 (or ESP8266 NodeMCU) | ATmega328P, 16 MHz | MCU / Wi-Fi (NodeMCU only) |
| 1 | Raspberry Pi 3B+/4B | Broadcom BCM2837/2711, Linux | SBC alternative |
| 1 | DHT11 or DHT22 | Single-wire digital, 3.3–5 V | Temp+Humidity sensor |
| 1 | 10 kΩ resistor | ¼ W, ±5 % | Pull-up on DHT DATA line |
| 1 | Breadboard + jumper wires | — | Prototyping |
| 1 | USB cable | Type-B / micro-USB | Power + serial |
| 1 | Wi-Fi AP with internet | 2.4 GHz | Connectivity to ThingSpeak |
| 1 | PC with Arduino IDE / Thonny | Latest LTS | Firmware upload / scripting |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Circuit Wiring

### Step 1: Power Rails
- Connect the **5 V** pin of Arduino to the **VCC** rail of the breadboard.
- Connect any **GND** pin of Arduino to the **GND** rail.

### Step 2: DHT Data Line Pull-up
- Connect DHT **VCC** → breadboard +5 V.
- Connect DHT **GND** → breadboard GND.
- Connect DHT **DATA** → Arduino **digital pin D2**.
- Place the **10 kΩ** resistor between DHT **DATA** and **VCC** (acts as an open-drain pull-up).

### Step 3: For NodeMCU / ESP8266 (alternate, recommended)
- Use **3.3 V** (not 5 V) on VCC to protect the ESP's GPIO.
- Connect DATA → **D4 (GPIO2)**.
- The 10 kΩ pull-up is still mandatory.

### Step 4: For Raspberry Pi
- Use the **3.3 V** rail (pin 1) for VCC.
- Connect DATA → **GPIO4** (pin 7).
- The internal pull-up on GPIO4 is software-activatable, but an external 10 kΩ is recommended for long wires.

## 3.3 ThingSpeak Cloud Configuration (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **These steps must be performed *before* running the firmware.**

1. Navigate to **https://thingspeak.com** and create a free MathWorks account.
2. Click **Channels → My Channels → New Channel**.
3. Fill the form:
   - **Name:** `KTU_IoT_Lab_Exp2`
   - **Field 1:** `Temperature_C`
   - **Field 2:** `Humidity_RH`
4. Click **Save Channel**. ThingSpeak generates two keys:
   - **Write API Key** (e.g., `ABC123XYZ456DEF7`)
   - **Read API Key** (e.g., `XYZ987ABC654DEF3`)
5. Copy both keys into a notepad; you will paste them into the firmware.

## 3.4 Arduino UNO + ESP8266 Wi-Fi Module (Stacked) — Step-by-Step Program Build

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the Arduino UNO lacks built-in Wi-Fi, an **ESP-01 (ESP8266)** is attached over **SoftwareSerial** to provide TCP/IP capability.

### Hardware Wiring (UNO ↔ ESP-01)

| ESP-01 Pin | Arduino Pin | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| VCC | 3.3 V | **Never** connect to 5 V |
| GND | GND | Common ground mandatory |
| CH_PD | 3.3 V | Chip-enable, must be HIGH |
| TX | D10 (SoftwareSerial RX) | Cross-coupled |
| RX | D11 (TX) via voltage divider | **Mandatory** 1 kΩ–2 kΩ divider to drop 5 V → 3.3 V |

### Firmware (Arduino Sketch)

```cpp
/*==========================================================
 * KTU PCCPL408 - Lab Experiment 2
 * Arduino UNO + ESP8266 + DHT11 -> ThingSpeak Cloud
 *==========================================================*/
#include <SoftwareSerial.h>
#include <DHT.h>

#define DHTPIN   2
#define DHTTYPE  DHT11
#define SS_RX    10
#define SS_TX    11

DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);
SoftwareSerial esp(SS_RX, SS_TX);  // RX, TX

const char* SSID      = "YOUR_WIFI_SSID";
const char* PASSWORD  = "YOUR_WIFI_PASS";
const char* API_KEY   = "YOUR_THINGSPEAK_WRITE_KEY";
const char* SERVER    = "api.thingspeak.com";

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);
  esp.begin(115200);
  dht.begin();
  Serial.println(F("System Booting..."));
  connectWiFi();
}

void loop() {
  float humi  = dht.readHumidity();
  float tempC = dht.readTemperature();

  if (isnan(humi) || isnan(tempC)) {
    Serial.println(F("Sensor read FAILED - check wiring."));
    delay(2000);
    return;
  }

  Serial.print(F("Temp: ")); Serial.print(tempC);
  Serial.print(F(" C  |  RH: ")); Serial.print(humi);
  Serial.println(F(" %"));

  pushToThingSpeak(tempC, humi);
  delay(20000);   // > 15s ThingSpeak free-tier limit
}

void connectWiFi() {
  esp.println(F("AT+CWMODE=1"));
  delay(2000);
  esp.print(F("AT+CWJAP=\""));
  esp.print(SSID); esp.print(F("\",\""));
  esp.print(PASSWORD); esp.println(F("\""));
  delay(8000);
  if (esp.find("OK")) {
    Serial.println(F("WiFi CONNECTED."));
  } else {
    Serial.println(F("WiFi FAILED."));
  }
}

void pushToThingSpeak(float t, float h) {
  esp.println(F("AT+CIPSTART=\"TCP\",\"api.thingspeak.com\",80"));
  if (esp.find("OK")) {
    String httpReq = "GET /update?api_key=";
    httpReq += API_KEY;
    httpReq += "&field1=";   httpReq += String(t);
    httpReq += "&field2=";   httpReq += String(h);
    httpReq += " HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: api.thingspeak.com\r\nConnection: close\r\n\r\n";

    esp.print(F("AT+CIPSEND="));
    esp.println(httpReq.length());
    delay(1500);
    esp.print(httpReq);
    Serial.println(F("Data PUSHED to ThingSpeak."));
  } else {
    Serial.println(F("TCP connect FAILED."));
  }
}
```

## 3.5 Raspberry Pi Equivalent — Python Implementation

```python
"""
KTU PCCPL408 - Lab Experiment 2
Raspberry Pi + DHT11 + Adafruit_DHT -> ThingSpeak Cloud
"""
import Adafruit_DHT
import requests
import time

DHT_SENSOR = Adafruit_DHT.DHT11
DHT_PIN    = 4                    # BCM GPIO4
API_KEY    = "YOUR_THINGSPEAK_WRITE_KEY"
URL        = "https://api.thingspeak.com/update"

while True:
    humidity, temperature = Adafruit_DHT.read(DHT_SENSOR, DHT_PIN)
    if humidity is not None and temperature is not None:
        print(f"Temp={temperature:0.1f} C  RH={humidity:0.1f} %")
        payload = {"api_key": API_KEY,
                   "field1": temperature,
                   "field2": humidity}
        r = requests.get(URL, params=payload, timeout=10)
        print("ThingSpeak response:", r.status_code, r.text)
    else:
        print("Sensor read FAILED.")
    time.sleep(20)              # honour 15 s ThingSpeak limit
```

## 3.6 Analytical Verification — Magnus Formula Derivation

To cross-check the DHT11's humidity reading, derive saturation vapour pressure and compare:

$$
\ln P_{\text{sat}}(T) = \frac{17.67 \cdot T}{T + 243.5} + \ln(6.112)
$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$
P_{\text{sat}}(T) = 6.112 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.67 \cdot T}{T + 243.5}\right)
$$

The derivation is purely empirical — fitted to the Bolton (1980) dataset — and provides a **±0.4 %** error band for the range **−40 °C to +50 °C**.

For a measured dry-bulb temperature $T$ and the DHT11's reported $RH$, the absolute humidity (mixing ratio) is:

$$
w = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_{\text{sat}}(T) \cdot \frac{RH}{100}}{P - P_{\text{sat}}(T) \cdot \frac{RH}{100}}
$$

where $P$ is the local atmospheric pressure (default **1013.25 hPa** at sea level).

flowchart LR

    D3 --> D4[Dec T Byte 8 bits]
    D4 --> D5[Checksum 8 bits]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2025]**
**What is ThingSpeak? Briefly explain the role of a "Channel" and a "Write API Key" in pushing sensor data to ThingSpeak.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
ThingSpeak is a publicly accessible **Internet of Things (IoT) analytics platform** developed by MathWorks, which receives, stores, time-stamps, and visualises numeric sensor data. A **Channel** is a logical container that holds up to 8 numeric fields of telemetry, configurable by the user (e.g., field1 = Temperature, field2 = Humidity). A **Write API Key** is a 16-character alphanumeric token uniquely assigned to a channel that **authenticates** an IoT device (Arduino / Raspberry Pi) and authorises it to *write* a new datapoint to the channel via the REST `GET /update` endpoint. Without a valid key, ThingSpeak rejects the request with HTTP status code `401 Unauthorized`. **(3 marks)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2025]**
**Explain the 40-bit single-wire data frame format of the DHT11 sensor and the role of the parity checksum.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The DHT11 transmits a **40-bit (5-byte) frame** over its single DATA pin in the order:
- **Byte 1:** Integer part of Relative Humidity (8 bits)
- **Byte 2:** Decimal part of RH (8 bits, always 0 for DHT11)
- **Byte 3:** Integer part of Temperature in °C (8 bits)
- **Byte 4:** Decimal part of Temperature (8 bits, always 0 for DHT11)
- **Byte 5:** Checksum (8 bits) = (Byte1 + Byte2 + Byte3 + Byte4) mod 256

The **checksum** is a *longitudinal parity byte* that detects single-bit and burst errors caused by EMI, loose wiring, or long cable runs. If the received checksum does not equal the locally-computed sum, the MCU discards the frame and issues a fresh read. This makes the link robust without needing retransmission. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (loses 2 marks):** Hard-coding the API key in a plain-text `.ino` file *without* commenting where to obtain it. Always state in the record: *"API key obtained from Channel → API Keys tab on thingspeak.com after channel creation."*
> - **Common Mistake #2 (loses 2 marks):** Using a `delay()` shorter than **15 seconds**. ThingSpeak silently drops the update and returns an empty body, which students mistake for a network failure.
> - **Common Mistake #3 (loses 3 marks):** Failing to draw the **separate pull-up resistor** between DHT DATA and VCC. The DHT uses an open-drain output, so without the pull-up the line floats and `readHumidity()` returns `NaN`.
> - **Common Mistake #4 (loses 2 marks):** Connecting ESP-01 RX to a raw 5 V Arduino TX pin. The ESP's RX is *not* 5 V tolerant; always include a 1 kΩ + 2 kΩ voltage divider.
> - **Common Mistake #5 (loses 1 mark):** Not mentioning the CO/SDG mapping in the viva.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Arduino + ESP8266 + DHT Path**

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]**
**Design and implement an Arduino UNO-based system to read temperature and humidity from a DHT11 sensor and push the data to a ThingSpeak channel every 20 seconds. Provide the circuit diagram, list the hardware components, and write the complete Arduino sketch with proper comments.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Hardware list (2 marks):** Arduino UNO, DHT11 sensor, 10 kΩ pull-up, ESP-01 Wi-Fi module, 1 kΩ + 2 kΩ resistors for voltage divider, breadboard, USB cable, 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi AP.

2. **Circuit diagram description (2 marks):** DHT VCC → 5 V; DHT GND → GND; DHT DATA → D2 with 10 kΩ pull-up to VCC. ESP-01 VCC/CH_PD → 3.3 V; ESP-01 GND → GND; ESP-01 TX → D10; ESP-01 RX ← D11 via 1 kΩ–2 kΩ divider. Common ground mandatory.

3. **Arduino sketch (3 marks):** Use the sketch in Section 3.4 verbatim. Key code lines:
   - `#include <DHT.h>` and `#include <SoftwareSerial.h>` (1 mark)
   - `connectWiFi()` issues `AT+CWJAP` with SSID and password (1 mark)
   - `pushToThingSpeak()` issues `AT+CIPSTART="TCP","api.thingspeak.com",80` followed by `AT+CIPSEND=` and the `GET /update?api_key=…` request (1 mark)

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]**
**Explain the HTTP `GET /update` request that the ESP8266 sends to ThingSpeak. State the role of each URL parameter, the expected response codes, and describe how a mobile client can retrieve the same data using a Read API Key.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **HTTP request anatomy (3 marks):**
   ```
   GET /update?api_key=ABC123XYZ&field1=26.00&field2=55.00 HTTP/1.1
   Host: api.thingspeak.com
   Connection: close
   ```
   - `api_key=` → the **Write API Key** authenticating the channel.
   - `field1=`, `field2=` → numeric sensor values, mapped to channel fields in the order declared during channel creation.
   - The `Host:` header is mandatory per HTTP/1.1; `Connection: close` tells the server to terminate the socket after the response, freeing ESP resources.

2. **Response codes (2 marks):**
   - `200 OK` body = the new **entry ID** (e.g., `12345`) on success.
   - `400 Bad Request` → malformed URL.
   - `401 Unauthorized` → wrong/missing API key.
   - HTTP body of length 0 → rate-limit violation (less than 15 s since last update).

3. **Read-API retrieval (2 marks):** A mobile client sends `GET https://api.thingspeak.com/channels/<CHANNEL_ID>/feeds.json?api_key=<READ_API_KEY>&results=10`. ThingSpeak returns a JSON array `{"feeds":[{"field1":"26.0","field2":"55.0","created_at":"…"},…]}`. This JSON is parsed by the mobile app (Kotlin/Swift/Flutter) and rendered in a chart or gauge.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Raspberry Pi + Python Path (Internal Choice)**

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]**
**Implement a Python program on a Raspberry Pi 4 to read temperature and humidity from a DHT11 sensor connected to GPIO4 and upload the readings to ThingSpeak every 20 seconds using the `requests` library. Write the complete program with comments and explain each library function used.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Library roles (2 marks):**
   - `Adafruit_DHT` — bit-bangs the single-wire DHT protocol on BCM pin 4 and returns `(humidity, temperature)` as a Python tuple.
   - `requests` — issues HTTP/HTTPS calls; `requests.get(URL, params=payload)` serialises the `payload` dict into URL-encoded query-string parameters.
   - `time.sleep(20)` — enforces the 20-second inter-update interval to respect ThingSpeak's 15-second rate limit.

2. **Code (3 marks):** Use the script in Section 3.5. Critical lines:
   ```python
   humidity, temperature = Adafruit_DHT.read(DHT_SENSOR, DHT_PIN)
   payload = {"api_key": API_KEY, "field1": temperature, "field2": humidity}
   r = requests.get(URL, params=payload, timeout=10)
   ```

3. **Installation steps on the Pi (2 marks):**
   ```
   sudo apt-get update
   sudo apt-get install python3-pip
   sudo pip3 install requests Adafruit_DHT
   ```
   The Pi must be on the same 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi as the ThingSpeak-reachable internet, and the DHT DATA pin must be wired to **physical pin 7 (GPIO4)** with a 10 kΩ pull-up to **physical pin 1 (3.3 V)**.

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]**
**Compare the Arduino + ESP8266 approach and the Raspberry Pi approach for this experiment. Discuss at least four parameters: power, cost, ease of firmware update, real-time performance, and cloud-protocol flexibility.**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Arduino UNO + ESP-01 | Raspberry Pi 4 |
|---|---|---|
| **Power consumption (1 mark)** | ~150 mA at 5 V (≈0.75 W) | ~600–1000 mA at 5 V (≈3–5 W) |
| **Cost (1 mark)** | ~₹600 (UNO) + ₹350 (ESP-01) | ~₹4500 (Pi 4 2 GB) |
| **Firmware update (1 mark)** | USB-UART flash via Arduino IDE; takes 5–10 s | scp / git pull / restart script; takes 2–5 s |
| **Real-time performance (1 mark)** | Single-threaded loop; deterministic `loop()` ~ 50 ms per cycle | Linux preemptive; non-deterministic latency 1–10 ms jitter |
| **Cloud flexibility (1 mark)** | HTTP REST only (with AT firmware); MQTT requires custom AT or reflashed NodeMCU | HTTP, MQTT, CoAP, WebSocket — any Python lib |
| **Boot time (1 mark)** | <1 s (bare-metal) | 20–30 s (full Linux) |
| **ADC channels (1 mark)** | 6 × 10-bit on UNO; ESP8266 has 1 × 10-bit | None on-board; requires external ADS1115 |

The Arduino path is **preferred for low-power, battery-operated, deterministic** telemetry nodes. The Pi path is preferred when **on-board analytics, ML, or multi-protocol** connectivity is needed.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 ESE Module 2 — Experiment 2)**

- ☑ The DHT11 outputs a **40-bit single-wire frame**: `Int_RH | Dec_RH | Int_T | Dec_T | Checksum`.
- ☑ The **checksum** = sum of the first 4 bytes mod 256. Validate before using the data.
- ☑ **Pull-up resistor (10 kΩ)** between DHT DATA and VCC is **mandatory** — the line is open-drain.
- ☑ Use **3.3 V** for ESP-01 and Raspberry Pi GPIO; **5 V** is safe for Arduino UNO's DHT.
- ☑ ThingSpeak channels have **≤ 8 numeric fields**; map field1 = Temperature, field2 = Humidity.
- ☑ The **Write API Key** is *write-only*; the **Read API Key** is *read-only*. Never expose the Write key to public clients.
- ☑ The HTTP `GET /update` URL has the form: `…/update?api_key=K&field1=T&field2=H`.
- ☑ Free-Tier ThingSpeak enforces **≥ 15 seconds** between updates; a shorter interval causes silent drop.
- ☑ Expected success response: `HTTP 200 OK` with the new `entry_id` in the body.
- ☑ On ESP-01, the RX pin needs a **1 kΩ + 2 kΩ voltage divider** when driven by Arduino's 5 V TX.
- ☑ The Raspberry Pi can use `Adafruit_DHT` (C-extension) or `adafruit-circuitpython-dht`; the former is faster.
- ☑ HTTP `requests.get(URL, params=payload, timeout=10)` is the standard Python invocation.
- ☑ For an NEP-2020 mapping answer, cite **SDG-13: Climate Action**.
- ☑ COs validated: **CO2** (firmware), **CO3** (IoT pipeline), **CO5** (HTTP/MQTT telemetry).
- ☑ Always state the **KVL/KCL** of the divider, the **Ohm's Law** calculation of the pull-up, and the **Nyquist criterion** when justifying sampling interval.
